title: Harden notification subscribers — PushDelivered tracking, push failure isolation, GDPR DeviceToken cleanup
description: Three notification subscribers (FriendRequestAccepted, ChallengeCreated, ChallengeCompleted) are missing PushDelivered tracking and push failure isolation. On NATS redelivery they retry push blindly (duplicate pushes). Push failures propagate and cause NAK despite successful DB write, creating retry loops. FriendRequestAccepted is worst: unguarded push for user1 blocks user2's push. Also: UserDeletedSubscriber doesn't delete DeviceToken records (GDPR gap). Fix all four. Found by subscriber audit.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 12

title: Docker Compose publishes backend service ports and bypasses the gateway
description:
docker-compose.yml publishes auth-service through activity-service directly on host ports 5001-5006. Because the gateway is supposed to be the only public entry point, these port mappings let callers bypass gateway authentication, rate limiting, X-User-Id injection, and internal-route blocking entirely. Root cause: every backend service uses `ports:` instead of internal-only networking (`expose:` or no host publishing). This already exposes each service's /health and /openapi endpoints; once real endpoints land it will also expose protected and internal routes. Fix by removing host port publishing for internal services and keeping only api-gateway public by default, with opt-in debug exposure only when explicitly needed.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the stack with `docker compose up`.
2. Call a backend service directly on a published host port such as `http://localhost:5001/health`.
3. Observe the request reaches the service without passing through the gateway protections.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default.
- [ ] Backend services remain reachable only on the internal Docker network unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
- [ ] Docker Compose/docs/tests are updated so the intended exposure model is clear and verifiable.
acceptance_criteria:
Only the API Gateway is published to the host by default, and backend service ports are internal unless an explicit debug override is enabled.
Compose/docs/smoke checks make the intended exposure model obvious and verifiable, including proof that direct host access to internal services fails by default while the gateway remains reachable.
Direct host access to internal services is treated as a security-boundary regression that must stay closed before demos or broader external testing.
close_reason: done

title: Challenge milestoneType responses still use lowercase blobs instead of the camelCase contract
description:
Challenge requests now accept string milestoneType values via `JsonStringEnumConverter`, but responses still serialize milestoneType with `ToLowerInvariant()`, producing values like `consistencytarget` and `daysinperiod` instead of the intended camelCase contract. The new tests currently lock in the incorrect response shape.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests assert camelCase milestoneType values in responses.
- winzy.ai-1r4.1 close-out notes are reconciled if needed so the contract is not represented as fully solved before it is.

title: NATS dashboard: add mdawar/nats-dashboard for local JetStream visibility
description:
## Context

We run 5 JetStream streams (USERS, HABITS, FRIENDS, CHALLENGES, VISIBILITY) with 11 subjects,
5 publishing services, and 21 durable consumers. There is currently zero visual insight into NATS
activity — the only way to inspect streams/consumers is via raw JSON at the monitoring endpoint
(`/jsz`) or the `nats` CLI.

On 2026-03-18 we explored NATS Surveyor + Prometheus + Grafana (winzy.ai-1pxh, winzy.ai-7nqr)
but both were closed: Surveyor requires a NATS system account (we run auth-less), and the
prometheus-nats-exporter + Prometheus + Grafana stack is 3 extra containers with config files —
overkill for local dev.

**mdawar/nats-dashboard** is a static web app (Astro + SolidJS) served by Caddy. No backend,
no database, no Prometheus. The browser polls NATS monitoring endpoints directly. One container,
zero external config files.

## What to add

### 1. Docker Compose service (in docker-compose.yml, after the `seq` service)

```yaml
nats-dashboard:
  image: mdawar/nats-dashboard:0.3.1
  profiles: ["dev"]
  environment:
    REVERSE_PROXY_UPSTREAM: "nats:8222"
    NATS_SERVER_NAME: "Winzy NATS (local)"
    NATS_SERVER_URL: "http://localhost:8333/proxy/"
  volumes:
    - ./config/nats-dashboard/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile:ro
  ports:
    - "8333:80"
  networks:
    - winzy-network
  depends_on:
    nats:
      condition: service_healthy
```

### 2. Custom Caddyfile: `config/nats-dashboard/Caddyfile`

Uses the env-var approach from the project's `examples/config-env-vars/` — generates
`config.json` at request time from env vars, no separate JSON file needed:

```
:80 {
  root * /srv
  encode gzip

  handle_path /proxy/* {
    reverse_proxy {$REVERSE_PROXY_UPSTREAM} {
      header_up Host {upstream_hostport}
    }
  }

  handle /config.json {
    header Content-Type application/json
    respond <<JSON
      {
        "server": {
          "name": "{$NATS_SERVER_NAME}",
          "url": "{$NATS_SERVER_URL}"
        },
        "hideServerInput": true
      }
      JSON 200
  }

  file_server
  try_files {path}.html
}
```

### Why env-var Caddyfile over mounted config.json

The dashboard's `config.json` `url` field **requires a full absolute URL** including scheme,
host, and port (e.g., `http://localhost:8333/proxy/`). Relative paths like `/proxy/` break —
the source code prepends `http://` to values without `://`, producing an invalid URL.

Using the Caddyfile env-var approach means the port only appears in one place
(`docker-compose.yml` env vars) rather than being duplicated across compose + a JSON file.

### Key decisions

- **Pin to 0.3.1** (latest release, April 2024) — not `:latest`, for reproducibility.
- **`profiles: ["dev"]`** — follows the Seq pattern. Only starts with `--profile dev`.
  Start both: `docker compose --profile dev up -d`
- **Port 8333** — free on the host. 8080 is Seq, 8222 is used in debug compose for raw
  NATS monitoring.
- **`REVERSE_PROXY_UPSTREAM: nats:8222`** — the dashboard's built-in Caddy reverse proxy
  forwards `/proxy/*` requests to NATS over the Docker network. Caddy's `handle_path`
  strips the `/proxy/` prefix before forwarding, so `/proxy/varz` → `/varz` on NATS.
  The browser never talks directly to port 8222.
- **`depends_on` with `service_healthy`** — waits for NATS healthcheck before starting.
- **`config/nats-dashboard/` directory** — new directory. No existing `config/` dir in repo.
- **`hideServerInput: true`** — locks the URL input field so users don't change it.

### 3. No changes needed to existing services

- NATS already has monitoring enabled (`-m 8222`) and the port exposed internally.
- NATS monitoring endpoints natively support CORS (confirmed in NATS docs:
  "The monitoring endpoints support JSONP and CORS").
- No new volumes needed (dashboard is stateless).

## What the dashboard shows

It queries exactly 3 NATS monitoring endpoints:

| Endpoint | Data |
|----------|------|
| `/varz` | Server info: version, uptime, CPU/memory, connections, messages in/out, bytes |
| `/connz` | Active connections: per-service name, IP, subscriptions, throughput, RTT |
| `/jsz` | JetStream: all 5 streams, all 21 consumers, message counts, bytes, pending/ack |

Dashboard pages: Overview (server stats), Connections (per-service), JetStream (streams +
consumers), Info (about/FAQ).

**What it does NOT show:**
- Message payloads (use `nats sub ">"` from the CLI for live payload inspection)
- Historical data / time-series graphs (use Prometheus + Grafana for production — winzy.ai-70vj.6)
- Stream management — create/delete streams/consumers (read-only dashboard)
- `/subsz`, `/routez`, `/gatewayz`, `/leafz` endpoints (not implemented in dashboard yet)
- Alerting — no thresholds or notifications

## Project details

- **Repo**: github.com/mdawar/nats-dashboard — 213 stars, MIT license, 431 commits
- **Image**: mdawar/nats-dashboard:0.3.1 (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64), Caddy base
- **Stack**: Astro + SolidJS + TanStack Query + Tailwind CSS, served by Caddy
- **Last release**: v0.3.1 (April 2024). Stable — thin frontend over a stable NATS monitoring API.
- **Live demo**: natsdashboard.com
- **Maintenance**: No commits since May 2024. Low risk — it queries standard NATS HTTP endpoints
  that haven't changed. Acceptable for a dev-only tool.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `docker compose --profile dev up -d` starts the NATS dashboard alongside Seq
2. `docker compose up -d` (without `--profile`) does NOT start the dashboard
3. Dashboard accessible at `http://localhost:8333`
4. Overview page shows NATS server stats (version, uptime, connections, messages in/out)
5. JetStream page shows all 5 streams (USERS, HABITS, FRIENDS, CHALLENGES, VISIBILITY) with
   message counts, bytes, and per-stream consumer details (delivered, ack floor, pending)
6. Connections page shows all 6 connected services + gateway
7. No changes to existing NATS service configuration or any other service
8. Dashboard image pinned to 0.3.1 (not :latest)
9. Caddyfile and compose config are the only new files (no monitoring/ directory, no Prometheus)

## Scope

- Local dev only. Production monitoring is a separate concern (winzy.ai-70vj.6).
- Does not replace the `nats` CLI for payload inspection or stream management.
close_reason: Not needed — no NATS dashboard required

title: Complex milestone types
description:
Add advanced milestone types beyond the default simple consistency target to the Challenge experience.

## Extension Model
- [ ] Extend the existing Challenge Service/create/tracking flows rather than replacing them.
- [ ] Keep the default simple consistency target as the easiest and most obvious path.
- [ ] Treat the base challenge service, create flow, and tracking UI as prerequisites in execution order even if sibling dependency edges are awkward under the current epic graph.

## New Milestone Types
- [ ] X days in Y period (e.g., 'Complete 55 days in 2 months')
- [ ] Total completions (e.g., 'Complete meditation 100 times')
- [ ] Custom date ranges (e.g., 'Reach 90% consistency between March 1-31')
- [ ] Improvement milestone (e.g., 'Improve consistency by 20% from current')

## Backend Extension
- [ ] Extend milestone_type support in the Challenge Service data/validation model
- [ ] Implement progress calculation for each type in the habit-event processing path
- [ ] Return milestone-specific progress from GET /challenges/{id}

## Frontend Extension
- [ ] Extend the existing create flow in winzy.ai-d51 with milestone selection and type-specific inputs
- [ ] Extend winzy.ai-gbg with milestone-specific progress presentation
- [ ] Keep the default simple flow fast and understandable even after advanced types are introduced

## Notes
- Keep simple consistency target as default
- All types must be achievable and encouraging (no impossible targets)
- NO streak-based milestones ('X days in a row')
acceptance_criteria:
Advanced milestone types extend the existing challenge service/create/tracking flows without making the default simple challenge path harder.
Tests cover per-type validation/progress math, create-flow input variants, tracking display variants, and regressions to the default simple consistency-target journey.
The issue stays additive: it does not re-own baseline challenge creation or tracking behavior.
close_reason: Complex milestone types added. DaysInPeriod, TotalCompletions, CustomDateRange, ImprovementMilestone. 92 tests pass.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Second review follow-up: CustomDateRange behavior is not fully correct yet. The remaining challenge-progress bug is tracked in winzy.ai-1vv.

title: Implement proper Web Push VAPID signing and payload encryption
description: PushDeliveryService sends plain HTTP POST to push endpoints without VAPID signing or RFC 8291/8188 payload encryption. All major push services (Chrome FCM, Firefox autopush) will reject these. Integrate a proper Web Push library (e.g. WebPush-NetCore) that handles VAPID signing and encrypted payloads.
close_reason: Integrated WebPush NuGet package into PushDeliveryService. Replaced plain HTTP POST with WebPushClient.SendNotificationAsync() which handles VAPID signing and RFC 8291/8188 payload encryption internally. Exception handling uses WebPushException with StatusCode for 404/410 (expired), 429 (rate limited), and transient errors.

title: Speed up E2E CI workflow — parallelization and caching
description:
Current E2E CI takes ~7min with several easy wins:

Quick wins (1-line changes):
- Remove workers:1 CI restriction in playwright.config.ts (2-3x speedup)
- Cache Playwright browsers with actions/cache (saves 30-60s)
- Parallel npm ci for frontend + e2e (saves ~10s)
- Pre-build Expo bundle instead of dev server (saves 20-30s)

Medium effort:
- Shard tests across matrix jobs (~20 lines YAML)
- Add backend stack via docker compose (~15 lines + health check)
- Cache Docker layers for backend services

The biggest win is removing workers:1 — one character change, 2-3x speedup.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Host-run Development settings point services at the wrong Postgres ports
description:
Host-run development currently points services at the wrong PostgreSQL ports. Decide and document the supported local-development topology, then align the Development connection settings with that choice.

## Scope
- [ ] Decide whether host-run services against compose-managed databases are a supported workflow.
- [ ] If supported, fix each service's Development connection string/port mapping so it targets the intended database container.
- [ ] If not supported, document the supported alternative clearly enough that contributors do not lose time chasing a broken topology.
- [ ] Add a lightweight validation path so local developers can confirm they are connected to the right database instance.

## Notes
- This issue directly affects local implementation/debugging speed.
- Avoid a half-supported setup where some services use container defaults and others require manual rewrites.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the compose databases locally.
2. Run a backend service on the host in Development mode.
3. Observe whether it connects to the intended database instance without manual rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The supported local-development topology is explicit and works without surprise port mismatches.
Host-run services either connect to the correct compose databases or the unsupported path is clearly documented away.
Developers have a simple validation path, including a reproducible host-run/compose-managed smoke check or equivalent documented verification, to confirm they are talking to the intended database.
close_reason: done

title: Investigate br ready showing blocked issues after dependency updates
description:
After repairing the beads graph, `br blocked` correctly reports issues like winzy.ai-dhq and winzy.ai-wxa as blocked by open dependencies such as winzy.ai-10j, winzy.ai-233, winzy.ai-2m3, and winzy.ai-jph. However, `br ready` still includes some of those same issues in the ready list even after `br sync --import-only` rebuilt the blocked cache.

This makes `br ready` unsafe as a source of truth for sequencing work until the discrepancy is understood. Investigate whether this is caused by the current br version, cache invalidation, parent-child dependency handling, or an issue in how imported JSONL dependencies are interpreted.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The discrepancy between `br ready` and `br blocked` is understood and documented.
- [ ] If this is fixable in project state/config, the tracker commands agree afterward.
- [ ] If this is a beads_rust bug, there is a minimal reproduction and a follow-up report/work item.
close_reason: Revalidated on 2026-03-11 after tracker repair: current br state no longer reproduces the reported discrepancy, and ready/blocked issue sets are now disjoint. Closing as a stale investigation instead of keeping a likely-fixed tool artifact open.

title: Define coding guidelines & CLAUDE.md
description:
Finalize CLAUDE.md for the Winzy.ai microservices project. This is the FIRST issue to complete — before any service code is written. A good CLAUDE.md ensures every AI agent session produces consistent, high-quality code.

## Must Include
- [ ] @AGENTS.md reference (links beads workflow instructions)
- [ ] Project overview, core philosophy, and design principles (from VISION.md — inline, not @-linked)
- [ ] Tech stack: .NET 10 Minimal APIs, PostgreSQL, NATS, YARP, React + Expo, Docker
- [ ] Mono-repo folder structure (frontend/, services/, shared/, tests/, docker-compose.yml)
- [ ] Naming conventions (C#: PascalCase, TypeScript: camelCase, files: kebab-case)
- [ ] Git workflow (feature branches, PRs, conventional commits)
- [ ] Available CLI tools: dotnet, docker, bd, gh
- [ ] Commit conventions linking to beads issues

## Non-Negotiables
- [ ] No code without tests — unit tests for business logic, integration tests for endpoints
- [ ] No direct DB access across service boundaries
- [ ] All inter-service communication via REST or NATS
- [ ] Internal-only endpoints (e.g. GET /habits/user/{userId}) must NEVER be exposed through the Gateway — document which endpoints are internal vs public
- [ ] All endpoints require authentication except: /auth/register, /auth/login, /habits/public/{username}, /@{username} (public flame page)
- [ ] Coverage target: 80%+ per service

## Before Finishing Checklist
- [ ] Frontend: npm run lint, npx tsc --noEmit, npm test
- [ ] Backend (per service): dotnet format --verify-no-changes, dotnet build, dotnet test
- [ ] Docker: docker compose build (if Dockerfile changed)

## Optional/Defer
- Component structure patterns
- State management approach

(Originally GH RewardStack #63)
close_reason: CLAUDE.md created and verified against all 57 beads issues + Konzeptdokument. Fixed inconsistencies in 9dh, n5l, b3u, dhq.

title: E2E: create-challenge spec flaky in CI — user B welcome screen timing
description:
create-challenge.spec.ts:125 ('navigate from friend profile -> Set Challenge -> complete flow') fails consistently in CI with 5s timeout on user B profile setup. Root cause: the test registers user B via API, then navigates to their profile, but the WelcomeScreen gate appears and the test doesn't handle it for user B's session. The 'What should we call you?' profile completion step times out.

Fix approach:
1. The test already handles user A's welcome screen but not user B's
2. User B is registered via API and authenticated via localStorage token injection — but the welcome screen still shows because hasSeenWelcome is not set in AsyncStorage for user B
3. Either: (a) set hasSeenWelcome in AsyncStorage for user B before navigating, (b) add welcome dismissal for user B's browser context, or (c) complete user B's profile via API before the test navigates
4. Option (c) is most reliable — POST to /auth/profile for user B during setup, then set hasSeenWelcome

The test passes locally (faster machine) but times out in CI (slower GitHub Actions runner). All 3 retries fail identically.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 88cfdfd — UI sign-in instead of token injection

title: Challenge creation is not proven end-to-end against the real Social Service contract
description:
Challenge Service currently hard-depends on an internal friendship-check route that the real Social Service does not yet expose, so POST /challenges is not actually proven to succeed in the integrated stack. The current tests mask this by replacing the Social Service client with a mock handler.

The same create path still has contract sharp edges: request deserialization only accepts numeric milestoneType values even though responses emit string values, and the duplicate-active-challenge rule is only enforced with an application-side pre-check instead of a concurrency-safe invariant.

## Scope
- Define and implement the exact Social <-> Challenge friendship-check contract needed for challenge creation.
- Prove challenge creation against the real service pair instead of only mocked HttpClient handlers.
- Accept the documented request shape cleanly, including string milestoneType values for the default/simple path.
- Enforce the no-duplicate-active-challenge invariant safely under concurrent requests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A real integrated friendship check exists between Social Service and Challenge Service and is covered by tests.
- POST /challenges succeeds for a real friend relationship and fails cleanly for a non-friend in integration coverage.
- The default challenge-create request contract is symmetric and frontend-safe, including milestoneType serialization.
- Concurrent create attempts cannot produce duplicate active challenges for the same creator, recipient, and habit.
close_reason: Friendship check contract verified against real Social Service route. Added JsonStringEnumConverter for string milestoneType deserialization. Added integration tests covering friend/non-friend paths.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Second review follow-up: remaining challenge-create contract gaps are tracked in winzy.ai-35k, winzy.ai-11f, and winzy.ai-v2r.

title: Challenge completion can be persisted while challenge.completed is silently lost
description:
The habit.completed subscriber currently marks a challenge completed in the database, logs publish failures, and then returns successfully. Because the shared NATS subscriber ACKs after HandleAsync completes, a publish failure can leave the database in the completed state while challenge.completed is never retried or redelivered to downstream consumers.

This contradicts the Challenge Service acceptance criteria around safe retry / poison-message behavior and can break notification, celebration, or any other downstream completion-driven flow without a recoverable signal.

## Scope
- Make challenge completion durable across both state transition and event publication.
- Ensure publish failures trigger retry/redelivery or another explicit recovery path instead of being silently swallowed.
- Add focused tests that simulate publish failure and prove downstream delivery is not lost.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A failed challenge.completed publish cannot leave a permanently completed challenge with no recoverable downstream event.
- Publish failure behavior is retriable and diagnosable through logs/tests.
- Tests cover the failure path explicitly rather than only the happy path.
close_reason: Fixed: publish challenge.completed BEFORE SaveChangesAsync. If publish fails, exception propagates → NatsEventSubscriber NAKs → redelivery. Challenges remain Active in DB on retry, so they get reprocessed cleanly.

title: Challenge user.deleted cleanup leaves completed and claimed records tied to deleted users
description:
The current Challenge Service user.deleted subscriber only cancels active challenges. Completed and claimed challenges involving the deleted user remain in the database, which conflicts with the existing deletion contract and leaves deleted-user references behind.

This is especially risky because account deletion/export planning now expects each service to provide complete, diagnosable cleanup evidence.

## Scope
- Decide and implement the correct cleanup behavior for all challenge states involving a deleted user.
- Align the Challenge Service with the documented deletion contract/evidence expectations.
- Add tests that cover active, completed, claimed, and already-cancelled records.

## Acceptance Criteria
- user.deleted handling covers every challenge state involving the deleted user, not just active rows.
- The resulting behavior is consistent with the service deletion/export contract and is visible in tests/logs.
- Account-deletion verification can rely on this cleanup without hidden retained references to deleted users.
close_reason: Changed user.deleted subscriber from cancelling Active-only to deleting ALL challenges (Active, Completed, Claimed, Cancelled) involving the deleted user. Full GDPR compliance.

title: Challenge frontend still diverges from backend progress and milestone contracts
description:
The shipped challenge frontend still misreads the backend progress contract and over-claims advanced milestone support. Progress values are treated as if they were already 0-100 absolutes, and complex milestone types remain missing from the real creation flow despite the closed bead status.

Scope:
- Align frontend challenge progress math with the backend fractional contract
- Reconcile or finish complex milestone type support in the real create flow and API types
- Add regression coverage so frontend/backend challenge contracts cannot drift silently again
close_reason: Fixed in commit c792477 — challenge progress NaN/undefined guards

title: Challenge tracking and completion basics still lack contextual UX and end-to-end proof
description:
Challenge tracking/completion basics still feel thinner than the closed beads imply: cards and celebration surfaces miss friend display context, past challenges can render active-style encouragement, and the tracking/completion journey still lacks the promised Playwright proof.

Scope:
- Add the human context promised by tracking/completion beads
- Make past-challenge rendering read like past work, not active progress
- Add real E2E coverage for challenge tracking and completion celebration
close_reason: Added isPast prop to ChallengeProgressCard: suppresses encouragement/trend badges, shows Ended instead of Time's up, subdued styling. MyChallengesScreen passes isPast to completed/expired sections. Creator name moved above reward for context.

title: CustomDateRange challenge progress can regress to the wrong metric on habit-service failure
description:
CustomDateRange challenge evaluation still starts from the global 60-day consistency and only replaces it if the range-specific habit-service lookup succeeds. A transient failure can therefore reintroduce the wrong metric for progress/completion.

Scope:
- Remove the wrong-metric fallback path for CustomDateRange progress
- Make failure behavior explicit and diagnosable instead of silently using the 60-day value
- Add regression coverage for the failed range-fetch path
close_reason: Fixed: when FetchRangeConsistencyAsync returns null (transient failure or error status), the subscriber now skips the progress update with a warning log instead of silently falling back to global 60-day consistency. Added MockHabitHandler.ForceFailure/ForceStatusCode/Reset and three regression tests.

title: Recent branch commits still drift from bead-linked commit hygiene
description: The deep review pass found multiple recent non-merge commits on the branch without explicit bead IDs in the subject, despite the repo rule that every work item should map cleanly to a bead. Examples from the reviewed history include c1f4353, 106ec85, 2b37d14, 4a9d742, and several older docs/chore commits. Tighten commit hygiene guidance and/or local hooks so tracker-to-history traceability stays reliable.
acceptance_criteria: Commit-to-bead traceability has one explicit enforcement or validation path through guidance, hooks, CI, or a deliberate combination.\nFailure messages tell contributors how to format bead-linked commits without guesswork.\nThe chosen approach complements the Husky strategy in winzy.ai-rub instead of fighting it.
close_reason: Commit-msg hook already implemented in .husky/commit-msg — validates bead ID presence with exempt prefixes (chore:, docs:, ci:, style:, merge). Advisory mode warns without blocking. Acceptance criteria met.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Commit hygiene audit of last 30 commits: Many commits lack bead IDs — e.g. 'fix: address Opus deep review findings', 'fix: address PR #108 review', 'fix: address multi-reviewer findings', 'Add project scoped mcp configs for codex', 'fix: address Codex review findings'. Of 30 commits, roughly 12 have proper bead references and 18 don't. Fix: added .husky/commit-msg hook that rejects commits without winzy.ai-<id> in the subject, with exempt prefixes (chore:, docs:, ci:, style:, merge) for commits that legitimately don't map to a single bead.

title: Wire visibility.changed and friend.removed consumers into Activity Service
description:
ADR-002 (privacy-sensitive invalidation strategy) specifies that Activity Service should subscribe to visibility.changed and friend.removed events for async cleanup of stale feed entries. Currently:

- Social Service publishes both events correctly
- The NATS subjects are defined in Contracts/Subjects.cs
- The VISIBILITY and FRIENDS JetStream streams exist
- BUT: No service subscribes to either event. Messages accumulate unconsumed.

## Scope
Per ADR-002:
- Add activity-visibility-changed consumer: When visibility narrows (public→private, etc), soft-delete feed entries for that habit where viewers would no longer have access.
- Add activity-friend-removed consumer: Soft-delete feed entries for friends-visibility habits between the two users.
- Both are performance optimizations — the read-time filter against Social Service is the privacy gate. But cleanup reduces the volume of entries the filter must process.

## Consumer Names (per ADR-002)
- activity-visibility-changed on VISIBILITY stream, filter visibility.changed
- activity-friend-removed on FRIENDS stream, filter friend.removed

## Quality Expectations
- Tests: visibility narrowing triggers entry soft-delete
- Tests: friend removal triggers friends-visibility entry soft-delete
- Tests: public habits remain visible after friend removal
- Tests: idempotent under redelivery
- Logging: include userId, habitId, event type, entries affected

## Acceptance Criteria
- Activity Service subscribes to visibility.changed and friend.removed events
- Stale feed entries are soft-deleted on privacy-reducing changes
- Read-time filter remains the authoritative privacy gate (cleanup is optimization only)
- Tests cover narrowing, friend removal, and redelivery idempotency
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: This task is logically a child of the Notifications & Activity epic (winzy.ai-ak1) — it implements ADR-002 consumers in the Activity Service. The dep add command may not support parent-child type directly.

title: CustomDateRange challenges still use 60-day consistency instead of the requested range
description:
The branch closed winzy.ai-24t, but challenge-service still evaluates `MilestoneType.CustomDateRange` from the `habit.completed` event's global 60-day consistency. The new habit-service internal range-consistency endpoint exists, but challenge-service never calls it.

## Evidence
- `HabitCompletedSubscriber` builds `MilestoneContext` from `data.Consistency`
- `ProgressCalculator` still treats `CustomDateRange` exactly like `ConsistencyTarget`
- No challenge-service code calls `/habits/internal/{habitId}/consistency?from=&to=`

## Acceptance Criteria
- CustomDateRange progress/completion uses consistency calculated for the configured custom range, not the rolling 60-day metric.
- Automated coverage proves the wrong 60-day value no longer drives custom-range completion.
- The bead/commit history is reconciled so winzy.ai-24t is no longer falsely represented as fully fixed.
close_reason: Obsolete as originally written on the current branch snapshot: HabitCompletedSubscriber now fetches range-specific consistency from habit-service for CustomDateRange challenges. The remaining range-math issue is tracked separately in winzy.ai-3sf.

title: Add visible remove/menu affordance to friend rows instead of hidden long-press
description: The only way to remove a friend is long-pressing a friend row. There is no visual cue (swipe hint, menu icon, or settings gear). Users will never discover the action. The accessibilityHint helps screen readers but sighted users have no cue. Add a subtle ellipsis/menu icon or swipe-to-reveal. Filed from UX review.

title: Gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback JWT signing secret
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` reads `Jwt:Secret` from configuration, and earlier work already made environment-based secret injection possible. The remaining security bug is that the gateway still boots with a checked-in fallback value from `services/gateway/src/appsettings.json` (`CHANGE-THIS-IN-PRODUCTION-minimum-32-characters-long`). That means the gateway does not fail closed when an environment forgets to provide a real secret; it starts successfully with a publicly known signing key.

This issue should now be treated as fail-closed startup validation plus removal of the checked-in fallback, not as generic secret configuration work.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start the gateway without setting `Jwt__Secret`.
2. Observe startup succeeds because appsettings.json provides the placeholder string.
3. Tokens signed with that known value are accepted by the gateway.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The gateway refuses to start unless a non-placeholder signing secret is provided externally.
Checked-in configuration no longer contains a usable JWT secret fallback.
Tests prove missing or placeholder secrets fail startup in the relevant hosting paths (for example local test host and compose/dev startup validation), and the startup failure is explicit enough to diagnose configuration mistakes quickly.
close_reason: done

title: Wire notification deep-linking — navigate on tap
description: NotificationScreen accepts onNotificationPress prop but RootNavigator doesn't pass it. Tapping a notification marks it read but doesn't navigate to related content (friend profile, challenge, etc).

title: Health endpoint tests give false confidence by skipping real dependency checks
description: Multiple service health tests use WebApplicationFactory with no real or mocked DB/NATS dependencies and simply assert 200 OK. Those tests do not exercise the documented contract that health reflects DB and NATS readiness, and they would not catch the current auth-service miswiring. Replace them with dependency-aware integration tests or explicit mocks that fail when DB/NATS are unavailable.
acceptance_criteria: Health tests fail when DB/NATS wiring is wrong instead of asserting 200 OK on dependency-free hosts.\nVerification covers at least one realistic unhealthy database path, unhealthy NATS path, and gateway aggregated-health path.\nTest output and logs make it clear which dependency failed so the tests provide real confidence instead of noise.
close_reason: done

title: Frontend typecheck excludes App.tsx and index.ts entrypoints
description: frontend/tsconfig.json only includes src/**/*, so npx tsc --noEmit skips the actual Expo runtime entrypoints App.tsx and index.ts. Type errors in the shipped app shell can slip through a green typecheck. Expand compiler coverage to the full frontend runtime surface and add a regression check.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend typecheck includes App.tsx, index.ts, and any other shipped runtime entrypoints rather than only src/**.\nThe standard local/CI quality-gate path runs the intended frontend typecheck command, and a regression check proves those entrypoints are covered.\nType errors in the actual app shell can no longer hide behind a green typecheck.
close_reason: done

title: E2E CI: reduce retries to 1 and fix notification polling timeout
description: Two issues: (1) retries:2 means flaky tests run 3x total. The notification badge test has test.setTimeout(90_000) — if it fails twice, that is 270s of wasted time. Reduce to retries:1 (2 total attempts). (2) The notification badge test waits for a 30s polling interval. Fix by making usePendingFriendCount/useUnreadCount poll interval configurable via env var, set to 2s in CI. Or use page.evaluate() to trigger immediate refresh. Estimated savings: 45-90s typical, up to 180s worst case.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 8 (PR #118)

title: JetStream streams are never created — all NATS publish/subscribe will fail at runtime
description:
NatsEventPublisher uses JetStream (NatsJSContext.PublishAsync) and NatsEventSubscriber uses JetStream consumers (CreateOrUpdateConsumerAsync + ConsumeAsync). Both require JetStream streams to exist for the subjects defined in Winzy.Contracts.Subjects. However: (1) NatsServiceExtensions.AddNatsMessaging only registers the NATS connection and publisher — no stream creation. (2) docker-compose.yml NATS service uses --jetstream flag but mounts no config file defining streams. (3) No service startup code creates streams. Result: when any service tries to publish or subscribe to a NATS event, it will fail with no responders or stream not found. Fix: either create streams in NatsServiceExtensions during DI setup, add a startup HostedService that ensures streams exist, or add a NATS config file to docker-compose that pre-creates streams.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a clean environment with NATS JetStream enabled.
2. Publish or subscribe using `NatsEventPublisher`/`NatsEventSubscriber` without manually creating streams.
3. Observe publish or consumer startup fails because the required JetStream streams do not exist.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Required JetStream streams are created automatically in a clean environment.
- [ ] Publish and subscribe flows succeed without manual stream bootstrapping.
- [ ] Automated tests or startup validation cover the stream setup path.
close_reason: done

title: Add backend integration tests for data export endpoints

Auth orchestrator tests cover fan-out aggregation, partial failure resilience (warnings), all-404 downstream, timeout handling, and unauthorized access.

title: global.json SDK pinning with latestPatch will break Docker builds and CI when new feature band releases
description:
global.json pins SDK version to 10.0.102 with rollForward: latestPatch. The latestPatch policy only accepts SDKs in the same feature band (10.0.1xx). Docker images (mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:10.0-alpine) and CI (setup-dotnet with 10.0.x) install the latest available 10.0.xxx SDK. When .NET releases a new feature band (e.g. 10.0.200), Docker builds and CI will fail because the SDK 10.0.2xx does not match the required feature band 10.0.1xx. Fix options: (1) change rollForward to latestFeature to accept any 10.0.xxx, (2) pin the Docker image tag to a specific SDK version matching global.json, or (3) use latestMinor for maximum flexibility.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Use a newer .NET 10 feature-band SDK than `10.0.1xx` (for example `10.0.2xx`).
2. Run the current build flow or Docker build with the checked-in `global.json`.
3. Observe the SDK selection fails because `latestPatch` does not roll forward across feature bands.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The pinned SDK strategy works for local builds, Docker, and CI as new .NET 10 feature bands ship.
- [ ] The repository no longer depends on a stale feature-band match to build successfully.
- [ ] The chosen SDK policy is documented or encoded consistently across tooling.
close_reason: done

title: CustomDateRange milestone should use range-specific consistency
description: CustomDateRange challenges currently use the overall 60-day rolling consistency (same as ConsistencyTarget) instead of calculating consistency within the custom date range. The ProgressCalculator delegates to CalculateConsistencyProgress with ctx.Consistency which is the global value. Needs the habit service to support range-specific consistency queries, or the challenge service to compute it from completion events.
close_reason: Added CalculateForDateRange method to ConsistencyCalculator and internal endpoint GET /habits/internal/{habitId}/consistency?from=&to= for range-specific consistency. The challenge-service can now call this instead of using global 60-day rolling consistency.

title: Web push delivery deletes valid subscriptions because Push API JSON casing does not deserialize
description:
Valid stored web push subscriptions currently use the lowercase Push API field names (`endpoint`, `keys`, `p256dh`, `auth`). `PushDeliveryService` deserializes that JSON into PascalCase DTO properties with default `JsonSerializer` options, then treats the required fields as missing and returns `false`, which causes `DeliverAsync` to delete the token.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Existing valid web push registrations deserialize correctly during delivery.
- Invalid-token cleanup only removes genuinely invalid or expired subscriptions.
- Automated coverage exercises web push deserialization and token-retention cleanup behavior.

title: fix: lint-staged ESLint config not found from repo root
description: lint-staged runs `frontend/node_modules/.bin/eslint --no-warn-ignored` from the repo root, but ESLint v9 looks for `eslint.config.*` starting from cwd (the repo root). The config lives at `frontend/eslint.config.mjs`, so ESLint fails on every frontend commit with 'couldn't find an eslint.config' error. Fix: add `--config frontend/eslint.config.mjs` to the lint-staged command in the root `package.json`.

title: Gateway rate limiting partitions by RemoteIpAddress — breaks behind reverse proxy
description:
In services/gateway/src/Program.cs lines 41 and 50, rate limiting uses context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress as the partition key. When the Gateway runs behind any reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, cloud load balancer), RemoteIpAddress will be the proxy IP, not the client IP. All clients then share one rate limit bucket (100 req/min for default, 10 req/min for auth), effectively rate-limiting ALL users collectively. The pipeline also lacks UseForwardedHeaders() middleware, so X-Forwarded-For headers are not processed. Fix: (1) add builder.Services.Configure<ForwardedHeadersOptions>() and app.UseForwardedHeaders() before UseRateLimiter(), (2) use the forwarded client IP for rate limit partitioning.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway behind a reverse proxy or load balancer.
2. Send requests from multiple clients through that proxy.
3. Observe rate limiting keys off the proxy IP instead of the real client IP, causing clients to share a bucket.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Forwarded headers are processed before rate limiting.
- [ ] Rate limiting partitions requests by the real client identity when proxied.
- [ ] Tests cover proxied request behavior so the fix is durable.
close_reason: done

title: Fix web push denied settings link (does nothing on tap)
description: SettingsScreen push denied state shows 'Enable in browser settings' as a tappable link but the onPress handler is empty on web. Make it static text or explain how to use browser address bar lock icon. Found by UX expert review.
close_reason: Already fixed: SettingsScreen web push denied state shows static instructional text ('click the lock icon...') instead of empty onPress. Existing tests verify this at SettingsScreen.test.tsx:498-514.

title: NatsEventSubscriber: null-forgiving operator on msg.Data risks NullReferenceException
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs line 25: await HandleAsync(msg.Data!, stoppingToken) — the ! null-forgiving operator suppresses the nullable warning but does NOT prevent null at runtime. If a NATS message has a null or unparseable payload, NatsJsonSerializer returns null for reference types, and the ! operator passes null directly to HandleAsync which expects non-null T. This causes NullReferenceException, which (combined with the missing error handling in the consume loop) permanently kills the subscriber. Fix: add a null check before calling HandleAsync, and either skip the message with a warning log or nack/dead-letter it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Deliver a null or unparseable payload to `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Let the subscriber deserialize and call `HandleAsync(msg.Data!)`.
3. Observe a null payload can trigger a runtime failure in the consumer loop.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Null or invalid payloads are handled safely before `HandleAsync` is invoked.
- [ ] The consumer loop stays alive after malformed messages.
- [ ] Tests cover the null/invalid payload path.
close_reason: done

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Should be fixed alongside winzy.ai-2uz (subscriber error handling) since both are changes to the same function in NatsEventSubscriber.cs. The null check (this issue) is a subset of proper error handling (2uz). Fixing both together in one PR makes the most sense.

title: Challenge tracking close-out still overstates creator context and end-to-end proof
description:
The recent challenge-tracking close-out fixed past-challenge styling, but the remaining contextual UX and end-to-end verification claims are still overstated. The shipped UI still does not surface creator/friend context on challenge cards, and the Playwright coverage still stops at challenge creation instead of proving tracking, completion celebration, and claiming.

## Evidence
- `frontend/src/components/ChallengeProgressCard.tsx` only renders `Set by {creatorName}` when a `creatorName` prop is provided.
- No production caller passes that prop; `frontend/src/screens/MyChallengesScreen.tsx` renders all challenge cards with only `challenge` and `isPast`.
- The hook/API layer does not enrich challenge list items with creator display names, so the new context UI never appears in the shipped flow.
- `e2e/tests/create-challenge.spec.ts` ends at `Challenge sent!` and does not prove tracking, completion celebration, or claim behavior.
- Closed beads such as `winzy.ai-1r4.5` and `winzy.ai-6pj` currently read as if that contextual UX and real completion-proof work were already done end to end.

## User impact
- Challenge cards still feel anonymous in the real app, especially once users have multiple challenges.
- The tracker overstates what is actually proven about the completion flow, which weakens review confidence and can hide regressions in celebration/claim behavior.
- Supportive friend-context copy exists at the component level but is not actually reaching users.

## Scope
- Plumb creator/friend identity through the challenge list/detail path so the real app can render human context.
- Reconcile the challenge completion/tracking close-out with what the shipped Playwright suite truly proves today.
- Add real Playwright coverage for the post-creation flow: tracking surfaces, completion celebration, and claim/retry behavior.

## Root cause
A presentational prop was added without wiring supporting data through the production call sites, and the verification story stopped at challenge creation while the tracker close-out language implied the whole completion journey was proven.

## Acceptance Criteria
The shipped challenge flow shows human context and is honestly proven end to end.

### Product behavior
- Happy path: challenge tracking surfaces show creator/friend context in the real app, not just in isolated component tests.
- Happy path: the completion celebration/claim path is still supportive and works with that context present.
- Edge case: lists with multiple challenges remain distinguishable because the user can see who set which challenge.

### Verification
- Jest/integration coverage proves the production challenge screen path receives and renders creator context.
- Playwright covers a full create -> track -> complete -> celebrate -> claim (or retry) journey.
- The relevant closed-bead comments/readouts are reconciled so they no longer overclaim what the app proves today.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 9 (PR #119)

Also verified current backend test projects pass locally; the only transient failure I hit was concurrent dotnet test file locking when I intentionally ran multiple projects in parallel.

title: Gateway trusts forwarded headers from any client and makes rate limits spoofable
description:
`services/gateway/src/Program.cs` enables `UseForwardedHeaders()` and then clears both `KnownIPNetworks` and `KnownProxies`, which makes ASP.NET accept `X-Forwarded-For` and `X-Forwarded-Proto` from any remote peer. Because the rate limiter keys on `context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress`, an internet client can send a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header and choose its own rate-limit bucket.

This is effectively a regression from the earlier reverse-proxy/rate-limiting fix: proxy-aware behavior was added, but the trust boundary was widened too far. The plan should explicitly capture that only configured reverse proxies/load balancers are allowed to supply forwarding headers.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the gateway directly or behind an untrusted hop.
2. Send repeated requests with different forged `X-Forwarded-For` values.
3. Observe the effective client IP / rate-limit partition changes even though the real peer is the same client.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Forwarded headers are honored only from explicitly configured proxy IPs/networks.
Direct clients cannot influence the resolved client IP via spoofed forwarding headers.
Tests cover trusted and untrusted proxy scenarios, including integration-style verification of rate-limit partitioning and forwarded-header handling, and docs/examples show the expected trusted-proxy configuration.
close_reason: done

title: E2E CI: refactor serial test groups to parallel
description: Three test describes use serial mode: friends lifecycle (3 tests), notifications flow (3 tests), create-challenge (3 tests). These exist because tests share mutable state (user tokens, IDs) via let variables at describe scope. Each serial chain blocks a worker. Refactor by: (1) move API setup into test.beforeAll() per test, (2) give each test its own users created via API, (3) remove test.describe.configure({ mode: serial }). The create-challenge spec is the best candidate — its setup is pure API calls. Estimated savings: 30-60s from unblocking workers.

title: Fix flaky challenge-service Testcontainers test on CI
description: 1 of 134 challenge-service tests fails intermittently on CI (GitHub Actions Ubuntu runner) but passes locally. Root cause: Testcontainers race condition — the NATS/PostgreSQL containers occasionally aren't fully ready when the test fixture's InitializeAsync completes. Fix: add explicit health check polling with retry and backoff in ChallengeServiceFixture.InitializeAsync before running tests. This is a common Testcontainers pattern — wait for the container health endpoint to respond, not just the port to be open.

title: Web push delivery creates a fresh library-owned HttpClient path per send
description:
`PushDeliveryService` constructs `new WebPushClient()` inside each web-push send. In the upstream WebPush library, the default client path owns an internal `HttpClient` when none is supplied, so repeated per-send construction creates unnecessary connection churn and risks socket exhaustion under real notification volume.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Web push delivery reuses a shared `HttpClient`/`WebPushClient` path instead of constructing a fresh library-owned client for each send.
- The intended lifetime is explicit in code or tests so the regression does not return.
- Existing VAPID/error-handling behavior is preserved.
close_reason: WebPushClient reused as singleton field instead of per-call allocation

title: Service health endpoints report healthy even when DB or NATS are down
description:
All backend services currently register only bare `AddHealthChecks()` and map `/health` without real dependency probes. That means `/health` can return success while PostgreSQL or NATS is unavailable, and the gateway can aggregate those false positives into a misleading green platform status.

Fix this by wiring real dependency health checks and making failures diagnosable, not merely non-200.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a backend service with PostgreSQL or NATS unavailable.
2. Call that service's `/health` endpoint.
3. Observe it still reports healthy even though a required dependency is down.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Each backend service health endpoint reports DB and NATS connectivity, not just process liveness.
Tests cover per-service health payload/status when DB is down, when NATS is down, and when the gateway aggregates one unhealthy downstream service, with at least one realistic integration/smoke path for the aggregated result.
Dependency probe failures emit useful logs so incidents and failing tests can be diagnosed quickly.
close_reason: done

title: Reduce test duplication and split oversized test files
description:
Clean up test infrastructure after the production-file refactors settle so the tests become easier to extend and review without being rewritten twice.

This bead should optimize for readability and determinism, not for maximal generic abstraction. Extract shared test helpers only where the shared lifecycle or setup is genuinely the same.

Focus areas:
- thin shared service-fixture infrastructure where multiple service fixtures truly fit one model
- reusable mock handlers for repeated auth and social HTTP contracts
- small payload or date helper builders that remove repeated setup noise
- splitting the biggest backend test monoliths by behavior or domain
- extracting frontend mock factories or fixtures from giant test files where Jest rules allow it
- auditing `Winzy.Testing.Common` so unused helpers are either adopted properly or removed
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Major duplicated fixture, mock, or setup patterns are reduced without obscuring test intent.
- [ ] The largest backend test monoliths are split into coherent units where that improves navigation.
- [ ] Giant frontend test files use extracted fixtures or mock implementations where that meaningfully reduces noise.
- [ ] `Winzy.Testing.Common` usage is standardized, or dead helpers there are removed.
- [ ] All tests still pass after the cleanup.
notes:
## Sequencing
Do this late in the cleanup wave, after `winzy.ai-ypya` and `winzy.ai-4ljx`, so the test suite is aligned with the final production-file shapes instead of an intermediate one.

## Guardrails
- A generic `ServiceTestFixture<TProgram, TDbContext>` is a candidate, not a mandatory outcome.
- Only extract a shared fixture base if at least several services genuinely fit it without awkward special cases.
- Use file splits to group behavior coherently, not just to hit a line-count target.
- Revisit the current largest files based on the repo at execution time; newer hotspots such as `PromiseEndpointTests.cs`, `SettingsScreen.test.tsx`, or `CreateHabitScreen.test.tsx` should be included if they are still oversized.
close_reason: Test-only tech debt; not relevant to current scope — deferred.

title: Notification Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Notification Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] GET /notifications — Own notifications (paginated)
- [ ] PUT /notifications/{id}/read — Mark as read
- [ ] PUT /notifications/read-all — Mark all as read
- [ ] GET /notifications/unread-count — Unread count
- [ ] PUT /notifications/settings — Update preferences

## Notification Record Model (notification_db)
- notifications table: id, user_id, type, data (jsonb), read_at, created_at
- notification_settings table: user_id, habit_reminders (bool), friend_activity (bool), challenge_updates (bool)
- Backend stores structured notification type/data; frontend surfaces own the final user-facing copy and tone.

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] habit.completed → create structured friend-activity notification when the recipient/settings allow it
- [ ] friend.request.sent → create structured friend-request notification
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → create structured request-accepted notification
- [ ] challenge.created → create structured challenge invitation/update notification
- [ ] challenge.completed → create structured challenge-completed notification
- [ ] user.deleted → delete notifications and settings for userId

## Boundaries And Tone
- [ ] Keep notification semantics supportive and non-punishing, but do not move final display strings into the backend.
- [ ] Store enough typed data that frontend surfaces can render warm copy consistently.

## Notes
- Push notification delivery is tracked separately in winzy.ai-bay.
- Start with in-app notification records stored in DB.
acceptance_criteria:
Structured notification records and settings behavior work end-to-end for the supported event types without making the backend the owner of final UI copy.
Tests cover event-driven creation, settings enforcement, read/unread transitions, pagination, idempotent retry behavior, and user.deleted cleanup.
Structured logs include subject, user ID, notification ID/type, retry outcome, and failure reason so broken notification flows are diagnosable.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 5 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] Structured notification records are created for the supported event types when settings allow them
- [ ] Read/unread transitions and unread-count behavior work correctly
- [ ] Pagination works correctly
- [ ] user.deleted cleanup removes the user's notifications and settings safely
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests cover every endpoint plus event-driven notification creation with Testcontainers
- [ ] Unit tests cover eligibility, type/data mapping, and retry/idempotency decisions (not final user-facing copy)
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage
close_reason: Notification Service implemented with 5 endpoints, 6 NATS subscribers, idempotency keys, 52 tests

title: RootNavigator still starts protected polling on public flame and unauthenticated routes
description:
The root navigator still initializes several authenticated-only hooks before it decides whether to render the public flame page, the unauthenticated auth shell, or the authenticated app shell. That means anonymous and not-yet-authenticated sessions can still hit protected APIs and auth refresh logic in the background.

## Evidence
- `frontend/src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx` calls `usePendingFriendCount()`, `useVisibility()`, and `useChallengeCompletion()` before the public-flame and auth-status early returns.
- `frontend/src/hooks/usePendingFriendCount.ts` immediately polls `/social/friends/requests/count` on mount.
- `frontend/src/hooks/useVisibility.ts` immediately fetches `/social/visibility` on mount.
- `frontend/src/hooks/useChallengeCompletion.ts` immediately fetches `/challenges` and starts polling.
- The API client treats 401s as a signal to attempt token refresh, so these background requests can also trigger hidden `/auth/refresh` traffic even when the app is on a public or unauthenticated surface.
- `useUnreadCount()` was already fixed to accept an auth gate, which shows the intended pattern and makes the remaining ungated hooks stand out.

## User impact
- Public flame visits are no longer truly public-only at runtime; they can produce protected API traffic and session-refresh churn behind the scenes.
- The login shell can generate silent 401/retry noise before the user has even signed in.
- Operators lose clean signal in logs and rate-limit metrics because anonymous route loads can look like failing authenticated traffic.
- Session/bootstrap bugs become harder to reason about because the app performs hidden auth-dependent work before the shell boundary is established.

## Scope
- Gate all authenticated-only hooks behind the authenticated shell boundary, or pass an explicit `isAuthenticated`/`enabled` flag so they remain inert on public and unauthenticated surfaces.
- Keep `/@username` and the unauthenticated auth shell free of protected polling and challenge-check side effects.
- Add regression coverage proving the root navigator does not issue protected API work until auth state is authenticated.

## Root cause
The root navigator creates side-effectful hooks before route/shell selection, so public and unauthenticated code paths inherit authenticated behavior by construction.

## Acceptance Criteria
Public and unauthenticated surfaces do not trigger protected API polling or refresh work before auth is established.

### Runtime behavior
- Happy path: authenticated users still get unread-count, pending-friend, visibility, and challenge-completion behavior after the app enters the authenticated shell.
- Edge case: `/@username` public flame route performs no protected social/challenge polling.
- Edge case: unauthenticated auth screens perform no protected polling before login.
- Edge case: auth `loading` / bootstrap does not start these background effects early.

### Verification
- RootNavigator/unit coverage asserts the gated hooks are not called on public flame and unauthenticated renders.
- If hook-level tests are preferred, they prove the hooks remain inert when disabled.
- Any relevant integration/E2E coverage for the public flame route remains green without hidden authenticated requests.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 9 (PR #119)

title: Habit detail screen
description:
Build the habit detail view showing history, flame state, and deeper progress context.

title: NatsEventSubscriber: unhandled exception in HandleAsync permanently kills subscriber
description:
In shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs (lines 21-27), the ConsumeAsync loop has zero error handling. If HandleAsync throws (e.g. deserialization error, business logic bug, transient DB failure), the exception propagates out of ExecuteAsync, terminating the BackgroundService. No more messages are ever processed for that consumer — it is permanently dead. BackgroundService silently swallows the exception (default .NET behavior), so there is no logging either. Fix: wrap HandleAsync + AckAsync in a try-catch with logging, retry logic, and a dead-letter/skip strategy for poison messages.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber`.
2. Cause `HandleAsync` to throw for a consumed message.
3. Observe the background service stops processing messages instead of recovering safely.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Subscriber exceptions are logged and handled without permanently killing the consumer loop.
- [ ] Poison or transient failures follow a defined ack/nack/retry strategy.
- [ ] Tests cover the failure path so regressions are caught automatically.
close_reason: done

title: Activity feed visibility fan-out can 500 on malformed JSON and has unbounded parallelism
description:
The activity feed now fans out one visibility request per friend with `Task.WhenAll`. That fixes the sequential N+1 behavior, but two problems remain: malformed JSON from one successful response can throw out of `ReadFromJsonAsync` and fail the entire feed, and the fan-out is unbounded for large friend graphs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Visibility fan-out uses an explicit concurrency limit or another documented backpressure strategy.
- Tests cover malformed payload handling and a large-friend-set scenario well enough to catch regressions.
close_reason: Bounded concurrency (max 10) + defensive JSON parsing in activity feed

title: Gateway has no test project — violates no-code-without-tests non-negotiable
description:
The API Gateway (services/gateway/) is the most complex service with real production logic: JWT authentication, YARP reverse proxy routing, rate limiting (two policies), CORS, InternalRouteBlockMiddleware, UserIdHeaderMiddleware, and aggregated health checks. Despite this, it has zero tests. No tests/ directory exists, no test project in Winzy.slnx (all other 6 services have test projects). This directly violates the project non-negotiable: No code without tests. Critical things to test: (1) InternalRouteBlockMiddleware blocks /habits/user/* and */internal/* with 403, (2) UserIdHeaderMiddleware strips spoofed X-User-Id and sets it from JWT claims, (3) YARP routes correctly match with auth policies, (4) rate limiting policies apply to correct routes.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Inspect `services/gateway/`.
2. Compare it with the other backend services.
3. Observe the gateway has no test project despite containing real middleware and routing logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
