title: Public flame basics still lack degraded-state truth and privacy-safe verification
description:
The public flame surface is implemented but still under-truthful and under-verified: degraded upstream reads can look like not-found or no-public-habits, visibility narrowing is not proven end-to-end, and key privacy-sensitive branches remain weakly covered.

Scope:
- Distinguish degraded public-flame reads from genuine not-found and empty states
- Add real visibility-narrowing and privacy/freshness verification for the public page
- Tighten share-surface contracts/tests where default-public exclusions and cross-surface parity matter
close_reason: Fixed degraded-state error differentiation (auth service 500 -> 503 instead of 404), added degraded flag to social-service failure path, added excludedHabitIds support to MockSocialHandler, added 13 new tests covering auth/social degradation, end-to-end visibility narrowing, and cross-surface parity

title: Public flame frontend still treats degraded reads as 'No public habits yet'
description:
The backend public-flame contract now returns a `degraded` flag when Social Service visibility reads fail safe, but the frontend still drops that signal and renders the same empty-state copy as if the user simply had no public habits.

## Evidence
- `GET /habits/public/{username}` returns `{ username, habits, degraded }`
- `PublicFlameScreen` only types `{ username, habits }` and ignores `degraded`
- the screen renders "No public habits yet" whenever `habits.length === 0`
- the current Jest coverage explicitly locks in that empty-state behavior instead of distinguishing degraded reads

## User impact
- a real upstream outage is presented as a truthful product state
- viewers can conclude that a user has chosen not to share habits when the real problem is a degraded dependency
- the recently closed backend hardening work is invisible at the product surface

## Scope
- plumb the `degraded` flag through the public-flame frontend contract
- show a truthful degraded or unavailable state instead of the normal empty-state copy when the backend says the read is degraded
- keep unknown-user and genuine-no-public-habits states distinct
- reconcile the existing tests and any close-out notes that currently overstate the fix as end-to-end complete

## Root cause
The backend/public API contract was hardened, but the frontend response type, state machine, and tests were not updated to consume the new field.
acceptance_criteria:
The public flame UI distinguishes degraded reads from genuine empty and not-found states using the live backend contract.

## Frontend behavior
- Happy path: `degraded=false` and visible habits render the normal profile state
- Happy path: `degraded=false` and zero habits render the real "No public habits yet" empty state
- Edge case: `degraded=true` renders a retryable degraded or unavailable state instead of the normal empty-state copy
- Edge case: `404 not_found` still renders the unknown-user state
- Error condition: transport or server failures that never produce a public-flame payload still render the existing retryable error state

## Verification
- Jest coverage asserts the three-way distinction: not found vs degraded vs genuine empty
- If Playwright already covers the public flame page, add or update a degraded-path assertion so the live UI contract is proven end-to-end
- The tracker and close-out history for winzy.ai-e4e.4 are reconciled so they no longer read as fully solved at the product surface when only the backend half was fixed
notes: This is intentionally narrower than reworking the backend. The backend already exposes the degraded signal; the missing work is making the shipped public page consume it truthfully.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 9 (PR #119)

title: E2E CI: set Playwright workers to 4 and add list reporter
description: Currently workers defaults to cpuCount/2 = 1 on the 2-CPU ubuntu-latest runner. All 48 tests run sequentially despite fullyParallel:true. Set workers:4 in CI to allow I/O-bound tests to overlap. Also add 'list' reporter for per-test timing in CI logs (currently only html+github, no stdout timing). One-line changes in playwright.config.ts. Estimated savings: 2-3 minutes.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 8 (PR #118)

title: Enhance Claude GitHub workflows
description:
Improve the Claude GitHub workflows for the Winzy.ai monorepo. This issue owns workflow ergonomics, review behavior, monorepo scoping, and artifact surfacing. It should not re-own browser-test infrastructure or generic CI setup that already belongs elsewhere.

## Workflow Hardening
- [ ] Tighten prompts/instructions so Claude has the right monorepo context for frontend and backend work.
- [ ] Ensure dependency/bootstrap steps are reliable for both stacks before Claude runs.
- [ ] Improve monorepo path filtering or change detection so workflows do not do unnecessary work.
- [ ] Make PR review output more structured and useful for human triage.

## Test And Artifact Surfacing
- [ ] Surface relevant lint/typecheck/test/build results clearly in workflow output.
- [ ] Link or upload useful artifacts from automated checks so review failures are diagnosable.
- [ ] Integrate with the shared testing expectations established in winzy.ai-n5l and the browser artifacts from winzy.ai-ftb.

## Non-Goals
- [ ] Do not duplicate the base CI setup already established in closed setup issues.
- [ ] Do not duplicate Playwright runner ownership from winzy.ai-ftb.

## Notes
- This is targeted workflow hardening, not first-time CI setup.
- Favor changes that reduce noisy reviews and make failures legible.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Claude workflows are narrowed to monorepo-aware execution, clearer review output, and better artifact surfacing.
The issue no longer overlaps with generic CI setup or Playwright runner ownership.
Workflow behavior is documented well enough that future tracker work can rely on it.
close_reason: GitHub workflows hardened with path filtering, caching, artifact surfacing, security fix

title: Consider retry-on-NAK for transient social-service failures in HabitCompletedSubscriber
description: HabitCompletedSubscriber catches HttpRequestException from social-service and returns normally (→ ack). If social-service is temporarily down, that habit.completed message is permanently lost instead of being retried via JetStream NAK. Current behavior is intentional graceful degradation for a secondary side-effect, but may lose notifications during brief outages. Consider: let transient HTTP failures propagate (→ NAK → retry after 5s), but keep the return-on-empty-friends path. MaxDeliver=5 limits retry count. Found by Codex review in Wave 10 PR #120.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 11 — transient HTTP failures now propagate for JetStream NAK retry

title: Friend profile screen
description:
Build the friend profile view. This is the core of passive accountability — seeing a friend's flames is the main value.

## Profile Header
- [ ] Friend's name and avatar
- [ ] Member since date

## Visible Habits — The Core Value
- [ ] List of habits they have shared with you
- [ ] Each habit shows consistency flame prominently (based on their 60-day score)
- [ ] Flame is the MAIN visual element — large, visible, emotional
- [ ] Tap habit to see their calendar/progress

## Actions (secondary)
- [ ] Set challenge button (opens Winzy.ai-d51) — visible but not dominant
- [ ] View active challenges between you

## Data
- [ ] Fetch friend profile from API (GET /friends/{id}/profile)
- [ ] Social Service checks visibility and returns only permitted habits

## Notes
- The friend profile is about CONNECTION, not competition
- Seeing someone's flame burning (or flickering) is itself the accountability mechanism
- You don't need to DO anything — just seeing it matters
- Challenges are an enhancement, not the primary interaction
- Show consistency flames, NOT streak counts

(Originally GH RewardStack #52)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
Friend profile data uses the explicit gateway social contract and includes the identity fields promised by the UI plus visibility-filtered habit/flame data.
Detailed friend-management mutations remain owned by winzy.ai-fj5 instead of being split across profile and management beads.

## Unit Tests (Jest)
- Happy path: renders friend profile header (name, avatar, member-since)
- Happy path: renders visible habits with flame indicators sized for profile view
- Happy path: Set Challenge button visible and navigates to challenge creation
- Edge case: friend with no visible habits shows encouraging empty state
- Edge case: friend with all habits private shows No shared habits message
- Error condition: profile fetch failure renders ErrorState with retry
- Error condition: Social Service down returns degraded response gracefully

## E2E Tests (Playwright)
- From Friends list, tap friend → FriendProfileScreen loads with profile data
- Visible habits show consistency flames (verify flame rendering at profile size)
- Set Challenge button is visible (verify navigation intent)
- Back navigation returns to Friends list
- Detailed step logging for each assertion

title: PWA and web-first infrastructure
description:
Configure the app for a genuinely web-first PWA experience rather than only basic metadata.

## PWA Foundation
- [ ] Configure manifest, icons, theme/background colors, and installability metadata.
- [ ] Define service-worker/offline-shell behavior appropriate for a server-truth app.
- [ ] Ensure route fallback behavior works for direct navigation and refresh on web routes.

## Responsive And Browser Support
- [ ] Define supported browser matrix and responsive breakpoints.
- [ ] Validate the main shell/navigation on common mobile and desktop layouts.
- [ ] Document browser-specific limitations clearly, especially Safari/PWA constraints.

## Web UX Details
- [ ] Keyboard navigation and focus management.
- [ ] SEO/social metadata for public/shareable surfaces where relevant.
- [ ] Fast, stable app boot with sensible route handling.

## Verification
- [ ] Add browser-oriented verification for installability, route fallback, and key supported browsers.
- [ ] Capture performance-relevant logging/measurements for initial load and major regressions.
- [ ] Coordinate with winzy.ai-ftb so the important browser behaviors are exercisable in Playwright.

## Notes
- Mobile web is primary, desktop is secondary but still intentional.
- This should make the app feel native enough on web without pretending unsupported features exist.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The app has a documented, tested web-first/PWA baseline including manifest/installability, route fallback, and supported-browser expectations.
Playwright-backed browser smoke coverage exists for installability, route fallback, and the key supported-browser behaviors this issue promises.
Performance and compatibility expectations are explicit rather than tribal knowledge, and regressions are catchable before release.

title: Friend management flows (add, accept, decline, remove)
description:
Frontend flows for managing friend connections. Backend is covered by Social Service (Winzy.ai-bdd).

## Add Friend
- [ ] Search by username or email (GET /auth/users/search?q=... via Gateway)
- [ ] User search results with avatar + name
- [ ] Send request button (POST /friends/request)
- [ ] Confirmation: 'Request sent!'
- [ ] Share invite link (future)

## Accept / Decline Request
- [ ] Notification or badge on Friends tab for pending requests
- [ ] Incoming request card with Accept / Decline buttons
- [ ] Accept: PUT /friends/request/{id}/accept
- [ ] Decline: PUT /friends/request/{id}/decline
- [ ] Friendly confirmation on accept ('You and X are now friends!')

## Remove Friend
- [ ] Accessible from friend profile (Winzy.ai-ekw) or friends list (Winzy.ai-78l)
- [ ] Confirmation dialog: 'Remove [name] as friend?'
- [ ] DELETE /friends/{id}

## Notifications
- [ ] friend.request.sent → push notification to recipient
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → push notification to sender

## Notes
- User search hits Auth Service (Winzy.ai-dhq), friend operations hit Social Service (Winzy.ai-bdd)
- All API calls go through Gateway
- Keep it low-friction — adding a friend should feel easy and natural
- No 'follower' concept — friendships are always mutual

(Originally GH RewardStack #49)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
Search, request send/accept/decline, and friend removal flows work end-to-end against the explicit gateway social/auth route contract.
This bead owns the detailed request-management interactions even if summary badges or cards also appear on the Friends screen.

## Unit Tests (Jest)
- Happy path: search users by username returns results with avatar and name
- Happy path: send friend request shows confirmation
- Happy path: accept incoming request shows now-friends message
- Happy path: decline incoming request removes from pending list
- Happy path: remove friend shows confirmation dialog and removes on confirm
- Edge case: search with no results shows No users found
- Edge case: send request to self is prevented
- Edge case: duplicate request to same user is prevented or shows appropriate message

- Log in as other user, see and accept friend request
- Both users now appear in each other's friends list
- Remove friend and verify removal on both sides
- Step-by-step logging for each mutation and state verification

description:

- [ ] Touch feedback on all interactive elements (opacity, scale)
- [ ] Empty state illustrations
- [ ] Error state animations (gentle shake, not aggressive)

## Performance

- [ ] Keep animations short (200-400ms)

title: Add Playwright for web E2E testing
description:
Add Playwright as the web E2E runner for the Winzy.ai PWA. This issue owns the actual browser-test infrastructure, fixtures, artifact strategy, and baseline specs. It does NOT own project-scoped agent MCP configuration.

## Scope
- [ ] Install and configure Playwright for deterministic local and CI runs.
- [ ] Create baseline browser projects/fixtures for the supported web target(s).
- [ ] Define a seeded or fixture-driven test-data strategy so E2E runs are repeatable.
- [ ] Add helper flows for auth/session bootstrap and common navigation.

## Baseline Specs
- [ ] Smoke test that the web app boots and renders a stable shell.
- [ ] Baseline auth journey coverage once auth screens are available.
- [ ] Baseline habit-tracking journey coverage once the first end-to-end slice exists.
- [ ] Establish the expectation that future user-facing beads add/update Playwright specs instead of leaving E2E coverage implicit.

## Failure Diagnostics
- [ ] Capture `trace.zip`, screenshots, video (where appropriate), browser console logs, and failed network requests on failure.
- [ ] Publish artifacts in CI so failures can be diagnosed without rerunning locally.
- [ ] Document the local debug workflow for reproducing failed browser tests.

## CI Integration
- [ ] Add root-level commands for Playwright install and test execution.
- [ ] Wire Playwright into CI in a way that works with the monorepo structure.
- [ ] Keep workflow ownership here focused on browser tests and artifacts; broader Claude workflow ergonomics live in winzy.ai-eep.

## Notes
- Depends on winzy.ai-n5l for the broader test harness conventions.
- Project-scoped MCP support for AI agents is tracked in winzy.ai-dy7.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Playwright runs deterministically locally and in CI with documented commands and fixtures.
Browser-test failures retain rich artifacts and logs that are automatically published by CI.
The tracker now has a clear convention that user-facing features must add or update Playwright coverage when behavior changes.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] Playwright installed locally (chromium)
- [ ] Playwright MCP config in .claude/settings.json
- [ ] test-data/ directory created
- [ ] Playwright setup in claude.yml workflow
- [ ] Playwright in claude-code-review.yml workflow
- [ ] Testing workflow documented in CLAUDE.md

title: Tech Debt: Readability, DRY & File Size Cleanup
description:
Coordinated cleanup program to make the Winzy.ai codebase easier to read, drier, and less monolithic without changing product behavior.

This epic exists to improve comprehension first. Smaller files, shared abstractions, and deleted code are all means to that end, not goals by themselves.

This wave should deliver:
- confirmed dead code removed before it gets moved around
- fewer copy-pasted backend, frontend, and test helpers
- clearer backend route organization
- smaller hotspot files where size is actively hurting readability
- tests and shared tooling that are cheaper to extend after the production code settles

Workstreams:
1. dead code and stale-helper cleanup
2. low-risk shared backend primitives
3. service-by-service backend route/module refactors
4. proven shared frontend building blocks
5. oversized screen refactors using those building blocks
6. late-wave test infrastructure cleanup
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Hotspot files are materially easier to scan and navigate than before.
- [ ] Shared abstractions are introduced only where they remove real duplication or clarify a hotspot.
- [ ] Dead code is removed before the structural refactors that would otherwise move it around.
- [ ] Backend route surfaces are easier to understand at a glance.
- [ ] Frontend screens and test files are less repetitive and less monolithic where that improves readability.
- [ ] Quality gates still pass; behavior changes are limited to explicit bug fixes uncovered during cleanup.
notes:
## Principles
- Readability over clever indirection.
- Smaller files are a means, not an end.
- Prefer local extraction over global abstraction when only one file benefits.
- A new helper or component should earn its existence with multiple real call sites or a major readability win.
- Gateway stays independent from Winzy.Common.

## Recommended order
- Do `winzy.ai-4r1y` first.
- Do `winzy.ai-i0a9` before `winzy.ai-ypya`.
- Do `winzy.ai-z8qx` before `winzy.ai-4ljx`.
- Do `winzy.ai-2ny6` late, after the production-file moves settle, so tests are only rewritten once.
close_reason: All child work complete; closing epic shell.

title: Docker Compose health checks and service boot order
description:
Prevent services from crashing on startup because PostgreSQL or NATS isn't ready yet.

## Problem
Without health checks and `depends_on: condition: service_healthy`, .NET services try to
connect to their database the moment Docker starts them — before PostgreSQL has finished
initialising. This causes startup crashes and requires manual `docker compose restart`.

## PostgreSQL Health Checks
Add to every Postgres container in docker-compose.yml:
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d ${DB_NAME}"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
  start_period: 10s
```

## NATS Health Check
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:8222/healthz || exit 1"]
  interval: 5s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 10
```

## Service Boot Order
Every .NET service should declare:
```yaml
depends_on:
  {service}-db:
    condition: service_healthy
  nats:
    condition: service_healthy
```

## Checklist
- [ ] healthcheck added to all 6 PostgreSQL containers (auth-db, habit-db, social-db, challenge-db, notification-db, activity-db)
- [ ] healthcheck added to NATS container
- [ ] All 7 .NET services have depends_on with service_healthy conditions
- [ ] Tested: docker compose up from scratch boots all services without crashes
- [ ] Tested: docker compose up after docker compose down (data persisted)
close_reason: Already implemented in docker-compose.yml — all health checks and depends_on conditions are in place, verified by smoke test

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: This issue appears to be ALREADY DONE. The current docker-compose.yml has: (1) healthchecks on all 6 PostgreSQL containers with pg_isready, (2) healthcheck on NATS with wget to /healthz, (3) depends_on with condition: service_healthy on all 7 services. Should be closed.

title: HTTP test files (.http) for all API endpoints
description:
## Problem

The microservice functionality is verified by ~564 xUnit integration tests and 11 Playwright E2E specs, but there are no manual API test files (Postman, Bruno, or .http) in the project. The course criterion requires: "Funktionalität der Microservices durch Tests (Postman/Bruno) überprüft."

## Goal

Create **.http files** (VS Code REST Client / JetBrains HTTP Client format) that cover every public and authenticated endpoint in the system. These files serve as:
1. **Living API documentation** — shows exactly how to call each endpoint with realistic payloads
2. **Manual verification tool** — one-click execution from the IDE
3. **Course criterion fulfillment** — equivalent to Postman/Bruno but versionable and lightweight

## Structure

Create a top-level \`http/\` directory with one .http file per service:

\`\`\`
http/
├── http-client.env.json       # Environment variables (JetBrains HTTP Client)
├── auth.http                  # Auth service endpoints (10 requests)
├── habits.http                # Habit service endpoints (14 requests)
├── social.http                # Social service endpoints (12 requests)
├── challenges.http            # Challenge service endpoints (6 requests)
├── notifications.http         # Notification service endpoints (8 requests)
├── activity.http              # Activity service endpoints (2 requests)
└── health.http                # Health + infrastructure checks (2 requests)
\`\`\`

**Total: 54 requests across 7 files.**

## Environment setup

### JetBrains (Rider/IntelliJ) — http-client.env.json

\`\`\`json
{
  "dev": {
    "baseUrl": "http://localhost:5050",
    "accessToken": "<paste-after-login>",
    "testEmail": "test@example.com",
    "testUsername": "testuser",
    "testPassword": "TestPassword123!",
    "friendId": "<paste-friend-guid>",
    "habitId": "<paste-habit-guid>",
    "challengeId": "<paste-challenge-guid>",
    "notificationId": "<paste-notification-guid>",
    "requestId": "<paste-from-friend-request-response>",
    "nextCursor": "<paste-from-first-feed-response>"
  }
}
\`\`\`

### VS Code (REST Client extension)

\`http-client.env.json\` is a JetBrains-specific format — VS Code REST Client does NOT read it. For VS Code compatibility, each .http file must start with file-level variable definitions:

\`\`\`http
@baseUrl = http://localhost:5050
@accessToken = <paste-after-login>
\`\`\`

The \`@variable = value\` syntax at file top works in **both** VS Code REST Client and JetBrains HTTP Client. The \`{{variable}}\` interpolation syntax in request URLs/bodies also works in both. So use \`@variable\` definitions at the top of each file as the primary mechanism, and keep \`http-client.env.json\` as a convenience for JetBrains users who want a central env file.

## Endpoint coverage per file

### auth.http (10 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body |
|---|--------|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | POST | /auth/register | Public | \`{ "email", "username", "password", "displayName" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /auth/login | Public | \`{ "emailOrUsername", "password" }\` |
| 3 | POST | /auth/refresh | Public | \`{ "refreshToken" }\` |
| 4 | POST | /auth/logout | Bearer | empty or \`{ "refreshToken" }\` |
| 5 | GET | /auth/profile | Bearer | — |
| 6 | PUT | /auth/profile | Bearer | \`{ "displayName", "avatarUrl" }\` |
| 7 | PUT | /auth/password | Bearer | \`{ "currentPassword", "newPassword" }\` |
| 8 | GET | /auth/users/search?q=test | Bearer | — |
| 9 | GET | /auth/export | Bearer | — |
| 10 | DELETE | /auth/account | Bearer | — (put last — destructive) |

**Flow**: Run register → copy token from response → paste into \`@accessToken\` variable. Login is an alternative entry point.

### habits.http (14 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Morning Run", "icon": "🏃", "frequency": "daily" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Read Book", "icon": "📖", "frequency": "weekly" }\` |
| 3 | POST | /habits | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Gym", "icon": "💪", "frequency": "custom", "customDays": ["Monday","Wednesday","Friday"] }\` |
| 4 | GET | /habits | Bearer | — |
| 5 | GET | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | — |
| 6 | PUT | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | \`{ "name": "Evening Run", "color": "#FF5733" }\` |
| 7 | POST | /habits/{{habitId}}/complete | Bearer | \`{ "timezone": "Europe/Vienna" }\` |
| 8 | POST | /habits/{{habitId}}/complete | Bearer | \`{ "date": "2026-03-15", "timezone": "Europe/Vienna" }\` — backfill |
| 9 | DELETE | /habits/{{habitId}}/completions/2026-03-15 | Bearer | — undo completion |
| 10 | GET | /habits/{{habitId}}/stats | Bearer | Header: \`X-Timezone: Europe/Vienna\` (required — returns 400 without it) |
| 11 | GET | /habits/completions?date=2026-03-18 | Bearer | — today's completions |
| 12 | GET | /habits/public/{{testUsername}} | Public | — public flame page |
| 13 | GET | /habits/public/{{testUsername}}/flame.svg | Public | — SVG badge |
| 14 | DELETE | /habits/{{habitId}} | Bearer | — archive (put last) |

### social.http (12 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /social/friends/request | Bearer | \`{ "friendId": "{{friendId}}" }\` |
| 2 | GET | /social/friends/requests | Bearer | — incoming + outgoing |
| 3 | GET | /social/friends/requests/count | Bearer | — pending count |
| 4 | PUT | /social/friends/request/{{requestId}}/accept | Bearer | — (requires second user's token) |
| 5 | PUT | /social/friends/request/{{requestId}}/decline | Bearer | — |
| 6 | GET | /social/friends?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — paginated list |
| 7 | GET | /social/friends/{{friendId}}/profile | Bearer | — friend's visible habits |
| 8 | PUT | /social/visibility/{{habitId}} | Bearer | \`{ "visibility": "friends" }\` — valid: private, friends, public |
| 9 | GET | /social/visibility | Bearer | — batch: default + all per-habit overrides |
| 10 | GET | /social/preferences | Bearer | — default visibility |
| 11 | PUT | /social/preferences | Bearer | \`{ "defaultHabitVisibility": "friends" }\` |
| 12 | DELETE | /social/friends/{{friendId}} | Bearer | — remove friendship (put last) |

### challenges.http (6 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | POST | /challenges | Bearer | \`{ "habitId": "{{habitId}}", "recipientId": "{{friendId}}", "milestoneType": "consistencyTarget", "targetValue": 80, "periodDays": 30, "rewardDescription": "Coffee at Starbucks" }\` |
| 2 | POST | /challenges | Bearer | \`{ "habitId": "{{habitId}}", "recipientId": "{{friendId}}", "milestoneType": "daysInPeriod", "targetValue": 20, "periodDays": 30, "rewardDescription": "Movie night" }\` |
| 3 | GET | /challenges?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — |
| 4 | GET | /challenges/{{challengeId}} | Bearer | — detail with progress |
| 5 | PUT | /challenges/{{challengeId}}/claim | Bearer | — claim completed challenge |
| 6 | DELETE | /challenges/{{challengeId}} | Bearer | — cancel (put last) |

Valid milestoneType values: \`consistencyTarget\`, \`daysInPeriod\`, \`totalCompletions\`, \`customDateRange\`, \`improvementMilestone\`. For \`customDateRange\`, also include \`customStartDate\` and \`customEndDate\` (ISO 8601 DateTimeOffset).

### notifications.http (8 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Request body / notes |
|---|--------|------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | GET | /notifications/vapid-public-key | Public | — VAPID key for web push |
| 2 | GET | /notifications?page=1&pageSize=10 | Bearer | — |
| 3 | GET | /notifications/unread-count | Bearer | — |
| 4 | PUT | /notifications/{{notificationId}}/read | Bearer | — mark single read |
| 5 | PUT | /notifications/read-all | Bearer | — mark all read |
| 6 | PUT | /notifications/settings | Bearer | \`{ "habitReminders": true, "friendActivity": true, "challengeUpdates": true }\` |
| 7 | POST | /notifications/devices | Bearer | \`{ "platform": "web_push", "token": "test-push-token", "deviceId": "test-device-1" }\` |
| 8 | DELETE | /notifications/devices | Bearer | \`{ "deviceId": "test-device-1" }\` |

### activity.http (2 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Notes |
|---|--------|------|------|-------|
| 1 | GET | /activity/feed?limit=20 | Bearer | — first page |
| 2 | GET | /activity/feed?cursor={{nextCursor}}&limit=20 | Bearer | — subsequent page (cursor from previous response's \`nextCursor\` field) |

### health.http (2 requests)

| # | Method | Path | Auth | Notes |
|---|--------|------|------|-------|
| 1 | GET | /health | Public | — aggregated gateway health |
| 2 | GET | http://localhost:8222/healthz | Public | — NATS monitoring health (requires debug compose overlay: \`docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.debug.yml up\`) |

Note: NATS monitoring runs on port 8222 (\`-m 8222\`), not the client port 4222. Port 8222 is only host-mapped in \`docker-compose.debug.yml\`, not the standard compose file.

## Format conventions

Each .http file should follow this pattern:

\`\`\`http
@baseUrl = http://localhost:5050
@accessToken = <paste-after-login>

### Register a new user
POST {{baseUrl}}/auth/register
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "email": "test@example.com",
  "username": "testuser",
  "password": "TestPassword123!",
  "displayName": "Test User"
}

### Login
# Copy accessToken from the response and paste into @accessToken above
POST {{baseUrl}}/auth/login
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "emailOrUsername": "test@example.com",
  "password": "TestPassword123!"
}

### Get profile (authenticated)
GET {{baseUrl}}/auth/profile
Authorization: Bearer {{accessToken}}
\`\`\`

Rules:
- **\`@variable = value\` at file top** — works in both VS Code REST Client and JetBrains HTTP Client
- **\`###\` separators** between requests (standard .http format)
- **Descriptive comment** above each request explaining what it does
- **\`{{variable}}\`** syntax for interpolation (compatible with both IDEs)
- **Realistic payloads** — use "Morning Run", "Europe/Vienna", actual enum values
- **Logical ordering** — CRUD order (create → read → update → use → delete). Destructive operations last.
- **Comments for flow dependencies** — e.g., "# Copy accessToken from the login response above"
- **Both happy path and edge case requests** where useful (e.g., duplicate registration, invalid email)

## What NOT to do

- **Do not include internal endpoints** (/internal/*, /habits/user/{userId}) — they are blocked by the gateway's InternalRouteBlockMiddleware and cannot be reached through the gateway
- **Do not hardcode tokens** — use {{accessToken}} variable
- **Do not create Postman collections or Bruno files** — .http is the chosen format
- **Do not auto-generate** — write each request manually with meaningful test data and descriptive comments

## Test plan

- Open each .http file in VS Code with REST Client extension (or JetBrains Rider)
- Run register → login → copy token → paste into @accessToken
- Execute requests top to bottom in each file
- Verify each returns expected status code and response shape
- Verify public endpoints work without Bearer token
- Verify authenticated endpoints return 401 without Bearer token
close_reason: Completed in Wave 8 (PR #118)

comments.author: Gabko14

- "notifications.http (7 requests)" → actually **8 rows**
- "activity.http (1 request)" → actually **2 rows**
- auth.http (10) and health.http (2) are correct

**Fix:** Use the table row counts as the source of truth. The tables themselves are correct — only the headers are stale.

### 2. NATS health endpoint uses wrong port

health.http says `GET http://localhost:4222/healthz`. Port 4222 is the NATS **client** port. The monitoring/health port is **8222** (configured via `-m 8222` in docker-compose).

Additionally, port 8222 is only host-mapped in `docker-compose.debug.yml`, not the standard compose (which uses `expose` only).

**Fix:** Change to `GET http://localhost:8222/healthz` and add a comment: `# Requires debug compose overlay: docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.debug.yml up`

### 3. Missing environment variables

`{{requestId}}` (used in social.http for accept/decline) and `{{nextCursor}}` (used in activity.http for pagination) are referenced but not in `http-client.env.json`.

**Fix:** Add placeholder entries to the env file:
```json
"requestId": "<paste-from-friend-request-response>",
"nextCursor": "<paste-from-first-feed-response>"
```

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: All corrections from both review rounds have been incorporated into the updated description. The comment above is superseded.

title: Extract low-risk shared backend primitives to Winzy.Common
description:
Create the shared backend primitives that clearly earn reuse before the big service-by-service refactor begins.

This bead should stay disciplined: it is not the place to build an internal framework. Only extract helpers whose behavior is genuinely identical across multiple services and whose call sites become simpler, not more magical.

Primary candidates:
- `HttpContext` user-id helpers for the repeated `TryGetUserId` pattern
- shared JSON serializer options used across services
- a request-body parsing helper for the repeated bad-JSON or missing-body flow
- a service `HttpClient` registration helper for the repeated `AddHttpClient` setup
- shared auth profile response shape in `Winzy.Contracts`
- optionally, a best-effort NATS publish wrapper if the call sites truly line up
- optionally, shared service bootstrap helpers if the non-gateway services are similar enough after careful comparison
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Low-risk shared primitives are added only for patterns that have multiple real call sites and materially improve readability.
- [ ] Unit tests cover the user-id helper, request-body parsing helper, and shared JSON options if those helpers are added.
- [ ] Any contract move such as `ProfileInfo` into `Winzy.Contracts` compiles cleanly.
- [ ] No broad service rewrite happens yet beyond the minimal changes needed to introduce shared contracts or helper tests.
- [ ] Shared-project builds and tests pass.
notes:
## Guardrails
- Gateway must remain independent from Winzy.Common.
- Do not hide materially different semantics behind one helper.
- Prefer tiny extension methods and utilities over large base classes.
- If a candidate abstraction only helps one service or makes call sites less obvious, skip it.

## Candidate output
A good outcome for this bead is a small set of boring, obvious primitives that the next backend bead can apply safely. If the helper list starts to look like a custom framework, the scope has drifted.
close_reason: Committed in Wave 13

comments.author: gabko14
comments.text: Implemented shared backend primitives: HttpContextExtensions (TryGetUserId + GetUserId), JsonDefaults (CamelCase options), RequestBodyHelper (TryReadBodyAsync), ServiceClientExtensions (AddServiceHttpClient), and ProfileInfo moved to Contracts. 20 tests pass. Gateway untouched.

title: Error handling and offline state management
description:
Define the shared frontend runtime behavior for errors, offline state, retries, and session recovery. This issue should keep major screens from inventing their own token-refresh and error-handling logic.

## Shared API/Session Layer
- [ ] Centralized API client with consistent request/response/error handling.
- [ ] Session bootstrap on app load using the supported auth refresh flow.
- [ ] Queue/retry behavior for requests interrupted by token refresh.
- [ ] Timeout and retry handling for transient failures.

## Error And Offline UX
- [ ] Friendly, actionable error messaging that aligns with the product tone.
- [ ] Shared loading/empty/error presentation patterns for major screens.
- [ ] Offline detection, subtle offline indicators, and graceful disabling/retry behavior.
- [ ] React error boundaries and safe fallback UI for unexpected client crashes.

## Logging And Diagnostics
- [ ] Structured client-side logging for token-refresh failures, retried requests, surfaced API errors, and offline/online transitions during E2E/debug runs.
- [ ] Document how downstream screens should hook into the shared runtime instead of bypassing it.

## Notes
- Server remains the source of truth; this is graceful recovery, not offline-first sync.
- The goal is consistency and recoverability across screens.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Major frontend flows can rely on one shared session/error/offline runtime.
Tests cover refresh/retry behavior, offline transitions, and user-facing error presentation.
Client-side diagnostics are detailed enough to explain failed auth/network flows during debugging and E2E runs.
close_reason: Frontend error handling with ErrorBoundary and OfflineIndicator implemented

title: E2E CI: pre-build service images in CI-backend, pull in CI-E2E
description: Instead of building 7 Docker images in the E2E workflow, build them in CI-backend (which already compiles all services) and push to ghcr.io. The E2E workflow then pulls pre-built images. Eliminates the 3-4 minute build step entirely. Trade-off: images may be stale on feature branches if only frontend changed — need conditional logic to rebuild when service code changes. Implementation: add docker build+push step to ci-backend.yml, change ci-e2e.yml to docker compose pull instead of --build.

(2) challenge-service/Program.cs: 1 request body ReadFromJsonAsync with no JsonException catch (line 67). Add JsonException catches, narrow NATS catches, add tests.

title: Consistency calculation algorithm (60-day rolling window)
description:
Define and implement the consistency calculation that powers The Flame.

## Core Algorithm
- [ ] Calculate: (actual completions / expected completions) over rolling window
- [ ] Default window: 60 days
- [ ] Result: percentage 0-100

## Edge Cases
- [ ] New habits (<60 days): Use available days as denominator
- [ ] Habits started today: Show 0% or 'Just started' state
- [ ] Weekly habits: Expected = weeks in window, not days
- [ ] Custom frequency: Calculate expected based on selected days

## The Flame Mapping
- [ ] Define percentage to Flame intensity mapping
- [ ] 'Grows quickly': Fast response to improvement
- [ ] 'Shrinks slowly': Gradual decline, forgiving

## Example Calculations
Daily habit, 60-day window:
- 60/60 completions = 100% (blazing)
- 45/60 completions = 75% (strong)
- 30/60 completions = 50% (medium)
- 6/60 completions = 10% (ember)

Weekly habit (1x per week), 60-day window (~8 weeks):
- 8/8 completions = 100%
- 6/8 completions = 75%

## Implementation
- [ ] Backend: calculateConsistency() in Habit Service (.NET)
- [ ] Returned as part of GET /habits/{id}/stats response
- [ ] Frontend: getFlameIntensity(consistencyPercentage) for visual mapping
- [ ] Consider caching with smart invalidation


## Timezone Handling (CRITICAL)
- [ ] Consistency window uses the user's LOCAL calendar dates, not UTC
- [ ] '60-day window start' = midnight of (today - 60 days) in user's timezone
- [ ] Client must supply IANA timezone (e.g. America/New_York) with every completion
- [ ] Stored as local_date column on the completions table for fast window queries
- Edge case: user logs at 11 PM UTC-5 -> server sees 4 AM next day UTC -> must use local_date, not completed_at

## Notes
- This replaces streaks as the PRIMARY metric
- Implemented in the Habit Service backend, consumed by frontend
- No streak counters anywhere in the app - consistency is the only metric

(Originally GH RewardStack #72)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
The consistency algorithm defines one clear backend/frontend contract, including the response fields needed by flame and stats consumers.
Tests cover boundary math, timezone-sensitive windows, and representative daily/weekly/custom-frequency scenarios.
The frontend-facing flame/intensity mapping inputs are explicit enough that downstream components do not reinterpret the rules differently.
close_reason: Flame intensity mapping with asymmetric thresholds and 54 new tests

title: Habit Tracking
description:
The core habit-tracking product: Habit Service, consistency algorithm, flame rendering, create/manage flows, Today screen, and habit detail. This epic is a coordination container, not a direct implementation bead.

## Critical Child Sequence
- Habit backend contract and consistency math (`winzy.ai-wxa`, `winzy.ai-izs`)
- Shared flame rendering contract (`winzy.ai-6m3`)
- First-habit management and daily-use surfaces (`winzy.ai-3mk`, `winzy.ai-k2x`, `winzy.ai-2r7`)
- Supporting extensions such as stats or public views build on that slice

## Done Means
- Child issues are linked and kept current.
- A user can create a habit, complete it, see consistent flame feedback, and correct/view progress without streak-based regressions.
- The first-habit journey is coherent end-to-end.

## Success Criteria
- The relevant child issues are linked and kept current.
- See this epic's description above for the intended child sequence and done state.
close_reason: All substantive children closed (wxa, izs, 6m3, 3mk, k2x, 2r7). Only in-progress dependent is 3sf (range consistency bug, tracked separately). The core habit tracking product is implemented end-to-end: create, complete, flame, detail, stats.

title: Honest Minimums — truthful fallback completions that keep the ember alive
description:
Let a habit define an optional "Honest Minimum": a smaller, specific version of the habit that counts as showing up on hard days without pretending it is the same as a full completion. This is not a streak-saving loophole and not a fake win. It is a truthful middle state between a full completion and a total miss.

## Core Concept
- Each habit can optionally define one Honest Minimum, such as "10-minute walk" for a 45-minute workout or "read 2 pages" for "read 20 pages"
- On a given day, the user can log either a full completion or an Honest Minimum
- Honest Minimums contribute less than full completions but more than zero to the habit's consistency/flame through one explicit shared weighting model
- UI and history must always distinguish full completions from Honest Minimums
- A habit sustained only on Honest Minimums should not look equivalent to one sustained on full completions

## Product Philosophy
This feature should operationalize Winzy's core belief that consistency matters more than perfection while still preserving honesty.

It should help users avoid all-or-nothing collapse:
- full completion > honest minimum > miss
- but honest minimum must never be framed as fully completing the habit

## V1 Scope
- Add optional Honest Minimum configuration to the existing create/edit habit flows
- Update the habit completion model so a logged day can be `full`, `minimum`, or `none`
- Today screen supports logging a full completion or an Honest Minimum for the current day
- Habit Detail supports viewing and correcting minimum vs full completions within the existing editable window
- Flame, consistency, and stats surfaces use one shared minimum-weighted contract instead of treating minimums as binary full wins
- Supportive copy makes it clear that the user kept the ember alive without claiming they fully did the habit

## Behavioral Rules
- Honest Minimum configuration should be a concrete smaller action, not a vague motivational sentence
- V1 should use one documented weighting model across the product rather than per-habit custom percentages
- Users should be able to move a day between `none`, `minimum`, and `full` through the existing correction flows
- Existing flame/public/stat consumers may inherit the updated aggregate consistency, but v1 does not need a separate public-facing minimum-day narrative

## Owner Experience
- Configure Honest Minimum while creating/editing a habit or later from the habit detail/edit flow
- On Today, choose between marking the habit fully done or logging the Honest Minimum
- On Habit Detail, calendar/history clearly show whether a day was full or minimum
- Stats show both the distinction and the aggregate effect without collapsing them into one number-only story

## Architecture Notes
- Completion records need an explicit completion kind/state so backend and frontend do not guess from copy
- Habit Service and consistency math remain the source of truth; do not bolt on a frontend-only interpretation of minimums
- Flame thresholds or messaging may need recalibration once partial contributions exist, but the result must still feel forgiving and legible
- Public/share surfaces that already consume aggregate flame should only expose what the underlying visibility rules already allow

## Quality Expectations
- Backend tests cover configuration, logging, corrections, weighted consistency math, timezone boundaries, and mixed full/minimum histories
- Frontend tests cover create/edit configuration, Today interactions, Habit Detail rendering, and supportive copy
- Playwright covers configure minimum -> log minimum -> verify distinct Today/detail/stats behavior -> upgrade that day to full -> verify stronger effect, with detailed step logging
acceptance_criteria:
Users can configure and log an Honest Minimum as a distinct weaker-than-full completion state for a habit without collapsing it into a fake full win or a binary miss.
Consistency, flame, and stats surfaces reuse one explicit minimum-weighted contract so Honest Minimums matter, remain visually/semantically distinct from full completions, and never introduce streak-style regression.
Tests cover configuration, daily logging, historical correction, weighted math, supportive copy, and mixed-history edge cases well enough that product-truthfulness regressions are catchable before release.
notes:
## Non-Goals
- No arbitrary user-defined weighting percentages in v1
- No multiple minimum tiers per habit in v1
- No streak-preservation language or "X days in a row" framing
- No silently auto-converting a miss into a minimum; the user must log or correct it intentionally
- No fake marketing language that treats Honest Minimums as equivalent to full completions

## Suggested Verification
### Backend Unit / Integration
- Happy path: habit with configured Honest Minimum can log `minimum` and `full` completion kinds on different days
- Happy path: changing a day from minimum to full increases the weighted consistency/flame contribution as expected
- Happy path: removing a minimum completion returns the day to `none` cleanly
- Edge case: mixed history of full/minimum/miss days produces the documented aggregate consistency result
- Edge case: weekly/custom-frequency habits apply the minimum weighting coherently within the existing expected-completions model
- Edge case: timezone-boundary logging still assigns the correct local date for minimum completions
- Error condition: invalid completion kind transitions or malformed minimum configuration return clear validation errors
- Error condition: habits without an Honest Minimum configured cannot log `minimum` accidentally

### Frontend Unit / Component
- Happy path: create/edit habit flow lets the owner define or remove an Honest Minimum
- Happy path: Today renders distinct affordances for `full` and `minimum` logging when configured
- Happy path: Habit Detail/calendar/history render minimum days distinctly from full days
- Edge case: supportive copy for minimum days says the user showed up without claiming full completion
- Edge case: habits without a configured minimum keep the simpler full/none interaction model
- Verification: no streak language or fake-equivalence copy appears anywhere in Honest Minimum UI
- Error condition: logging or saving minimum config failures preserve context and show recoverable feedback

### E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Create or edit a habit to add an Honest Minimum
- Log an Honest Minimum from Today and verify the day is rendered distinctly in Habit Detail
- Verify stats/flame change, but less than a full completion would
- Upgrade that same day from minimum to full and verify the stronger effect
- Remove the Honest Minimum configuration and verify the extra Today affordance disappears
- Detailed step logging for configuration, logging, correction, and flame/stat assertions
close_reason: Completed in Wave 12

title: Habit consistency truthfulness still drifts at the 60-day boundary and on day one
description:
Two core truthfulness problems remain in the habit model: the API accepts a completion date outside the visible/correctable 60-day window, and a habit created today can become effectively 100 percent or blazing after a single same-day completion.

Scope:
- Align accepted completion dates with the actual visible correction window
- Revisit the day-one consistency/flame contract so new habits do not overstate continuity
- Add tests that lock the final truthfulness rules across backend and frontend surfaces

title: Core habit flows still break archive and weekly scheduling behavior
description:
Existing core habit flows still have two major basics failures: archive from Habit Detail is a no-op in the real shell, and weekly habits created through the shipped UI do not collect/persist the schedule shape that Today filtering expects.

Scope:
- Wire Habit Detail archive to the real archive mutation path
- Make weekly frequency coherent end-to-end in create/edit, Today filtering, and tests
- Add regression coverage through real shell wiring, not just leaf-screen unit tests

title: HabitDetailScreen needs visible web feedback for failed date corrections

Habit Detail currently avoids showing mutation feedback on web when a date-toggle correction fails, leaving the user with a silent no-op.

Scope:

- Add regression coverage for the real web failure case

title: Consistency timezone contract still diverges across friend profile, public flame JSON, and flame badge
description:
Habit-service now supplies precomputed consistency to social-service, but the different share surfaces still evaluate or transport that truth under different timezone rules.

## Evidence
- `/habits/user/{userId}` computes consistency with `TimeZoneInfo.Utc`
- `/habits/public/{username}` uses viewer `X-Timezone` (or UTC fallback)
- `/habits/public/{username}/flame.svg` recalculates aggregate consistency in UTC
- existing `ConsistencyCalculator` tests already prove UTC fallback can materially change results near timezone boundaries

## User impact
- the same habit or user can show different consistency and flame levels across friend profile, public page, and badge
- viewer timezone can change public JSON while the SVG badge ignores it
- the current "habit-service is the source of truth" claim remains only partially true because each route still applies a different time context

## Scope
- choose one explicit timezone policy for non-owner share surfaces
- align friend-profile composition, public flame JSON, and flame badge behavior to that policy
- add parity coverage for a boundary case where UTC fallback would currently drift

## Root cause
The recent fix centralized the math in habit-service, but the timezone context for share surfaces remained implicit and route-specific.
acceptance_criteria:
One explicit timezone policy exists for share surfaces, and friend profile, public flame JSON, and flame badge all follow it consistently.

## Contract
- The chosen source of timezone for share surfaces is documented explicitly instead of being inferred from whichever endpoint happens to calculate the score
- Public JSON and flame badge no longer disagree because one honors a request timezone while the other ignores it

## Verification
- Happy path: the same seeded habits produce the same consistency and flame outputs across friend profile, public JSON, and badge when evaluated under the chosen policy
- Edge case: a habit created or completed near local midnight yields identical cross-surface results in an automated boundary test
- Tests document whether share surfaces use owner timezone, request timezone, or a deliberate fixed policy so future work cannot reintroduce silent drift
notes: Auth-service does not currently expose a stored user timezone, so this may require an explicit design choice rather than a blind code alignment. The important outcome is cross-surface parity and a documented contract, not a specific policy by default.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 9 (PR #119)

title: EF Core & PostgreSQL Migrations
description:
Establish the shared EF Core + PostgreSQL migration conventions and bootstrapping pattern used by all services. This issue owns the common approach; concrete service schemas still live in the relevant service implementation beads.

## Shared EF/Migration Foundation
- [ ] Standardize package choices, DbContext registration, migration project layout, and naming conventions.
- [ ] Define shared PostgreSQL conventions: snake_case naming, UUID keys, timestamptz/date usage, and indexing expectations.
- [ ] Document the migration/bootstrapping pattern every service follows in development and CI.

## Runtime Behavior
- [ ] Services can apply pending migrations safely in the supported development workflow.
- [ ] Migration startup logs clearly show what was applied or why startup failed.
- [ ] Migration application is idempotent for a clean database and a partially initialized database.

## Validation
- [ ] At least one representative service proves the pattern end-to-end with integration coverage.
- [ ] Service-specific beads remain responsible for their own entities, schema details, and concrete migrations.
- [ ] Dev seed-data behavior is kept separate from this issue unless it is needed only to validate the migration pattern.

## Notes
- This issue should reduce duplicated EF setup work without hiding service-specific schema ownership.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented migration pattern exists that service beads can reuse consistently.
Clean and partially initialized databases can boot through the supported migration path with useful logs.
The issue no longer ambiguously owns every service schema in full.
close_reason: done

title: Today screen
description:
Build the main Today screen, the core daily touchpoint of the habit tracker.

## Layout
- [ ] Header with local-date context
- [ ] List of habits scheduled for today
- [ ] Strong empty state when no habits exist
- [ ] Clear CTA into the create-habit flow

## Habit Item
- [ ] Habit name and icon
- [ ] Completion action
- [ ] Flame indicator based on the 60-day consistency contract
- [ ] Fast visual feedback on completion

## Interactions
- [ ] Navigate to habit detail
- [ ] Quick actions where appropriate
- [ ] Pull to refresh and recover cleanly from transient errors/offline states

## Data And Timezone Behavior
- [ ] Fetch habits/completions through the shared runtime
- [ ] Filter by today's schedule using the device/user timezone
- [ ] Pass IANA timezone with completion requests
- [ ] Support the first-habit empty-state journey cleanly with winzy.ai-3mk

## Quality Expectations
- [ ] Component/state tests cover empty, loading, error, and populated states.
- [ ] A Playwright journey covers the first successful habit completion path with detailed step logging.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The Today screen works as the main daily surface for empty, loading, error, and populated states.
Timezone-sensitive completion behavior and the first-habit journey are exercised in tests.
The screen relies on shared frontend runtime/design primitives instead of custom one-off handling.

title: Auth screens (Sign in, Sign up)
description:
Build the authentication UI screens for the web-first app.

## Screens
- [ ] Sign In screen with email/password form and sign-up link.
- [ ] Sign Up screen with username, email, and password inputs plus sign-in link.

## UX Requirements
- [ ] Inline validation, loading states, and supportive error messaging.
- [ ] Username capture during sign-up with URL-safe validation and collision handling.
- [ ] Clear feedback for reserved/invalid usernames and auth failures.
- [ ] Keyboard-aware behavior and sensible input focus order.

## Navigation And Session Flow
- [ ] Auth stack stays separate from the main app shell.
- [ ] Successful auth lands the user in the app and restores session correctly on reload/app launch.
- [ ] Invalid/expired sessions route back through the shared runtime from winzy.ai-im1.

## API Integration
- [ ] POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, and POST /auth/refresh use the supported token-storage strategy.
- [ ] Web path uses in-memory access token plus HttpOnly refresh cookie.
- [ ] Native path can continue using secure storage where supported.

## Quality Expectations
- [ ] Component/hook tests cover validation, loading, and failure states.
- [ ] At least one Playwright auth journey includes detailed step logging.

## Notes
- Forgot-password/reset stays out of scope for MVP.
- This screen is also where the public-username model becomes real for downstream social/public-page features.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Sign-in and sign-up flows work end-to-end with the intended session bootstrap behavior.
Tests cover username validation/collision handling, auth error states, and session recovery on web.
A documented Playwright journey verifies the main auth flow with useful failure logs.
close_reason: done

title: Optimize claude-code-review CI workflow — add max_turns, strip tools, pre-compute diff
description: The claude-code-action@v1 review step takes 10+ minutes per PR because: (1) no max_turns limit so the LLM can loop indefinitely, (2) Edit/Write/Bash tools are enabled but unnecessary for a read-only review, (3) no pre-computed diff passed in the prompt forces the LLM to discover changes itself, (4) no timeout-minutes cap. Fix: add max_turns (e.g. 15), strip tools to read-only (Read, Glob, Grep + inline comment), pre-compute the diff and pass it in the prompt, add timeout-minutes: 15. Found during Wave 11 CI investigation.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 12

title: Add promise visibility model for public/witness flame surfaces
description: Promise entity has no visibility field. The bead spec says 'If the owner opts in, an active promise can appear on existing flame surfaces' and 'promise visibility can only be equal to or narrower than the habit.' V1 deliberately omits this — promises are owner-only. V2 needs: IsPublicOnFlame bool on Promise, public flame endpoint includes promise data when opted in, witness viewer includes promise for allowed habits. Found by Codex review.
close_reason: Committed in Wave 13

title: Challenge Service HabitService client defaults to wrong port 5001 instead of 5002
description:
In services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs line ~37, the HabitService HttpClient defaults to http://habit-service:5001 but the Habit Service listens on port 5002. The docker-compose environment variable overrides this in practice, so it works in the stack — but host-run development or any configuration that relies on the default will fail silently.

## Fix
Change the default URL from http://habit-service:5001 to http://habit-service:5002 in the AddHttpClient registration.

## Tests
- Verify existing integration tests still pass
- Verify the service resolves habits correctly when no env override is present

## Acceptance Criteria
- The default HabitService client URL matches the actual Habit Service port (5002)
- Tests confirm habit resolution works without environment overrides
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Setup testing infrastructure
description:
Configure testing infrastructure for frontend and backend. Testing is a hard gate: implementation beads should be able to rely on a real shared harness instead of inventing ad-hoc tests per feature.

## Testing Philosophy
- Every backend service gets unit tests for domain logic and validators.
- Every backend HTTP surface gets integration tests against real infrastructure.
- Messaging behavior is covered with JetStream-enabled integration tests, not only mocks.
- Frontend user flows get component/hook coverage plus Playwright journeys for critical paths.
- Failures must be diagnosable from logs and artifacts, not just a red test name.

## Frontend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Configure Jest for the Expo web-first app with shared render/test utilities.
- [ ] Provide patterns for component, hook, and screen tests.
- [ ] Standardize API, storage, and navigation mocking helpers.
- [ ] Add coverage reporting and root-level commands for local and CI runs.

## Backend Testing Foundation
- [ ] Create reusable xUnit test patterns for all services.
- [ ] Provide Testcontainers helpers for PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS.
- [ ] Add reusable helpers for HTTP assertions, DB assertions, and event assertions.
- [ ] Cover startup stream setup, publish/subscribe flows, malformed payload handling, retry/ack behavior, and dependency failure scenarios.
- [ ] Avoid the known concurrent `dotnet test` file-locking problem by serializing runs or isolating outputs when needed.

## Diagnostics And Artifacts
- [ ] Test failures emit useful logs: request/response details, affected entity IDs, emitted NATS subjects, and relevant assertion context.
- [ ] CI publishes machine-readable test results plus human-useful failure artifacts.
- [ ] Document how feature beads are expected to add or extend tests using the shared harness.

## CI Integration
- [ ] Frontend and backend test commands run from the repo root.
- [ ] Coverage/results are visible in CI checks.
- [ ] Later feature and bug beads can depend on this issue as the canonical test foundation.

## Notes
- Playwright runner/setup is tracked in winzy.ai-ftb.
- Project-scoped agent MCP config is tracked separately in winzy.ai-dy7.
- Better to tighten the harness now than rediscover infrastructure bugs during feature work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
A shared frontend/backend test harness exists with documented local and CI entry points.
Backend integration coverage uses real PostgreSQL and JetStream-enabled NATS containers rather than mock-only messaging tests.
Failure output includes actionable logs/artifacts so feature teams can debug broken flows quickly.
close_reason: done

title: Add GET endpoint for habit completions by date range
description: The Today screen needs to know which habits are completed today on initial load. Currently there's no endpoint to query completions for a specific date. Add GET /habits/completions?date=YYYY-MM-DD (or date range) that returns completion status for all user habits on the given date. This will eliminate the need for the Today screen to start with all habits showing as uncompleted.
close_reason: Added GET /habits/completions?date=YYYY-MM-DD endpoint returning completion status for all user habits on a given date. Returns habit id, name, icon, color, and completed boolean.

title: Per-habit visibility settings (frontend UI)
description:
Frontend UI for controlling who can see each habit. This issue is the client-side control surface for Social Service visibility rules.

## Visibility Options
- [ ] Private — only you can see this habit
- [ ] Friends — all approved friends can see this habit
- [ ] Public — anyone with the public flame link can see this habit

## UI Location
- [ ] Toggle in Create Habit flow for initial visibility
- [ ] Toggle in Edit Habit flow for later changes
- [ ] Clear visual indicator on habit items/details

## Implementation
- [ ] Default remains private unless the user's explicit social preference says otherwise.
- [ ] Save visibility through Social Service, which remains the source of truth.
- [ ] Read current visibility/defaults from Social Service-backed data, not an independent habit-side visibility model.

## Notes
- Privacy is the default and sharing is explicit.
- Keep the control simple: clear choices, no permission-matrix complexity.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Users can set and update habit visibility from the create/edit flows using the Social Service source of truth.
The frontend no longer assumes a second independent visibility store.

## Unit Tests (Jest)
- Happy path: create habit flow shows visibility picker (private/friends/public)
- Happy path: default visibility comes from Social Service user preferences
- Happy path: changing visibility in edit flow calls PUT /social/visibility/{habitId}
- Happy path: visibility badge renders correct icon/text on habit items
- Edge case: Social Service down during create → habit created as private, user informed
- Edge case: visibility change fails → optimistic UI reverts to previous value with toast
- Edge case: habit created just now has no visibility row yet → defaults to private correctly
- Error condition: PUT returns 404 (habit ownership validation fails) → clear error
- Error condition: batch GET /social/visibility fails → habits shown without visibility badges

## E2E Tests (Playwright)
- Create a habit with visibility set to friends
- Verify visibility badge shows on habit list
- Edit habit visibility to public
- Check public flame page includes the now-public habit
- Set back to private and verify public flame page no longer shows it
- Detailed step logging
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text:
Audit 2026-03-13: Test requirements enhanced. Key implementation note: Social Service already has PUT /social/visibility/{habitId}, GET /social/visibility, and PUT /social/preferences endpoints.

## Unit Tests (Jest)
1. Happy path: create habit flow shows visibility picker (private/friends/public)
2. Happy path: default visibility comes from Social Service user preferences
3. Happy path: changing visibility in edit flow calls PUT /social/visibility/{habitId}
4. Happy path: visibility badge renders correct icon/text on habit items
5. Edge case: Social Service down during create → habit created as private, user informed
6. Edge case: visibility change fails → optimistic UI reverts to previous value with toast
7. Edge case: habit created just now has no visibility row yet → defaults to private correctly
8. Error condition: PUT returns 404 (habit ownership validation fails) → clear error
9. Error condition: batch GET /social/visibility fails → habits shown without visibility badges

## E2E Tests (Playwright)
1. Create a habit with visibility set to 'friends'
2. Verify visibility badge shows on habit list
3. Edit habit visibility to 'public'
4. Check public flame page includes the now-public habit
5. Set back to 'private' and verify public flame page no longer shows it
6. Detailed step logging

title: Add dead letter queue and MaxDeliver exhaustion metrics for NATS subscribers
description: After MaxDeliver=5 retries, JetStream silently drops messages with no notification. There's no DLQ (MaxDeliverSubject) configured and no metric emitted on final delivery attempt. This means notification fan-out failures during social-service outages >25s are permanently lost with no alerting. Add: (1) ConsumerConfig.MaxDeliverSubject for dead letter routing, (2) counter metric nats_message_max_deliver_exhausted{consumer=...} for alerting. Found by DevOps expert review in Wave 11.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 12

title: Add rate limiting to data export endpoint
description: GET /auth/export fans out to 4+ services in parallel. No rate limit prevents abuse. Add per-user rate limiting (e.g. 1 req/min).
close_reason: Completed in Wave 6, merged in PR #115

title: Local development data seeding script
description:
Create a local development seed flow that populates the running stack with realistic but deterministic data for cross-service testing and demos.

## Seed Goals
- [ ] Create a stable set of users, habits, completions, friendships, visibility settings, and at least one challenge.
- [ ] Use deterministic identities, usernames, and passwords so docs and tests can reference them consistently.
- [ ] Produce data varied enough to exercise different flame states and social scenarios.

## Execution Model
- [ ] Prefer seeding through the API Gateway so the real request path is exercised.
- [ ] Decide whether the script is idempotent, resettable, or both; document that choice clearly.
- [ ] Make the minimum required stack explicit before the seed flow runs.

## Scope Boundary
- [ ] Core deterministic coverage is required for Auth, Habit, Social, and Challenge domains.
- [ ] If derived consumers such as Notification or Activity are also seeded/verified, that must be explicit and deterministic rather than implied.
- [ ] If derived consumers are out of scope for the current seed flow, the output/docs must say so clearly to avoid misleading demos.

## Output And Diagnostics
- [ ] Print a verbose summary of created or reused entities, including stable IDs/usernames where helpful.
- [ ] Make failures easy to diagnose with step-by-step logging rather than a generic crash.
- [ ] Document how the seed data maps to common manual test and demo scenarios.

## Notes
- This is for local development and demos, not production bootstrapping.
- Keep the data realistic, but favor determinism over randomness.
acceptance_criteria:
A documented seed flow can populate deterministic core domain data across the supported local stack.
The seed output clearly states whether derived consumers such as notifications/activity were also verified or are intentionally out of scope for that run.
Manual testing and demos can rely on known seeded users/relationships, and failures are diagnosable from the script output.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Publish habit.archived NATS event for Social Service visibility cleanup
description:
ADR-001 (cross-service visibility orchestration) recommends publishing a habit.archived event when a habit is deleted/archived, so the Social Service can clean up orphaned visibility_settings rows. Currently:

- DELETE /habits/{id} in Habit Service soft-deletes (sets ArchivedAt) but publishes NO event
- Social Service has no subscriber for habit archival
- Orphaned visibility rows accumulate in social_service DB (harmless but messy)

## Scope
- Add HabitArchived subject to Contracts/Subjects.cs
- Add HabitArchivedEvent record to Contracts/HabitEvents.cs  
- Publish habit.archived from Habit Service DELETE endpoint after successful archive
- Add HabitArchivedSubscriber to Social Service that deletes the visibility_settings row
- The HABITS JetStream stream already captures habit.> so no new stream needed

## Quality Expectations
- Unit test: Habit Service publishes habit.archived on archive
- Integration test: Social Service subscriber deletes visibility row on receiving event
- Test: idempotent — re-archiving same habit doesn't fail
- Test: redelivery doesn't cause errors (DELETE WHERE is naturally idempotent)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Archiving a habit publishes a habit.archived event that triggers Social Service visibility cleanup
- Orphaned visibility rows are cleaned up within JetStream delivery window
- Tests cover the publish, subscribe, cleanup, and idempotent redelivery paths
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Additional context from backend audit 2026-03-13: The visibility.changed and friend.removed NATS subjects are defined in Contracts/Subjects.cs and published by Social Service, but NO service subscribes to them. The VISIBILITY and FRIENDS streams will accumulate unconsumed messages. ADR-002 specifies that the Activity Service should subscribe to both for async cleanup. This bead covers habit.archived; a separate bead may be needed for wiring the existing visibility.changed and friend.removed consumers into Activity Service.
