title: Port notifications module to Go
description:
Port notification-service to Go: notification storage/read-state, settings, device tokens, VAPID web push, and the fan-out logic. Ground truth: epic winzy.ai-rdc7 comment 1 (ENDPOINTS/NOTIFICATIONS, DATA MODEL notification_db, NOTIFICATIONS section, notification NATS consumers) + epic comment 2 PUSH section (client subscription flow). C# source services/notification-service/src is the spec.

HARD COMPATIBILITY CONSTRAINT: existing browsers hold push subscriptions signed against the CURRENT VAPID key pair. The Go service must use the SAME keys (values live only in Railway env: WebPush__Subject/PublicKey/PrivateKey - copied to new env names at cutover) or every user must re-subscribe. Library: verify current Go options (SherClockHolmes/webpush-go is the known candidate) against docs before implementing.

DATA MODEL (exact): Notification(UserId idx, Type string, Data jsonb, ReadAt?, IdempotencyKey? UNIQUE WHERE NOT NULL, PushDelivered bool); NotificationSettings(UserId unique, HabitReminders/FriendActivity/ChallengeUpdates bool default true); DeviceToken(UserId idx, Platform web_push/expo_push, Token text = the FULL web-push subscription JSON {endpoint,keys:{p256dh,auth}}, DeviceId?; unique(UserId,DeviceId) WHERE NOT NULL).

ENDPOINTS: GET /notifications?page&pageSize; PUT /notifications/{id}/read; PUT /notifications/read-all; GET /notifications/unread-count; PUT /notifications/settings; POST /notifications/devices (register: {platform:"web_push", token: subscription JSON, deviceId}) + DELETE (by deviceId); PUBLIC GET /notifications/vapid-public-key -> {publicKey}.

DELIVERY SEMANTICS (port faithfully): best-effort per token; HTTP 404/410 Gone from the push service -> delete that token; 429/transient -> keep; missing VAPID config -> skip delivery but KEEP tokens (log once). Push payload JSON consumed by frontend/public/sw.js: {title, body, icon, badge, url, tag}.

FAN-OUT (was HabitCompleted NATS subscriber, becomes a hook handler): resolve the actor's friends via the social package; filter out self + dedupe; drop friends with FriendActivity=false; idempotency key format "habit_completed:{friendId}:{actorId}:{habitId}:{date}" (unique index makes replays no-ops); batch-insert notifications; push with BOUNDED CONCURRENCY (the C# uses a semaphore of 10 - keep ~10 goroutines max); set PushDelivered after send; on a duplicate event, retry push ONLY where PushDelivered=false (partial-delivery recovery).
Other handlers (single-target, same idempotency + PushDelivered pattern): FriendRequestSent -> notify recipient; FriendRequestAccepted -> notify BOTH users; ChallengeCreated -> notify recipient; ChallengeCompleted -> notify user with reward text; UserDeleted -> delete the user's notifications, settings, device tokens.

TRANSACTIONAL CAUTION: hooks now run inside the caller's request (e.g. a habit completion). Notification INSERTS can share the transaction, but PUSH SENDS are network calls - do them after commit (or in a short-lived goroutine), never inside the tx. Document the chosen structure; a slow push service must not slow habit logging.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Web-push send verified against a real browser subscription in dev (manual step, PM will verify at review) + unit tests with a fake push server for 201/404/410/429 handling
- vapid-public-key endpoint shape {publicKey} exact
- Idempotency: replaying HabitCompleted creates no duplicate notifications; PushDelivered=false rows get push retried (tests)
- FriendActivity filter + self-filter tests; bounded concurrency verified structurally
- Push dispatch demonstrably outside the DB transaction
- Settings/read/unread-count/device register+unregister parity; device upsert by (UserId,DeviceId)
- UserDeleted cleanup test; happy/edge/error coverage per CLAUDE.md

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch.)
close_reason: Notifications module ported: storage/read-state/settings/devices, VAPID web push (same-keypair compatible via marknefedov/go-webpush/v2), fan-out with idempotency + PushDelivered retry, post-commit push dispatch. 2-fix review round + fresh-eyes fixes all verified; worker+PM full gates green. MANUAL AC outstanding: real-browser push verify at/before cutover.

1. MIGRATION 0007: notifications / notification_settings / device_tokens per the bead DATA MODEL, incl. idempotency_key UNIQUE WHERE NOT NULL and device (user_id, device_id) UNIQUE WHERE device_id IS NOT NULL partial indexes. Match C# snapshot types.

2. WEB PUSH — library decision is MADE (PM-verified via docs 2026-07-12): use github.com/marknefedov/go-webpush/v2 (NOT SherClockHolmes — the bead predates this verification). Key facts you rely on: `webpush.VAPIDKeys` unmarshals from web-push-compatible JSON `{"publicKey":"<base64url>","privateKey":"<base64url>"}` — EXACTLY our Railway env format, satisfying the HARD COMPATIBILITY CONSTRAINT (same keys, browsers keep their subscriptions); `client.Send` returns *webpush.PushServiceError with .SubscriptionExpired (404/410 → delete token) and .Temporary (429/5xx → keep); `webpush.Subscription` unmarshals the stored full subscription JSON; Config.MaxConcurrentSends bounds concurrency per origin. Config: VAPID_SUBJECT, VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY, VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY env vars in config.go (document the WebPush__Subject/PublicKey/PrivateKey → new-name mapping in a comment for the rdc7.10 cutover); ALL three missing → push disabled: skip sends, KEEP tokens, log once at startup (C# parity). Verify the library's current API against its docs as you implement — do not trust my snippets blindly.

3. TX STRUCTURE (prescribed — document it in code): notification INSERTS use the ctx querier (db.QuerierFrom — FriendRequestSent/Accepted and ChallengeCreated/Completed are emitted INSIDE caller transactions; HabitCompleted arrives post-commit outside any tx). PUSH SENDS never run inside the caller's call stack: after inserting, hand the new notification IDs to a detached delivery goroutine (context.Background + timeout ~30s) that (a) polls the POOL for those rows becoming visible (committed), a few retries over ~2s — if the caller's tx rolled back the rows never appear and the push is silently dropped; (b) loads the recipient's device tokens + settings; (c) sends with bounded concurrency ≤10; (d) sets push_delivered=true per notification after its sends; per-token outcome handling: SubscriptionExpired → DELETE token; Temporary → keep, no immediate retry (the PushDelivered=false retry on a future duplicate event is the recovery path — C# parity). This structure satisfies "push demonstrably outside the DB transaction" and is testable: the visibility-poll is skipped in tests that insert directly via the pool.

4. FAN-OUT (HabitCompleted handler): friends via social.FriendIDs; drop self + dedupe; drop friends with friend_activity=false settings (missing settings row = defaults all true); idempotency key "habit_completed:{friendId}:{actorId}:{habitId}:{date}"; batch insert; ON CONFLICT (idempotency_key) DO NOTHING → for rows that already existed, re-push ONLY where push_delivered=false (partial-delivery recovery per bead). Single-target handlers: FriendRequestSent → recipient; FriendRequestAccepted → BOTH; ChallengeCreated → recipient; ChallengeCompleted → user (reward text in payload); UserDeleted → delete notifications + settings + device tokens (uses ctx querier — cascade tx). Verify every notification Type string and Data payload field against the C# subscribers — the frontend reads these.

5. ENDPOINTS per bead: GET /notifications?page&pageSize, PUT /notifications/{id}/read, PUT /notifications/read-all, GET /notifications/unread-count, PUT /notifications/settings, POST+DELETE /notifications/devices, PUBLIC GET /notifications/vapid-public-key → {publicKey}. Conventions: three-way decodeJSON with per-endpoint C#-verified null behavior + exact error strings; habits.UTF16Len for any .Length parity; response shapes byte-compatible with C# (read Program.cs carefully). expo_push platform: accept + store as explicit NO-OP stub (PM addendum 1 — do not drop). Device register = upsert by (UserId, DeviceId).

6. WIRING (you own main.go): register service (events handlers + export section — verify the C# export literal from Program.cs and match it exactly, incl. ErrNoData-when-empty semantics vs the old 404), routes, and add "/notifications/vapid-public-key" to the publicRoutes allowlist. Mirror wiring into your package's testserver_integration_test.go per the social/challenges pattern (full middleware chain).

7. LOGGING (PM addendum 3): per-token outcome log (notification id, device id, status, kept/deleted, duration); fan-out summary (resolved/filtered/sent/failed counts); NEVER log subscription keys or full token JSON.

8. TESTS (happy/edge/error per CLAUDE.md): fake push server via httptest for 201/404-delete/410-delete/429-keep; idempotency replay (no duplicates + PushDelivered=false retried) against real Postgres; FriendActivity filter + self-filter; settings/read/read-all/unread-count/device upsert+delete parity; UserDeleted cleanup; bounded concurrency verified structurally (e.g. counting concurrent fake-server requests). Real-browser push is a MANUAL PM step at review — flag anything the PM needs for it (e.g. a dev VAPID keypair path).

9. GATES: package-scoped during dev; full repo gates + ONE integration run (-race -count=1 -p 1) only inside your granted slot; release the slot with per-package output.

10. REPORT on the thread: files per AC; C#/library receipts at file:line for every parity + API decision; the tx/push structure documented; deviations; open questions. Reservations released after report.

title: Port activity feed module to Go
description:
Port activity-service to Go: the friends activity feed. Ground truth: epic winzy.ai-rdc7 comment 1 (ENDPOINTS/ACTIVITY, DATA MODEL activity_db, activity NATS consumers). C# source services/activity-service/src is the spec.

PLANNED SIMPLIFICATION (intentional, documented here so nobody "restores" it): the C# stores ActorUsername/ActorDisplayName on each row and maintains them via 3 mechanisms (inline capture, lazy read-time enrichment through auth internal HTTP, and a 60s ActorNameBackfillJob). That machinery exists only because names lived in another service's DB. In the single database, DROP the name columns and the backfill job entirely - join users at read time. Fewer moving parts, names never stale. Consequence for winzy.ai-rdc7.9: migration does not carry the two name columns.

DATA MODEL (after simplification): FeedEntry(ActorId idx, EventType string, Data jsonb, IdempotencyKey? UNIQUE WHERE NOT NULL, DeletedAt? soft-delete; every read filters DeletedAt IS NULL - the C# uses a global query filter; replicate consistently, partial index on not-deleted).

ENDPOINT: GET /activity/feed?cursor&limit - cursor = CreatedAt ISO timestamp (keyset pagination); shows friends + self; VISIBILITY-FILTERED: an entry about a habit appears only if that habit is visible to the viewer (via the social package visibleHabits/friendship checks - read the C# feed query carefully for the exact filter semantics).

HOOK HANDLERS (were NATS consumers):
- UserRegistered -> feed entry
- HabitCreated, HabitCompleted -> feed entries (Data jsonb carries habit name etc. per C# payloads)
- FriendRequestAccepted -> TWO entries, one per user
- ChallengeCreated, ChallengeCompleted -> entries
- VisibilityChanged -> NARROWING (e.g. Friends->Private) soft-deletes that habit's entries; WIDENING restores them (the C# does this with raw JSONB SQL matching habitId inside Data - port the semantics, write it cleanly)
- FriendRemoved -> soft-delete the pair's friend.request.accepted entries
- UserDeleted -> HARD-delete the actor's entries AND entries referencing the user inside Data jsonb
All entry inserts use IdempotencyKey where the C# does (check per event type).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Keyset pagination exact (cursor in/out format identical to C# - the frontend feed.ts types are the contract)
- Visibility filtering tests: private habit entries invisible to friends; visibility narrowing hides existing entries, widening restores them
- FriendRemoved + UserDeleted cleanup semantics (soft vs hard) match C# (tests)
- Read-time name join returns username + displayName identical in shape to the old enriched response
- Idempotent inserts (replay = no duplicates)
- Happy/edge/error coverage per CLAUDE.md; port C# activity tests

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch.)
close_reason: Activity feed ported: simplified model (no name columns/backfill job — read-time auth.BatchProfiles join), exact feed batch loop + visibility filtering, all 9 handlers with JSONB SQL literal ports, soft/hard delete semantics. 2-fix review round (visibility cache merge, pair atomicity) + fresh-eyes fixes verified; scoped+PM gates green.

Integration tests per the rdc7.1 recipe (soft-delete filter, visibility narrowing/restore, and keyset pagination tested against real Postgres). Logging per convention; visibility-driven soft-delete/restore operations log affected-row counts.

Data-reference deletion); idempotent replay = no duplicates; name-join shape; soft-delete invisible everywhere. title: OpenAPI spec, typed TS client, frontend switchover
description:
COORDINATING PARENT for the frontend switchover. Split:
- winzy.ai-rdc7.8.1 - OpenAPI spec + typed contract behind the existing api facade
- winzy.ai-rdc7.8.2 - same-origin web serving + full frontend gates + E2E green against the Go stack

KEY STRATEGY (from the frontend ground-truth, epic comment 2): the frontend's ~190 Jest test files mock at the domain-module boundary (36 mock sites on frontend/src/api/*). Therefore we do NOT swap in a wholesale generated client - we keep every exported name/signature/return type of frontend/src/api/* stable and change only internals/types. Nearly all tests then pass untouched; only api/__tests__/client.test.ts is coupled to client internals.

This parent closes when both children are verified. After 8.x, the frontend runs fully against Go locally - everything beyond this point (9, 10) is data and ops.
close_reason: Both children verified and closed (8.1: 0470891; 8.2: this commit). Frontend runs fully against Go locally; facade untouched, 36 mock sites intact.

title: Data migration: 6 Railway Postgres into one database
description:
Migrate real user data from the six per-service Railway Postgres instances into the single new database. This is the owner's and friends' actual habit history - it must survive intact. Ground truth: epic winzy.ai-rdc7 comment 1 (DATA MODEL for all six DBs). Claude operates Railway via the CLI.

MIGRATION DECISIONS (made 2026-07-09, do not reopen without the PM):
- UUIDs are preserved verbatim - cross-service references (UserId in habits etc.) were logical FKs and become valid real references in one DB. Add actual FK constraints where the new schema defines them; validate referential integrity during load (orphans -> report, do not silently drop).
- refresh_tokens are NOT migrated. Consequence: every user re-logs-in once (web refresh cookie fails -> login screen). Acceptable for an MVP with ~5 users; massively simplifies the token story.
- Password hashes copy VERBATIM - format "base64(salt):base64(hash)" is identical in the Go implementation (winzy.ai-rdc7.2 acceptance criterion).
- Device tokens (push subscriptions) migrate as-is - they keep working because VAPID keys are reused (winzy.ai-rdc7.6/10).
- FeedEntry ActorUsername/ActorDisplayName columns are DROPPED (read-time join now - winzy.ai-rdc7.7); do not carry them.
- Jsonb payloads (Notification.Data, FeedEntry.Data, Challenge.ProcessedCompletionDates, Habit.CustomDays) carry byte-for-byte.
- Enum-as-string columns carry verbatim (values must match what the Go code expects - cross-check each enum against the Go schema).

TASKS
1. Export: pg_dump each of the six Railway Postgres services via railway CLI (document the exact commands; these dumps are also the rollback archive for winzy.ai-rdc7.10).
2. Transform + load script (repeatable, idempotent: drop/recreate target schema or truncate-and-load): map each service's tables into the single schema.
3. Verification report the script emits: per-table source vs target row counts; referential integrity check results; distinct-user counts match across modules.
4. Spot-check procedure (manual, PM-assisted): the owner's account end-to-end - habits, completion history, current flame values, promises, friends, witness links, challenges, notifications, push device rows.
5. FULL LOCAL REHEARSAL: run the entire export-transform-load against a local target before cutover; the flame golden-master (winzy.ai-rdc7.12) runs against this rehearsal dataset.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- One-command repeatable migration with an emitted verification report
- Zero unexplained row-count deltas; zero orphaned references (or each one explained and approved by the PM)
- Rehearsal completed locally; owner-account spot-check passes
- Dumps archived and documented as the rollback artifact
- Known .NET-era password hash logs in against the migrated local target (proves the auth chain end-to-end)

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch. Sequencing: runs after 8.x so the final schema is stable; feeds 12's golden-master; blocks 10.)
close_reason: All ACs met: one-command idempotent rehearse w/ verification report (all counts exact, zero orphans); dumps archived as rollback artifact; enum mapping identity-confirmed; owner spot-check PASSED (real login with .NET-era password against the rehearsal stack, data verified in real browser). The 'empty completion history' during the spot-check was a stale March-18 frontend dist served by the PM's test setup — resolved by fresh expo export, verified in-browser; NOT a migration defect. Worker: CloudyLantern.

comments.text:

PM has ALREADY completed task 1: all six production DBs are dumped (pg_dump 18, custom format + schema-only SQL + source row counts) at ~/work/side-projects/winzy-data-archive/2026-07-12_1945/ — READ-ONLY for you: never modify, never delete, and NEVER copy any of it into the repo (real user data). Railway production Postgres is version 18.4 (relevant for cutover, noted for rdc7.10).

FACTS RESOLVED SINCE THE BEAD WAS WRITTEN:
- Habit.CustomDays jsonb = INT arrays (DayOfWeek ordinals, sunday=0) — confirmed on winzy.ai-h0ne by reading the C# EF config. No day-name rewrite.
- Go's request decode now accepts both C# enum forms (h0ne), but STORED enum-string COLUMNS may differ in CASE between stacks (C# enum ToString = PascalCase e.g. "Full"/"Daily"/"Active"; check what the Go schema/code expects, e.g. habits.completionKindFromDB). Read the actual dump data (restore first), not just code.

STEPS
1. Everything you create lives in tools/migrate/ (new dir; Node or Go — pick what tools/parity chose if reasonable, justify briefly). Reserve tools/migrate/** (reason=winzy.ai-rdc7.9).
2. RESTORE sources: create logical DBs winzy_mig_src_{auth,habit,social,challenge,notification,activity} on the winzy-db instance (localhost:5439, user winzy) and pg_restore each dump (postgres:18-alpine client via docker run — version matters). Never touch databases winzy or winzy_parity.

TARGET: create winzy_rehearsal on the same instance; apply the Go schema by running backend migrations against it (goose/golang-migrate — check backend/internal/db for the mechanism; a tiny cmd in tools/migrate may invoke the backend's migration runner as a library, or run cmd/api once with a throwaway config pointed at winzy_rehearsal and kill it — choose the least hacky, document it).
4. TRANSFORM+LOAD (one command, idempotent: truncate-target-then-load): map each source table -> target schema per the bead's decisions: UUIDs verbatim; NO refresh_tokens; password hashes verbatim; device tokens as-is; feed_entries WITHOUT actor name columns; jsonb payloads byte-for-byte; enum columns via your mapping table; timestamps timestamptz verbatim. Add real FK constraint validation: load order parents-first, orphans REPORTED not dropped (fail the run with a report section).
5. VERIFICATION REPORT (emitted by the tool, one file): per-table source vs target row counts (cross-check against source-row-counts.txt in the archive — expected: users=6, habits=10, completions=63, promises=0, friendships=2, social_preferences=1, visibility_settings=10, witness_links=1, witness_link_habits=2, challenges=1, notifications=61, device_tokens=0, notification_settings=0, feed_entries=92; refresh_tokens=169 deliberately NOT migrated); referential integrity results; distinct-user counts per module vs auth users.
6. AUTH CHAIN PROOF: after load, run a targeted check that a known .NET-era password hash verifies through the Go code path (a tiny Go test/cmd in tools/migrate that calls backend/internal/auth's verify with the real stored hash of the owner account and a PLACEHOLDER password — DO NOT guess passwords; instead verify format parseability + argon2id params match, and leave the actual login to the owner spot-check). Report the hash format audit for all 6 users.
7. DB DISCIPLINE: message the PM for a GO before creating any database on the winzy-db instance (other workers hold windows). Steps 1 + code + mapping table need no DB.
8. Report: files, enum mapping table, verification report content, exact rerun commands, deviations, open questions. Standard gates for whatever language tools/migrate uses. Never commit/push/close beads/br writes.

OUT OF SCOPE: the flame golden-master (rdc7.12 Part 2, other worker), cutover itself (rdc7.10), restoring into the old compose stack (Part 2 orchestration, PM-coordinated later).

title: Add double-tap prevention on notification press in NotificationScreen
description: Rapid double-tap on a notification fires handlePress twice. First tap marks read and navigates via overlay.push. Second tap pushes duplicate overlay entry — user needs to press back twice. The markRead API is idempotent so no data corruption, but the overlay stack gets a duplicate. Add debounce or disable-on-first-tap guard in NotificationScreen handlePress. Found by React expert review in Wave 11.
close_reason: Fixed in Wave 11 — added debounce guard in NotificationScreen handlePress

title: Authentication
description:
Auth Service, auth screens, username model, and session bootstrap behavior. This epic is a coordination container, not a direct implementation bead.

## Critical Child Sequence
- Backend auth/token/username contract (`winzy.ai-dhq`)
- Frontend sign-in/sign-up/session UX (`winzy.ai-m69`)
- Downstream social/public features rely on the username and session model defined here

## Done Means
- Child issues are linked and kept current.
- Registration/login/refresh work end-to-end with the intended web/native token behavior.
- Username rules are dependable enough for public profiles and friend discovery.

## Success Criteria
- The relevant child issues are linked and kept current.
- See this epic's description above for the intended child sequence and done state.
close_reason: All child work complete; closing epic shell.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Audit 2026-03-13: All children closed except winzy.ai-rt9.1 (legacy localStorage refresh token cleanup, in_progress P2). This epic is very close to done. Once rt9.1 is resolved, this epic can be closed.

title: Web auth hardening still leaves legacy refresh tokens readable in localStorage
description:
The recent web auth fix stopped writing new refresh tokens to localStorage, but it does not actively clear old winzy_refresh_token values that may already exist from previous sessions. On web, refresh/bootstrap logic can still read that legacy token until logout or manual storage clearing, which weakens the intended XSS mitigation for upgraded users.

## Scope
- Add an explicit cleanup/migration path for legacy web refresh tokens.
- Preserve the intended session model: access token in memory, refresh token in HttpOnly cookie on web.
- Add regression coverage for users upgrading from the pre-fix storage behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Legacy web refresh tokens are cleared or ignored safely during auth/session bootstrap.
- Web refresh logic no longer depends on a stale localStorage refresh token being present.
- Tests cover the upgrade/migration path, not just fresh installs.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

title: Auth Playwright happy-path sign-in test can pass when authentication is broken
description:
The existing Playwright test for signing in with known credentials treats either a successful authenticated landing or a visible server-error banner as a passing outcome. That turns the intended happy-path test into a false-green smoke check and can hide regressions in real login behavior, seeded-user setup, cookie/session handling, or backend availability.

## Scope
- Make the existing-credentials auth journey assert real authentication success.
- Keep detailed step logging so failures remain diagnosable in CI.
- If backend preconditions are required, make them explicit instead of silently accepting the error path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The happy-path sign-in test fails when the user cannot actually authenticate.
- Backend/data prerequisites for the test are explicit and reproducible.
- The resulting logs still make auth failures easy to diagnose in CI.
close_reason: Fixed false-green: replaced Promise.race that accepted server-error as passing with strict assertions requiring Today screen visibility and no server-error.

title: Web auth session model still violates the intended cookie-plus-memory design
description:
Current web auth still breaks the promised security model in multiple ways: auth responses expose refresh tokens to JavaScript, access tokens are persisted in localStorage instead of memory, and logout can clear local state while the real HttpOnly-cookie session remains alive on network failure.

Scope:
- Remove refresh tokens from web-visible JSON response paths
- Restore the intended in-memory-only web access-token model
- Make logout truthfully terminate the web session or surface failure clearly
- Reconcile tracker comments/closed auth beads with the actual implemented model
close_reason: Fixed in commit 6c2d903 — cookie-plus-memory auth session model

title: Auth and gateway basics still have startup and route-policy verification gaps
description:
The auth/gateway path is materially improved but still under-proven in places that matter to trust: auth-service does not fail fast on weak or placeholder JWT secrets, and the gateway test suite still does not prove key YARP route/auth policy behavior promised by the closed gateway testing bead.

Scope:
- Add fail-fast JWT secret validation to auth-service startup
- Verify key gateway auth-exemption and protected-route mappings in tests
- Tighten cookie-path/security assertions where the session model depends on them
close_reason: Added fail-fast JWT secret validation to auth-service TokenService (rejects <32 char, empty, placeholder secrets at startup). Added RouteAuthPolicyTests to gateway verifying YARP route/auth policy behavior: public routes (register, login, refresh, habits/public, vapid-key) pass without JWT, protected routes (habits, social, challenges, notifications, activity, auth/profile) return 401 without JWT, valid JWTs pass through, expired/wrong-key/garbage tokens are rejected, route ordering is correct.

title: Setup Husky pre-commit hooks
description:
Configure Husky to run quality checks before commits and pushes in the mono-repo.

## Setup
- [ ] Install husky: npm install -D husky
- [ ] Initialize husky: npx husky init
- [ ] Add pre-commit hook
- [ ] Add pre-push hook

## Pre-commit Checks (fast, <10 seconds)
- [ ] Frontend: Run ESLint on staged files (lint-staged)
- [ ] Backend: Run dotnet format --verify-no-changes on staged .cs files

## Pre-push Checks (thorough)
- [ ] Frontend: npx tsc --noEmit + npm test
- [ ] Backend: dotnet build + dotnet test on changed service projects
- [ ] Fail push if any test fails

## Configuration
- [ ] Configure lint-staged for fast pre-commit checks
- [ ] Scope pre-push checks to changed services only (detect changed paths)
- [ ] Skip hooks with --no-verify when needed (document in CLAUDE.md)

## Notes
- Pre-commit = fast formatting/lint (catches typos before they enter history)
- Pre-push = full test suite (catches bugs before they reach remote)
- No code reaches the remote without passing tests

(Originally GH RewardStack #34)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
Pre-commit and pre-push hooks run the intended fast-vs-thorough checks without surprising contributors or duplicating unrelated CI logic.
Changed-path scoping, skip guidance, and local setup/documentation are clear enough that the hooks help rather than block routine work.
The hook scripts themselves are tested or validated well enough that failures are diagnosable and reproducible.
close_reason: done

title: Parallelize push delivery in HabitCompletedSubscriber for 100+ friend fan-out
description: Push delivery in HabitCompletedSubscriber is sequential — each Web Push call takes ~100ms. With 100+ friends, total delivery time exceeds 10s, risking NATS message timeout and redelivery. Use Task.WhenAll with SemaphoreSlim concurrency limit (e.g., 10) for parallel push delivery, or fan out to a separate push delivery queue. Not a problem at current scale but will be as user base grows. Found by Architecture + .NET expert review in Wave 11.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 12

description:
Tooling, coding guidelines, CI/CD, and testing infrastructure. This epic is a coordination container, not a direct implementation bead.

## Critical Child Sequence

## Done Means

## Success Criteria

title: Frontend and agent verification workflows still overstate what is actually proven
description:
Some of the project verification story is still less truthful than the tracker claims: frontend CI no longer builds the web app even though the closed CI setup bead promised it, and auth-dependent E2E bootstrap can fail open into an empty storage state instead of failing loudly.

Scope:
- Restore or explicitly narrow frontend CI build verification
- Make auth setup fail loudly when bootstrap/authentication is broken
- Reconcile closed workflow/testing beads with what the workflows and tests actually prove today
close_reason: Completed in Wave 8 (PR #118)

title: Concurrent br writes during multi-agent audit can corrupt the local tracker DB
description:
During the basics-hardening audit, concurrent br writes from multiple agents corrupted the local SQLite tracker DB and forced a rebuild from JSONL. The current project workflow assumes several br operations are safe per teammate, so this needs a documented or technical fix.

Scope:
- Reproduce and document the multi-agent write pattern that corrupts the DB
- Define the safe tracker-write workflow for agent teams, including recovery expectations
- Add whichever lock, serialization, or operational guard is needed so audit/review workflows do not risk tracker data integrity
close_reason: Trigger (multi-agent concurrent br writes) no longer used; single-agent br is serial and safe. Reopen if agent teams resume.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Fresh-eyes follow-up: after the concurrent-write corruption and DB rebuild, a forced `br sync --flush-only --force` exported 224 issues from a DB that still contained 225 issue rows and silently dropped `winzy.ai-rt9.2` from `.beads/issues.jsonl`. I manually restored that record from backup and re-imported JSONL to get DB/JSONL back to 225/225. This bead should capture not only the concurrent-write corruption trigger, but also the unsafe/stale export behavior that can follow during recovery.

title: Flame component animations still flood frontend tests with act() warnings
description:
Targeted Jest runs for the public and social screens pass, but rendering `Flame` logs repeated React `act(...)` warnings from the animation setup path, which makes green suites noisy and reduces confidence in the test signal.

## Evidence
- `Flame.tsx`'s animation effect calls `pulseAnim.setValue(1)` during test renders
- targeted screen suites emit repeated console errors about updates not wrapped in `act(...)`
- the warnings are broad enough to hide real console noise in otherwise passing runs

## User impact
- verification output becomes harder to trust and harder to review quickly
- future regressions can be buried under known warning spam
- the project's "tests tell the truth" goal is weakened even when assertions still pass

## Scope
- eliminate or intentionally suppress the animation-driven `act(...)` warnings in test environments without muting real console errors
- preserve runtime Flame behavior in the actual app
- codify the chosen reduced-motion, animation-mocking, or test-helper strategy so the warnings do not silently return

## Root cause
The Flame component's animation setup still runs in Jest without a test-friendly animation strategy, so otherwise harmless visual setup is reported as console-error noise.
acceptance_criteria:
Frontend test runs that render `Flame` no longer emit repeated `act(...)` warnings from its animation setup.

## Verification
- The targeted public and social screen suites run cleanly without the current warning spam
- Design-system or screen-level regression coverage keeps the test-only Flame behavior intentional and documented
- The fix does not globally suppress unrelated `console.error` output that should still fail or alert the team
notes: This is a verification-quality issue rather than a product-runtime bug, so the fix should stay narrow: clean test output without weakening real runtime behavior or muting useful failures.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 9 (PR #119)

title: Activity Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Activity Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] GET /feed — Own activity feed (paginated, friends only)

## Activity Record Model (activity_db)
- feed_entries table: id, actor_id, event_type, data (jsonb), created_at
- Backend stores structured activity types/data; frontend surfaces own the final rendered sentences/copy.

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] user.registered → create a structured joined activity event when in scope
- [ ] habit.created → create a structured new-habit activity event
- [ ] habit.completed → create a structured habit-completed activity event
- [ ] friend.request.accepted → create a structured friendship activity event
- [ ] challenge.created → create a structured challenge-created activity event
- [ ] challenge.completed → create a structured challenge-completed activity event
- [ ] user.deleted → delete feed entries owned by or no longer safely visible because of that user

## Feed Logic
- [ ] Only show entries from friends (query Social Service for friend list)
- [ ] Respect habit visibility settings
- [ ] Paginate with cursor-based pagination
- [ ] Any cached/denormalized friend or visibility data must invalidate safely on friend removal and visibility changes

## Notes
- Feed is read-heavy, write-seldom (events trickle in)
- Consider denormalization for performance, but keep privacy boundaries explicit and testable
acceptance_criteria:
Structured activity ingestion, privacy-aware feed composition, and cleanup behavior work end-to-end without moving final presentation copy into the backend.
Tests cover visibility enforcement, friend-removal/visibility-change invalidation, event-handler idempotency, retry/poison-message behavior, and pagination/filtering edge cases.
Structured logs include subject, actor/user IDs, feed entry IDs/types, invalidation path, and failure reason so event-processing and feed bugs are diagnosable.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] GET /feed returns correct, paginated structured results
- [ ] Supported activity events create structured feed entries with the expected data contract
- [ ] Only friend activity is shown, and visibility changes/friend removal invalidate stale visibility safely
- [ ] Cursor-based pagination works correctly
- [ ] user.deleted cleanup removes or hides no-longer-valid feed data safely
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests cover the feed endpoint plus event-driven activity ingestion with Testcontainers
- [ ] Unit tests cover feed filtering, denormalization/invalidation, and retry/idempotency decisions
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage
close_reason: Activity Service complete with visibility filtering and subscriber idempotency. 28/28 tests pass.

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Implementation complete. All 22 tests pass (3 health checks, 11 feed endpoint tests, 8 subscriber tests). Quality gates clean: build 0 warnings, format verified. Awaiting Social Service (winzy.ai-bdd) to unblock claiming.

title: Public Flame Page (winzy.ai/@username)
description:
A public URL per user that shows their flame(s) with zero-friction viewing. This is passive accountability and a shareable landing page at the same time.

## Core Concept
- Each user gets a public profile route: winzy.ai/@username
- Shows only habits explicitly marked public through the Social visibility model
- Displays current flame state and consistency context for public habits
- Beautiful, mobile-optimized, fast page with no viewer login required

## Public Profile Page
- [ ] Route: /@{username} (frontend)
- [ ] User display name/avatar plus public habit list and flame states
- [ ] Clear CTA to create your own account/flame
- [ ] Responsive, cache-friendly presentation that still reflects visibility changes quickly enough

## Embeddable Flame Badge
- [ ] Route: /api/flame/{username}.svg (backend)
- [ ] Dynamic SVG for aggregate/public flame state
- [ ] Suitable for bios, blogs, Notion, and similar embeds
- [ ] Links back to the public profile page

## Data Contract
- [ ] Frontend page consumes a dedicated exact-username public flame contract rather than fuzzy search.
- [ ] Backend public endpoint remains `GET /habits/public/{username}` but must be backed by exact username resolution and visibility-aware composition/projection.
- [ ] Social Service remains the source of truth for public visibility.
- [ ] Rate limiting, caching, and anti-scraping/privacy considerations are explicit in the implementation plan.

## Verification
- [ ] Playwright coverage for public access, unknown usernames, and visibility changes propagating to the page.
- [ ] Tests/documentation make the public/private boundary very clear.

## Notes
- This page is both a product feature and marketing surface.
- Username must be unique and URL-safe; the auth flow should make that dependable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
The public flame page and embeddable badge work without viewer auth while respecting exact username lookup and public-only visibility rules.
Tests cover happy-path public viewing, bad usernames, CTA routing into auth, and visibility/cache propagation behavior with privacy-safe freshness expectations.
Rate limiting, caching, and public/private-boundary decisions are explicit and logged well enough that regressions are catchable before release.
close_reason: Public flame page at /@username implemented. Public endpoint, E2E spec, RootNavigator integration.

title: habit-service + auth-service error handling hardening
description: Tech debt audit found: (1) habit-service/Program.cs: 3 request body ReadFromJsonAsync calls with no JsonException catch (lines 62, 131, 201) → malformed JSON returns unhandled 500. (2) habit-service/Program.cs: 4 service-to-service ReadFromJsonAsync calls catching HttpRequestException|TaskCanceledException but missing JsonException (lines 544/550, 576/582, 643/649, 673/679). (3) auth-service/Endpoints/AuthEndpoints.cs: 1 service-to-service ReadFromJsonAsync missing JsonException in export fan-out (line 406/419). (4) habit-service/Program.cs: 3 NATS publish catch(Exception) blocks that should narrow to NATS-specific types (lines 89, 184, 266). (5) auth-service/Endpoints/AuthEndpoints.cs: 1 NATS publish catch(Exception) block (line 85). Add JsonException catches, narrow NATS catches, add tests for each fix.

Frontend screen for the social activity feed. Backend is covered by Activity Service.

## Quality Expectations

- [ ] At least one Playwright journey exercises feed loading and avatar navigation with detailed step logging.

The issue depends explicitly on the friend-profile surface it links to.

title: smoke test
description: delete me

title: Improve deriveDeviceId hash to use Web Crypto SHA-256
description: The current deriveDeviceId in frontend/src/utils/push.ts uses a 32-bit JS hash (bitwise | 0). Birthday-problem collisions become probable around ~65k endpoints per user. Replace with crypto.subtle.digest('SHA-256', ...) and take first 12 hex chars (48 bits). Low priority -- collision requires 65k+ devices per user which is unrealistic, but trivial to fix. Flagged by R6 review.
close_reason: Merged in PR #113 (Wave 4)

comments.author: Gabko14
comments.text: Audit 2026-03-13: This is a P4 trivial fix (replace 32-bit JS hash with crypto.subtle.digest SHA-256). Has been in_progress for 2 days. Should take <15 minutes. Either do it now or move back to open so it doesn't block agent capacity calculations.

title: Optimize usePendingFriendCount polling — add dedicated count endpoint
description: Hook polls GET /social/friends/requests every 30s and counts incoming.length. Over-fetches full request objects. Add a lightweight GET /social/friends/requests/count endpoint and use it instead.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 6, merged in PR #115

title: Track push delivery state to prevent duplicate pushes on NATS redelivery
description: HabitCompletedSubscriber (and FriendRequestSentSubscriber) re-run DeliverAsync on duplicate idempotency key detection. If a message is redelivered after a successful push+ack was lost, the user gets a duplicate push notification. Consider adding a PushDelivered flag to the Notification entity and skipping DeliverAsync when already delivered. Found by Codex review in Wave 10 PR #120.
close_reason: Completed in Wave 11 — PushDelivered flag prevents duplicate pushes on redelivery

title: Wire PushDeliveryService into notification subscribers
description: PushDeliveryService exists and handles Web Push delivery, but none of the notification NATS subscribers (FriendRequestSentSubscriber, ChallengeCompletedSubscriber, etc.) call it. They only create in-app notifications. Wire DeliverAsync() into each subscriber after creating the in-app notification so users actually receive push notifications on their devices.
close_reason: Wired PushDeliveryService.DeliverAsync() into all 4 notification-creating subscribers: FriendRequestSentSubscriber, FriendRequestAcceptedSubscriber, ChallengeCreatedSubscriber, ChallengeCompletedSubscriber. Push delivery is called after in-app notification is saved. HabitCompleted and UserDeleted subscribers not wired (no notification creation).

title: Count-based challenge progress is not idempotent for redelivered habit.completed events
description:
`HabitCompletedSubscriber` increments `CompletionCount++` for `DaysInPeriod` and `TotalCompletions` without deduplicating the specific completion being processed. Under JetStream's at-least-once delivery, a redelivered `habit.completed` event can increment the same challenge twice and overstate progress or complete a challenge early.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Count-based challenge progress is idempotent when the same completion event is redelivered.
- Tests cover a redelivery/retry path and prove `CompletionCount` does not inflate.
- The deduplication key or approach is explicit enough to maintain safely.
close_reason: CompletionCount tracked via idempotent processed-dates JSON set

title: Habit Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Habit Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /habits — Create new habit
- [ ] GET /habits — List own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/{id} — Habit details
- [ ] PUT /habits/{id} — Update habit
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id} — Delete/archive habit
- [ ] POST /habits/{id}/complete — Mark habit complete (body: { date?: string, timezone: string } — timezone REQUIRED to resolve 'today' correctly for the user's locale)
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id}/completions/{date} — Remove a completion (for corrections via calendar view)
- [ ] GET /habits/{id}/stats — Consistency stats (60-day rolling window)
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} — Internal: habits for a user (called by Social Service)
- [ ] GET /habits/public/{username} — Public flame data for shareable profiles, backed by exact username resolution plus visibility-aware read logic

## Database Schema (habit_db)
- habits table: id, user_id, name, icon, color, frequency, custom_days, created_at, archived_at
- completions table: id, habit_id, user_id, completed_at, local_date, note

## Consistency Algorithm
- [ ] 60-day rolling window calculation
- [ ] Handle edge cases: new habits, weekly frequency, custom days
- [ ] Return percentage 0-100
- [ ] All window boundary calculations use the user's supplied IANA timezone

## Timezone Handling
- completions.completed_at stored as timestamptz (UTC) in DB
- completions.local_date stores the user's local calendar date when the completion was logged
- Backend derives 'today' and 60-day window start from the user's supplied timezone, not server UTC
- Prevents: user in UTC-5 logging at 10 PM being counted as the next day

## Cross-Service Responsibilities
- [ ] Habit Service owns habit data and consistency calculations.
- [ ] Social Service owns visibility rules and privacy defaults; Habit Service must not become the source of truth for visibility state.
- [ ] Public flame reads can use an internal composition/projection, but they must respect Auth username identity and Social visibility rules.

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish habit.created { userId, habitId, name }
- [ ] Publish habit.completed { userId, habitId, date, consistency }
- [ ] Subscribe user.deleted → delete all habits and completions for userId

## Authorization
- [ ] user_id from X-User-Id header (set by Gateway)
- [ ] Users can only access their own habits
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} is internal-only (no auth check, trusted network)

## Notes
- Core service of the application
- Consistency calculation is the heart of the product (replaces streaks)

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
Habit creation, completion, stats, and deletion flows in the issue description work end-to-end with correct timezone handling.
Tests cover consistency math, timezone boundaries, internal/public endpoint contracts, and event-handler behavior including idempotency or safe retry handling where applicable.
Structured logs make habit-command handling and event processing diagnosable by subject, entity IDs, and failure reason.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 9 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] 60-day rolling window consistency calculation works for daily, weekly, custom
- [ ] Edge cases handled: new habits, habits started today, weekly frequency
- [ ] habit.created and habit.completed events publish via NATS
- [ ] GET /habits/user/{userId} internal endpoint works (for Social Service)
- [ ] DELETE /habits/{id}/completions/{date} removes completion and recalculates consistency
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests for every endpoint (Testcontainers)
- [ ] Unit tests for consistency algorithm (all edge cases)
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage
close_reason: done

title: Habit detail consistency can show zero despite completions
description: Habit detail can display logged completions in the calendar while the consistency/flame summary shows 0%/no flame. Investigate whether the rolling consistency calculation, frontend mapping, or stats endpoint is using a mismatched date/timezone/window.
close_reason: Fixed consistency calculation so completed today and backfilled completions inside the rolling window contribute to flame stats.

title: NATS subscriber transient JetStream errors can stop deployed services
description: Railway staging exposed that a transient NATS/JetStream 503 during NATS redeploy can bubble out of NatsEventSubscriber background services and stop the ASP.NET host. Social-service shut down after 'Consumer appears to be deleted after 10 consecutive 503 errors' while the rest of staging was otherwise healthy. Harden subscriber reconnect/recreate behavior or host exception handling so transient broker restarts do not permanently stop services.
close_reason: Superseded by Go backend rewrite epic winzy.ai-rdc7 (decided 2026-07-09): the .NET/NATS/multi-Postgres topology this targets is being replaced by a single Go service + one Postgres. Old stack stays live only until cutover (winzy.ai-rdc7.10); interim risk accepted for a friends-only MVP.

title: Activity Service feed has N+1 HTTP calls for visibility
description: The GET /activity/feed endpoint makes sequential HTTP calls to Social Service for each friend to fetch visibility data. With N friends, that's N sequential HTTP requests with 5-second timeouts. Fix by either: (a) making calls concurrent with Task.WhenAll, (b) adding a batch endpoint to Social Service, or (c) caching visibility data.
close_reason: Fixed: replaced sequential foreach loop with Task.WhenAll for concurrent HTTP calls to social-service for friend visibility data.

title: Split backend route monoliths into readable endpoint modules
description:
Service-by-service backend readability pass that turns oversized `Program.cs` files into clear bootstraps plus cohesive endpoint modules.

The goal is faster navigation and smaller hotspot files, not blind uniformity. Work one service at a time with reviewable diffs, and only split where the result is actually easier to read.

Suggested order:
1. social-service
2. habit-service
3. challenge-service
4. activity-service and notification-service if the split clearly helps
5. auth-service polish only if it meaningfully improves readability
6. gateway only if a cleanup keeps it simpler while preserving its independence

What good looks like:
- `Program.cs` shows bootstrap, service registrations, hosted services, and a readable route map
- route handlers move into domain-based endpoint files instead of living in one dumping ground
- obvious service-local helper duplication is reduced during the move
- shared helpers from `winzy.ai-i0a9` are adopted where they make call sites clearer
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The largest backend hotspot services are materially easier to scan, especially social-service, habit-service, and challenge-service.
- [ ] `Program.cs` files act as readable route indexes and no longer hide most of the service logic.
- [ ] Endpoint files are grouped by domain responsibility rather than arbitrary line slicing.
- [ ] Shared helpers from `winzy.ai-i0a9` are applied where they simplify the code, not by rote.
- [ ] Gateway remains independent from Winzy.Common.
- [ ] Backend builds and tests still pass after the refactor.
notes:
## Guardrails
- Do not force every service into the exact same folder layout.
- Do not split inseparable logic into tiny files just to hit a line-count target.
- Prefer a cohesive 220-line endpoint file over four 60-line files that scatter context.
- Keep feature work out of this bead unless a bug is discovered during the refactor.

## Real hotspots today
- `services/social-service/src/Program.cs`
- `services/habit-service/src/Program.cs`
- `services/challenge-service/src/Program.cs`

## Nice secondary wins
- Reduce duplicate service-local helper logic such as repeated profile or visibility plumbing.
- Make it obvious from the top-level file where each route family lives.
close_reason: Committed in Wave 13 — 3 services split into endpoint modules

title: Notifications screen is a navigation dead end in the authenticated shell
description:
The notifications bell now opens NotificationScreen from RootNavigator, but the screen provides no back/home/profile navigation and RootNavigator does not render any escape hatch while `screen === "notifications"`. Users can reach the inbox but cannot return to Today without reloading the app.

## Evidence
- frontend/src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx switches to `screen === "notifications"` and renders NotificationScreen without an `onBack` prop or any surrounding shell controls.
- frontend/src/components/notifications/NotificationScreen.tsx has no back button or navigation affordance of its own.

## Impact
This regresses the authenticated shell into a dead-end route and undermines the goal of wiring notifications into the real app navigation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Users can return from notifications to the main authenticated surfaces without a page reload.
- [ ] At least one Playwright journey covers opening the inbox and navigating back out through the shipped UI.
- [ ] The navigation model remains consistent with the existing RootNavigator state machine or a deliberate replacement.
close_reason: Fix verified in code: RootNavigator.tsx:155-163 passes onBack={goToHome} to NotificationScreen, and NotificationScreen.tsx:82-90 renders a back button header when onBack is provided. Users can navigate back from notifications.

title: Extract proven shared frontend building blocks
description:
Create the small set of frontend building blocks that clearly earn reuse by shrinking hotspot screens and removing repeated UI patterns.

This bead should stay selective. The goal is not to invent a mini framework or centralize every style decision; the goal is to extract the components and helpers that make the next screen-refactor bead easier and cleaner.

Primary candidates:
- `ScreenHeader` for repeated back-button and title layouts
- `InlineError` for inline banner-style errors
- `Avatar` for initials-in-circle renderings, backed by `getInitials`
- `HabitFlameRow` for repeated habit plus flame cards
- `OverlayShell` for the repeated `OfflineIndicator` plus `ErrorBoundary` plus `StatusBar` wrapper in `RootNavigator`
- `DataGuard` only if a real implementation stays simpler than the existing per-screen guards across multiple screens
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] A small set of shared frontend building blocks is created and unit tested.
- [ ] Each abstraction has multiple real adoption targets and clearly improves readability or duplication in the next bead.
- [ ] New exports are wired where appropriate, especially for design-system-level components.
- [ ] The bead does not turn into a broad screen rewrite; it prepares the pieces for the next task.
- [ ] Frontend quality gates still pass.
notes:
## Guardrails
- Prefer components and tiny helpers over a generic style or theme abstraction dump.
- Mass replacements such as swapping every `lightTheme` usage to a new hook are not the goal unless they directly simplify the hotspot files.
- If a proposed shared abstraction hides too much screen-specific behavior, keep that logic local instead.

## Good output
A good outcome is a handful of obvious, well-tested building blocks that the next bead can apply with confidence to large screens and repeated UI patterns.
close_reason: Committed in Wave 13

title: Shared NATS subscriber consumes entire wildcard streams instead of filtering to one subject
description:
`shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/JetStreamSetup.cs` creates broad streams (`user.>`, `habit.>`, `friend.>`, `challenge.>`), while `shared/Winzy.Common/Messaging/NatsEventSubscriber.cs` creates consumers without configuring `FilterSubject`/`FilterSubjects`. JetStream therefore delivers every subject in that stream to the consumer. A subscriber intended for `user.deleted` on the `USERS` stream would also receive `user.registered`; a `habit.completed` subscriber on `HABITS` would also receive `habit.created`.

This is not only a routing bug. It sits in the same subscriber surface area that previously needed resilience fixes, so the final plan must ensure wrong-subject or incompatible payloads are handled safely and logged clearly rather than killing the consumer loop.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a subscriber derived from `NatsEventSubscriber<T>` for one event type on a wildcard stream such as `USERS`.
2. Publish a different event on another subject within the same stream (for example `user.registered`).
3. Observe the consumer still receives that message because no subject filter was configured.

## Notes
- This blocks every service that relies on subject-specific JetStream consumers.
- The fix should verify both routing correctness and failure isolation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- See structured acceptance criteria field on this issue for the detailed done criteria.
acceptance_criteria:
`NatsEventSubscriber` requires/configures an explicit subject filter for the intended event subject(s).
Wrong-subject or incompatible payload deliveries are logged and isolated safely rather than killing the subscriber loop.
At least one real JetStream integration test proves a consumer such as `user.deleted` ignores `user.registered` while the consumer stays healthy.
close_reason: done

title: Challenge Service Implementation
description:
Implement the Challenge Service (.NET Minimal API + PostgreSQL).

## Endpoints
- [ ] POST /challenges — Create challenge for a friend
- [ ] GET /challenges — List own challenges (sent + received)
- [ ] GET /challenges/{id} — Challenge details + progress
- [ ] PUT /challenges/{id}/claim — Mark reward as claimed
- [ ] DELETE /challenges/{id} — Cancel challenge

## Database Schema (challenge_db)
- challenges table: id, habit_id, creator_id, recipient_id, milestone_type, target_value, period_days, reward_description, status, created_at, ends_at, completed_at

## NATS Subscriptions
- [ ] Subscribe to habit.completed
- [ ] Subscribe to user.deleted → cancel all challenges involving userId
- [ ] On event: find active challenges for this habit_id
- [ ] Calculate progress toward milestone
- [ ] If milestone reached: set status=completed, publish challenge.completed

## NATS Events
- [ ] Publish challenge.created { challengeId, fromUser, toUser, habitId }
- [ ] Publish challenge.completed { challengeId, userId, reward }

## Notes
- Rewards are shared experiences (coffee, hiking) — NOT material gifts
- No failed state — challenges expire gracefully
- Challenge creator must be a friend of the recipient (validate via Social Service)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The scope described above is complete end-to-end.
- [ ] Tests, documentation, and configuration updates required by this work are included.
- [ ] Relevant quality gates pass for the changed areas.
acceptance_criteria:
Challenge creation, progress calculation, completion, and cleanup behavior in the issue description work end-to-end.
Tests cover milestone evaluation, event-handler idempotency, safe retry/poison-message behavior, and challenge completion edge cases.
Structured logs make challenge command handling and habit-event processing diagnosable by subject, challenge ID, habit ID, and failure reason.
notes:
## Done When
- [ ] All 5 endpoints return correct status codes
- [ ] NATS subscription to habit.completed works
- [ ] Progress calculation correct for consistency_target milestone type
- [ ] Automatic status update to completed when milestone reached
- [ ] challenge.created and challenge.completed events publish via NATS
- [ ] Friendship validation via Social Service before creating challenge
- [ ] Challenges expire gracefully (no failed state)
- [ ] GET /health responds with DB + NATS status
- [ ] Dockerfile builds and runs in Docker Compose
- [ ] Integration tests for every endpoint (Testcontainers)
- [ ] Unit tests for progress calculation and milestone detection
- [ ] 80%+ code coverage
close_reason: Challenge Service fully implemented: 5 endpoints, NATS subscribers, PostgreSQL with EF Core migrations, 45 tests passing, review fixes applied

title: Parity harness: dual-stack scenario diff + flame golden-master
description:
Prove behavioral parity between the .NET stack and the Go rewrite by running BOTH and diffing. The old stack stays live during the whole rewrite - this bead exploits that unusual luxury. A green run here is a HARD precondition for cutover (blocks winzy.ai-rdc7.10). Ground truth: both epic comments; the danger-zone list below is derived from them.

PART 1 - SCENARIO DIFF (can start once 3.x lands, grows with each module):
Scripted API scenario suite executed identically against the old compose stack (:5050) and the Go stack, with identically seeded data. Diff JSON responses field by field, normalizing ONLY documented volatile fields (generated UUIDs via an id-mapping table built during seeding, timestamps within tolerance). Report field-level differences; empty report = pass.
Scenario coverage (minimum): register/login/refresh/logout incl. web-cookie vs native-body flows; single-use refresh rotation; habit CRUD + archive; completions incl. backfill (dates before habit creation!), minimum-kind, upsert-same-day, delete-by-date; GET /stats across timezones (X-Timezone variations, DST boundary, date-line tz); public flame page + flame.svg (UTC contract - MUST differ from owner-tz stats when the dates straddle midnight); promises incl. lazy Kept/EndedBelow resolution and PrivateNote exclusion; friends request/accept/decline/remove; visibility changes incl. feed narrowing/restore; witness links incl. revoked-token 404 and rotate; all 5 challenge milestone types incl. same-date replay dedupe; notifications list/read/unread-count + idempotent fan-out; activity feed pagination cursor round-trip; ERROR SHAPES: 409 {"error"} and 422 {"errors"} for representative failures; 401/404 behaviors.
KNOWN INTENTIONAL DIFFS (normalize, do not fail on): activity feed name join (winzy.ai-rdc7.7) response should be shape-identical - verify it actually is; anything else discovered goes in a reviewed allowlist with justification, approved by the PM.

PART 2 - FLAME GOLDEN-MASTER (runs on the winzy.ai-rdc7.9 rehearsal dataset):
For the migrated REAL dataset: compute every user's every habit's flame/consistency value (owner-tz stats AND public UTC values) through the old API and the new API. ZERO mismatches allowed. Real habit history is the ultimate spec - it contains every weird pattern the owner and friends actually produced (backfills, minimums, archived habits, weekly habits).
Pay special attention to rounding (.NET banker's rounding vs Go - see winzy.ai-rdc7.3.2) - if a mismatch appears, it is almost certainly this.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- One-command run producing a pass/fail parity report (scenario diff + golden-master)
- All scenario areas above covered; danger zones (timezone split, backfill, minimums, weekly ISO weeks, rounding) have dedicated cases
- Golden-master: zero mismatches on the rehearsal dataset
- Any allowlisted difference documented + PM-approved
- Wired as the documented cutover precondition

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch.)
close_reason: Part 1: scenario diff FINAL 19/19 (5 approved allowlist entries, all frontend-inert). Part 2: flame golden-master over the real migrated dataset — 6 users, 10 habits, 26 comparisons, ZERO mismatches, 0 errors, 4 legitimate skips (private habits on public surface). Cutover precondition SATISFIED and documented. Workers: SnowyMaple (both parts).

A green run's log must make it obvious what was actually covered (scenario names + request counts), so "pass" is auditable, not just a green exit code. ADD SCENARIO: /auth/export equivalence - full export document diffed old vs new (normalize section ordering and volatile fields; this proves the export registry reproduces the five-service fan-out).

comments.author: gabko14
comments.text: GROUND-TRUTH CORRECTION (PM, 2026-07-09, from the rdc7.2 review): validation errors in the real C# system are HTTP 400 with an {"errors": {field:[msgs]}} body, NOT 422 as this bead's description and the epic's frontend inventory stated. ASP.NET's Results.ValidationProblem defaults to 400; the C# tests assert 400; the frontend's mapHttpError treats any body containing "errors" as a validation error regardless of status. The Go implementation (rdc7.2) matches the real 400 behavior. Parity scenarios must expect 400+errors-body for validation failures; a 422 expectation would be a false failure.

title: Habits schema, CRUD, and completions
description:
Port habit and completion storage + endpoints from habit-service. Ground truth: epic winzy.ai-rdc7 comment 1 (ENDPOINTS/HABITS + DATA MODEL habit_db); C# source services/habit-service/src is the spec.

DATA MODEL (exact): Habit(UserId idx, Name, Icon?, Color?, Frequency Daily/Weekly/Custom stored as string, CustomDays? jsonb list of weekdays, MinimumDescription?, ArchivedAt? = soft-archive marker). Completion(HabitId + LocalDate UNIQUE composite, UserId idx, CompletedAt utc, LocalDate date, CompletionKind None/Full/Minimum stored as string default Full, Note?). Honest Minimums = MinimumDescription on the habit + CompletionKind on completions; a Minimum completion is REJECTED if the habit has no MinimumDescription.

ENDPOINTS: POST /habits; GET /habits; GET/PUT/DELETE /habits/{id}; POST /habits/{id}/complete; DELETE + PUT /habits/{id}/completions/{date}; GET /habits/completions?date=. All owner-scoped via the JWT middleware userId (404, not 403, for other users' habits - match C# behavior; verify in source).

CRITICAL SEMANTICS:
- Completion.LocalDate is computed at WRITE time from the client's IANA timezone (X-Timezone header) - this is what makes flame math timezone-correct later. Invalid IANA id -> 400.
- DELETE /habits/{id} = soft archive: set ArchivedAt, cancel any active promise (emit a hook or call the promise package once 3.3 lands - leave a clearly-marked integration point), emit HabitArchived.
- Unique (HabitId, LocalDate) means completing twice the same local day is an update/conflict, not a duplicate - match the C# response behavior exactly.
- Emit hooks via the registry from winzy.ai-rdc7.2: HabitCreated(UserId,HabitId,Name), HabitCompleted(UserId,HabitId,Date,Consistency,Timezone,DisplayName,HabitName,CompletionKind), HabitArchived. NOTE: HabitCompleted carries the freshly computed consistency - until 3.2 lands, emit with consistency=0 and a TODO(3.2) marker, or coordinate if 3.2 is already done.
- Error shapes: 409 {"error"}, 422 {"errors": {field:[msgs]}}.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

title: Flame/consistency engine (exact port + golden tests)
description:
Port services/habit-service/src/Services/ConsistencyCalculator.cs to Go EXACTLY. This is the single highest-risk file in the rewrite: it is the product (The Flame), the math is subtle, and users will notice a 1-point difference. The C# unit tests are the primary spec; port every one, then add more.

ALGORITHM (verified against source 2026-07-09, incl. the a35bd06 backfill fix):
- Constants: WindowDays=60, FullWeight=1.0, MinimumWeight=0.5.
- Calculate(habit, completions map[LocalDate]Kind, today, habitCreatedLocalDate?):
  1. windowStart = today - 59 (60-day window inclusive of today).
  2. BACKFILL RULE: firstCompletionInWindow = min completion date within [windowStart, today], else habitCreatedDate; scoreStart = min(firstCompletionInWindow, habitCreatedDate); effectiveStart = max(windowStart, scoreStart); if effectiveStart > today return 0. (This lets backfilled completions inside the window count even when earlier than habit creation.)
  3. Daily/Custom frequency: iterate effectiveStart..today; a day is applicable if Daily, or if Custom and CustomDays contains its weekday; sum weight of completions on applicable days; result = Round(sum/applicableDays*100, 1).
  4. Weekly frequency: ISO weeks starting Monday; each week overlapping the range counts once; the week's weight = best completion kind in it (Full short-circuits); result = Round(sum/totalWeeks*100, 1).
- CalculateForDateRange: same weighting over an arbitrary range clamped to the habit creation date (consumed by challenges in winzy.ai-rdc7.5).
- Flame levels with ASYMMETRIC HYSTERESIS: rising thresholds None <10= Ember <30= Steady <55= Strong <80= Blazing; falling thresholds (only when new < previous) 5/20/40/65, capped at previous level. Most call sites pass no previous level (nothing is persisted), so hysteresis is largely dormant - port it anyway for parity.
- Rounding: match .NET Math.Round(x, 1) semantics (banker's rounding - MidpointRounding.ToEven is the .NET default; VERIFY in source and replicate deliberately in Go, which rounds half-away-from-zero by default. This is exactly the kind of bug the parity harness exists to catch).

TIMEZONE CONTRACT (critical, from ground truth):
- Owner surfaces (GET /habits/{id}/stats, promise evaluation) resolve "today" in the owner's X-Timezone (required header, IANA, invalid -> 400).
- Share surfaces (public flame page, flame.svg - built in 3.3) hardcode UTC. Documented must-match contract; do not "fix" this asymmetry.
- No caching anywhere - computed fresh per request from all completions. Fine at this scale; do not add caching.

DELIVERABLES: internal/habits flame package + GET /habits/{id}/stats endpoint; wire real consistency into the HabitCompleted hook payload (replacing the 3.1 placeholder).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Every C# ConsistencyCalculator test ported and passing
- Additional table-driven tests: backfill before creation date, DST transitions, date-line timezones, weekly best-kind selection, minimum-weight mixes, rounding midpoints (x.x5 cases), empty window, single-day-old habit
- .NET rounding semantics replicated and covered by an explicit test
- GET /stats parity: same JSON shape as C#, X-Timezone required
- HabitCompleted hook carries real consistency

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch.)
close_reason: Flame/consistency engine ported exactly (worker-rdc7-32/Opus), PM line-read against the C# source: banker's rounding replicated bit-for-bit, per-surface timezone contract preserved, every C# test ported with exact values + Kiritimati date-line edge root-caused and tested, 16/16 live cross-stack golden pairs with zero mismatches. See PM REVIEW comment.

comments.text:

1. CONTEXT: read CLAUDE.md ("PM / Worker Split" binds you); br show winzy.ai-rdc7.3.2 + comments; br show winzy.ai-rdc7 + ALL its comments (the FLAME section of ground-truth comment 1 is your map; note both GROUND-TRUTH CORRECTIONS); PM REVIEW comments on rdc7.1/rdc7.2/rdc7.3.1 (binding conventions; the consistency=0 TODO(3.2) markers you will replace live in internal/habits).
2. READ THE SPEC LINE BY LINE: services/habit-service/src/Services/ConsistencyCalculator.cs and its COMPLETE test suite; git show a35bd06 (the backfill fix); the stats endpoint handler (X-Timezone handling + response DTO); where the C# computes the consistency it embeds in the habit.completed event (which timezone it uses at publish time - replicate exactly); frontend/src/api/habits.ts stats types (the other half of the contract).
3. ROUNDING: verify the C# rounding call (Math.Round(x,1) defaults to banker's/ToEven). Implement an explicit Go helper replicating the verified semantics, with unit tests on midpoint cases (x.x5 values on both even and odd tenths). Do NOT use a float printf shortcut.
4. Implement the calculator as pure functions in the habits package (consistency.go or flame.go): Calculate per the bead's algorithm spec (window, backfill scoreStart rule, Daily/Custom applicable days, Weekly ISO Monday weeks best-kind), CalculateForDateRange (clamped to creation; challenges consume it in rdc7.5), GetFlameLevel with the asymmetric hysteresis thresholds.
5. PORT EVERY C# CALCULATOR TEST, preserving each test's inputs/expected values exactly. Then add the AC's table-driven cases: backfill-before-creation, DST transitions, date-line timezones, weekly best-kind mixes, minimum-weight mixes, rounding midpoints, empty window, day-old habit.
6. GET /habits/{id}/stats: X-Timezone required (missing/invalid -> match C# status + body exactly), response JSON field-identical to the C# DTO.
7. Replace both consistency=0 placeholders (HTTP response field + HabitCompleted event payload) with real values, using the same timezone source the C# uses in each spot.
8. LIVE CROSS-STACK GOLDEN CHECK (the decisive verification): the old stack is running (docker compose, gateway :5050; a parity worker is also using it - create your own throwaway users only, prefix flame-golden-*). Via public APIs only, seed IDENTICAL habit+completion patterns on the old stack and your Go stack (same local dates via backfill, same kinds, same frequencies incl. a weekly and a custom-days habit), then fetch stats from both under at least 3 X-Timezone values and diff the numbers. ZERO numeric mismatches allowed. Paste the side-by-side values in your report.
9. VERIFY (paste outputs): gofmt -l empty; go vet -tags=integration ./...; go build; go test -race ./...; TEST_DATABASE_URL=... go test -tags=integration -race -p 1 ./...; the step-8 golden diff.
10. STOP. No commit, no push, no bead changes. Report: files, decisions (esp. rounding + timezone-per-surface), full verification incl. golden side-by-sides, deviations, open questions. If ANY C# test value disagrees with your implementation, do not "fix" the expected value - stop and report the discrepancy.

comments.author: gabko14
comments.text:
PM REVIEW (2026-07-09, worker: worker-rdc7-32 on Opus): APPROVED after one held round. Review depth: consistency.go read line-by-line against ConsistencyCalculator.cs - every branch, boundary, and constant verified identical (backfill scoreStart rule, weekly ISO overlap, asymmetric hysteresis incl. the falling-cap, range calculator deliberately lacking the backfill rule, IsApplicableDay semantics). roundNET verified as a faithful reimplementation of .NET Math.Round(double,int) ToEven (scale/RoundToEven/unscale + doubleRoundLimit guard), empirically confirmed by the worker against live .NET 10. All modified-file diffs read. Fresh -count=1 unit + integration gates green. Cross-stack golden: 16/16 pairs zero mismatches with per-tz divergence present on both stacks.

The parity worker's "both views 0" observation was an artifact of the pre-rebuild stale images; live reproduction on current source shows the expected divergence identically on both stacks. title: Flame Promises + public flame endpoints
description:
Port Flame Promises (habit-service Endpoints/PromiseEndpoints.cs) and the public flame surfaces. Ground truth: epic comment 1 (data model habit_db Promise, ENDPOINTS/HABITS public rows, FLAME timezone contract).

PROMISES: Promise(UserId, HabitId FK cascade, TargetConsistency double, EndDate date, PrivateNote?, Status Active/Kept/EndedBelow/Cancelled string, IsPublicOnFlame bool default false, ResolvedAt?; UNIQUE (UserId,HabitId) WHERE status='Active'). Endpoints: POST/GET/DELETE/PATCH /habits/{habitId}/promise + PATCH .../promise/visibility. Semantics: LAZY auto-resolution on owner GET - when EndDate has passed, resolve to Kept or EndedBelow by comparing current consistency (owner timezone) against TargetConsistency; archive of the habit cancels an active promise (wire the integration point left in 3.1). Public projection NEVER includes PrivateNote.

PUBLIC FLAME SURFACES (no auth - on the gateway public allowlist): GET /habits/public/{username} and GET /habits/public/{username}/flame.svg. Username resolves via the auth module's exported resolve function (was /auth/internal/resolve). Flame values computed with UTC as "today" (the share-surface contract from 3.2 - do NOT use any viewer timezone). flame.svg renders the SVG badge - port the C# generation faithfully (compare rendered output for the same inputs). Only habits visible per social settings appear - the visibility filter arrives with winzy.ai-rdc7.4; until then, match the C# query behavior and leave a marked integration point.

CONSIDERATIONS
- Public page powers winzy.ai/@username zero-friction sharing - a core product surface; SPA route /@username is served by the frontend, which then calls these endpoints.
- Constant-time username lookup failure is NOT required here (that is witness links); match C# behavior.
- IsPublicOnFlame gates whether the promise appears on the public page.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Promise CRUD parity incl. unique-active constraint (creating a second active promise on a habit: match C# conflict behavior)
- Lazy resolution proven by tests (Kept and EndedBelow paths, owner-timezone evaluation)
- PrivateNote absent from every public/shared projection (test)
- Public page + flame.svg byte/shape-compatible with C# for identical inputs; UTC contract tested
- Archive cancels active promise (integration test with 3.1)
- Happy/edge/error coverage per CLAUDE.md

(EXECUTION STEPS added by the PM at dispatch.)

Two review rounds (PM line-read + GPT adversarial pass), all gates green.

STEP 4 — archive cancels active promise: implement at the marked INTEGRATION POINT in internal/habits/service.go (~line 197), inside the same transaction as the archive. Integration test proves it.

STEP 5 — public surfaces GET /habits/public/{username} and GET /habits/public/{username}/flame.svg (no auth): resolve username via auth Service.ResolveUsername (in-process call, was /auth/internal/resolve). Public + shared projections NEVER include PrivateNote (explicit test asserting absence from raw JSON). Only promises with IsPublicOnFlame appear publicly. Visibility filtering of habits arrives with rdc7.4 — match the current C# query behavior and leave a marked INTEGRATION POINT (winzy.ai-rdc7.4) comment.

Extend auth.Middleware (or the allowlist type) to support prefix entries so GET /habits/public/ works without auth; keep existing exact entries working; unit-test both. STEP 8 — tests per CLAUDE.md (happy/edge/error) using the internal/dbtest recipe (build tag integration, compose winzy-db, TEST_DATABASE_URL). Port every PromiseEndpointTests.cs case. GATES (run all, paste full output in report): gofmt -l ., go vet ./..., go build ./..., go test ./..., then docker compose up -d winzy-db && TEST_DATABASE_URL=postgres://winzy:winzy@localhost:5439/winzy?sslmode=disable go test -tags=integration -race -v -p 1 ./... (from backend/).

WORKER RULES: never commit, never push, never close beads, no br write commands. When done: STOP and report files changed, decisions taken, deviations from C#, full gate output, open questions.

comments.text:
PM REVIEW RECORD (2026-07-11): Sonnet worker implemented all 8 EXECUTION STEPS; PM line-read every non-test source file against PromiseEndpoints.cs / PublicEndpoints.cs / InternalEndpoints.cs and independently re-ran all gates (green, 357 integration subtests, -race -p 1, uncached). Verified independently: ServeMux ambiguity between GET /habits/{id}/stats|promise and GET /habits/public/{username} is real — worker's merged habitOrPublicGET dispatch is the correct fix.
