title: Challenge non-users via invite link
description:
From GH issue #125 (deleted 2026-07-16): "Allow creating challenges for non existing users. 'I challenge u to XXX, install app to participate'". Gabriel's growth-loop idea; specced 2026-07-16.

WHY THIS IS MORE THAN A LINK (research findings): challenges today target the RECIPIENT'S habit — CreateChallengeScreen picks one of the friend's shared habits, and the backend enforces recipient is an existing accepted friend (challenges/service.go:77-85). A non-user has no account, no friendship, and no habits. So an invite must carry a PROPOSED HABIT plus the challenge terms, and claiming must materialize everything.

PRODUCT SPEC (v1):
- Creator builds an invite: proposed habit (name, icon, frequency incl. customDays), milestone (consistencyTarget + targetValue + periodDays), reward description ("loser buys coffee" energy — shared experiences). Gets a share link winzy.ai/ci/{token}.
- Anyone opening the link (no auth) sees a landing page: "<Creator> challenges you: <habit>, <target> — reward: <reward>. Join Winzy to accept."
- Claiming (after signup/login) atomically creates: accepted friendship, the invitee's habit from the proposal, and the Active challenge; creator gets a push. One-time use; 30-day expiry; revocable.
- Token mechanics mirror witness links exactly (crypto/rand base64url, unique index, no timing oracle, never log tokens).

SAFETY/ABUSE: landing exposes creator display name + proposal to anyone with the link — same exposure class as witness links, acceptable. Max 20 pending invites per creator caps spam potential.

STRUCTURE (4 children): .1 backend lifecycle (table/token/create/list/revoke/public view) -> .4 claim transaction (friendship+habit+challenge atomically — the hardest bead; gpt-5.6-sol review alongside grok + PM) -> then in parallel: .2 public landing page /ci/{token} (needs .1 + .4) and .3 creator create-and-share flow (needs .1 only).

CROSS-LINK: if profile images (winzy.ai-mfns) lands first, the landing page should show the creator's avatar — a face makes "join me" dramatically more compelling. Noted in .2.

title: Challenge invites backend: lifecycle (table, token, create/list/revoke, public view)
description:
Backend child of the Challenge-invite epic — invite LIFECYCLE only. The claim transaction is a separate bead (winzy.ai-jc38.4) that depends on this one.

SPEC:
- Migration (next number): challenge_invites — id uuid PK, created_at, updated_at, creator_id uuid NOT NULL, token varchar(64) UNIQUE NOT NULL, habit_name varchar(256) NOT NULL, habit_icon varchar(64), habit_frequency varchar(16) NOT NULL, habit_custom_days jsonb, milestone_type varchar(32) NOT NULL, target_value double precision NOT NULL, period_days int NOT NULL, reward_description varchar(512) NOT NULL, status varchar(16) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' (pending|claimed|revoked), claimed_by uuid, claimed_at timestamptz, expires_at timestamptz NOT NULL. Lives in the challenges module. Down migration too.
- Token generation: copy the witness-link pattern verbatim (crypto/rand 32 bytes -> base64.RawURLEncoding, 43 chars — social/witness_store.go:24-33). Same lookup discipline: length-bounds precheck (20-64) before DB, fetch by token WITHOUT status filter in SQL then branch in Go (avoids the revoked-vs-nonexistent timing oracle — see the doc comments in social/witness_service.go), and NEVER log raw tokens (log invite id/creator id only).
- POST /challenges/invites (authed): body {habitName, habitIcon?, frequency, customDays?, milestoneType, targetValue, periodDays, rewardDescription}. Validation reuses the existing rule sets — challenge terms per challenges/validation.go bounds (v1 restricts milestoneType to consistencyTarget, matching what the wizard sends), habit proposal per habits rules (name 1-256 runes, frequency enum lowercase, customDays int ordinals 0-6). Enforce max 20 PENDING invites per creator -> 409 {"error": ...}. expires_at = now + 30 days. Returns {id, token, url}.
- GET /challenges/invites (authed): creator's pending invites, newest first (feeds the manage/revoke UI).
- DELETE /challenges/invites/{id} (authed, creator only, 404 for others' invites): revoke (status=revoked), idempotent.
- GET /challenges/invites/{token} (PUBLIC — deliberate allowlist entry in cmd/api/main.go; verify the mux route shape does not collide with the authed GET list route): returns {creatorDisplayName, habitName, habitIcon, milestoneType, targetValue, periodDays, rewardDescription, status}. status included so the landing page can render claimed/expired/revoked states kindly. Creator name via the auth service interface (BatchProfiles-style), never a cross-module store read. Expired-but-still-pending rows report status "expired" in the response (computed), no background job needed.
- Cascade: extend the challenges UserDeleted handler — creator deleted -> their invites hard-deleted in the same transaction (proves the claim-side 404 behavior tested in jc38.4). Export: pending invites included in the challenges export section.
- openapi.yaml + npm run generate:api-types.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Integration tests (ConnectParallel): happy (create -> list -> public view roundtrip; revoke -> public view shows revoked), edge (exactly-20-pending boundary; 21st -> 409; revoked/claimed don't count toward the cap; expiry boundary computed status; token length precheck rejects garbage without DB round trip — assert via store call count or just behavior), error (invalid proposal fields matrix, bad token 404, revoke others' invite 404, unauthenticated on authed routes 401, public view needs no auth).
- Account-deletion test extended: creator's invites gone.
- Backend gates green.
notes:
EXECUTION STEPS
1. `br show winzy.ai-jc38.1` AND `br show winzy.ai-jc38` (epic = product spec, READ-ONLY — no br write commands ever). The claim transaction is NOT yours (separate bead jc38.4) — lifecycle only.
2. Reserve backend/internal/challenges/** and backend/migrations/0013_challenge_invites.*; openapi.yaml + generated schema.ts only while editing (release fast).
3. Migration number is 0013. Schema per the bead spec verbatim.
4. Token mechanics: COPY the witness-link pattern (social/witness_store.go:24-33 generation; witness_service.go lookup discipline — length precheck, no status filter in SQL, never log raw tokens). Do not invent a new scheme.
5. Endpoints per spec: POST /challenges/invites (validation reuses challenges/validation.go bounds + habits rules for the proposal; max 20 PENDING per creator -> 409; expires_at now+30d), GET /challenges/invites (creator's pending, newest first), DELETE /challenges/invites/{id} (revoke, idempotent, 404 for others'), GET /challenges/invites/{token} (PUBLIC — deliberate allowlist entry in cmd/api/main.go; verify no collision with the authed list route; computed "expired" status in the response).
6. Creator display name via the auth service interface already injected into challenges (see how existing code resolves names) — never a store reach-through.
7. Cascade: extend the challenges UserDeleted handler (invites hard-deleted, same tx). Export: pending invites in the challenges export section. Tests for both.
8. Test coverage per the bead's acceptance list (ConnectParallel, t.Parallel, -race). Package-scoped gates during dev; full tree stays PM-coordinated.
9. openapi + typegen at the end. Report on this thread + release. NOTE: main.go allowlist edit = one quick compilable pass, release immediately.
close_reason: Lifecycle shipped; witness-pattern token discipline verified incl. log redaction; challenges+contract -race green independently; committed

title: Challenge invite public landing page (/ci/{token})
description:
Frontend child of the Challenge-invite epic. DEPENDS ON the backend invites bead.

SPEC:
- New web path /ci/{token}, handled exactly like the existing /w/{token} witness route: parsed from window.location.pathname in RootNavigator (see RootNavigator.tsx:64-73, 180-188 for the pattern), rendered outside the tab/overlay system, no auth required.
- Landing screen (new ChallengeInviteScreen): fetches the public GET /challenges/invites/{token}; shows creator display name, proposed habit (icon + name), the terms ("<targetValue>% consistency over <periodDays> days"), and the reward, in the same warm visual language as PublicFlameScreen. Clear CTA: "Join Winzy & accept" -> routes into the existing sign-up flow (same click-through mechanism the witness/@username pages use, incl. the history.replaceState URL-clearing behavior).
- Token handoff: persist the token (in-memory + sessionStorage) across the signup/login flow; after auth completes, POST claim, then land the new user on their Today screen with the new habit + an acknowledgment of the accepted challenge (a simple success toast/banner v1 — no new ceremony screen).
- Already-logged-in user opening the link: show the landing with CTA "Accept challenge" -> claim directly.
- Non-pending states handled kindly: claimed -> "This challenge was already accepted"; expired/revoked -> "This invite is no longer active" (+ generic join CTA). Claim-time 409/4xx (raced, expired between view and claim) -> same kind messaging, never a raw error.
- SEO/OG: inject Open Graph tags like register-sw.ts does for flame pages ("<Creator> challenges you on Winzy").

ACCEPTANCE:
- Logged-out flow: open link -> sign up -> challenge auto-accepted -> Today shows the new habit; challenge visible in MyChallenges for both parties.
- Logged-in flow: open link -> accept -> same result without signup.
- Claimed/expired/revoked links show the kind states; abandoned signup does not claim anything.
- Component tests for state rendering + token persistence; Playwright E2E for the logged-out happy path. Frontend gates green.

title: Challenge invite claim transaction (friendship + habit + challenge, atomically)
description:
Child of the Challenge-invite epic — the HARDEST bead of the epic, split out from the lifecycle bead so it gets focused implementation and review. DEPENDS ON the invites lifecycle bead (table/token/create/view exist).

SPEC — POST /challenges/invites/{token}/claim (AUTHED; the invitee signed up or logged in first):
In ONE Postgres transaction:
1. SELECT the invite FOR UPDATE by token; re-check status=pending AND not expired. Concurrent double-claim: exactly one wins, the loser gets 409. Expired/revoked/claimed -> 409 with a distinct message ("This invite is no longer active" / "already accepted") so the landing page can message kindly.
2. Reject self-claim by the creator -> 400 "You cannot accept your own challenge".
3. Ensure an ACCEPTED friendship creator<->claimer: if none exists, create it; if a PENDING request exists in either direction, upgrade it to accepted (do not error). Needs a tx-aware social-service method (e.g. EnsureFriendship) exposed through the cross-module interface — never reach into social's store.
4. Create the habit for the claimer from the invite's proposal fields (name/icon/frequency/customDays) via a tx-aware habits-service method.
5. Create the Active challenge (creator_id=creator, recipient_id=claimer, habit_id=new habit, milestone/target/period/reward from the invite).
6. Mark the invite claimed (claimed_by, claimed_at, status).

TRANSACTION THREADING is the crux: study how auth.Service.DeleteAccount threads its tx to other modules via db.WithQuerier / db.QuerierFrom (auth/service.go:335-358, events/registry.go:22-34) BEFORE designing. The social/habits service methods used here must resolve their querier from ctx so all six steps write through the caller's tx. If existing methods close over the pool, add tx-aware variants — do not fork parallel logic.

AFTER COMMIT: emit ChallengeCreated (best-effort, post-commit — notification-class event per the habits/service.go:399-409 pattern) so the creator gets the existing push. Also emit FriendRequestAccepted? NO — decide deliberately: suppress the friend-request push here (the challenge push already tells the story; two pushes for one tap is noise). Document the choice in code.

EDGE SEMANTICS:
- Claimer already friends with creator: skip step 3, proceed.
- Claimer already has an Active challenge from this creator on a same-named habit: allowed (the unique index is per habit_id and this is a NEW habit row).
- Creator deleted their account between create and claim: invite rows are cascade-deleted with the creator, so token lookup 404s naturally — add a test proving it.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Integration tests (ConnectParallel): happy path materializes all three artifacts atomically and marks claimed; double-claim race test (two goroutines, one 200 one 409); pending-friend-request upgrade; already-friends path; self-claim 400; expired/revoked/claimed 409s; unauthenticated 401.
- ROLLBACK test: force failure at step 4 or 5 (e.g. invalid proposal snuck past, or injected store error) -> NOTHING persisted, invite still pending.
- Backend gates green.

REVIEW POLICY (PM): this bead is top-5% complexity — gpt-5.6-sol HIGH review alongside grok and the PM's own line-read before it merges.
notes:
EXECUTION STEPS
1. `br show winzy.ai-jc38.4` + epic (READ-ONLY, no br writes). Your lifecycle work is COMMITTED (bf4daaa) — build on the tree as-is.
2. BEFORE writing any code: study the tx-threading pattern end-to-end — auth.Service.DeleteAccount (auth/service.go:335-358), db.WithQuerier/QuerierFrom (internal/db), events/registry.go:22-34 contract. Your claim must thread ONE pgx tx through social + habits service calls the same way. If existing social/habits methods close over their pool, add tx-aware variants resolving via db.QuerierFrom — never fork business logic.
3. Reserve backend/internal/challenges/**; you will also need small additive methods in internal/social (EnsureFriendship-style) and internal/habits (tx-aware create path) — reserve those specific FILES only while editing them (no other backend worker is active, but keep the discipline).
4. Implement POST /challenges/invites/{token}/claim exactly per the bead's 6-step transaction, edge semantics, and post-commit ChallengeCreated emit (suppress the friend-request push — document the decision in code per spec).
5. Tests per the bead's acceptance list, ESPECIALLY: the double-claim race (two goroutines, exactly one 200), the rollback test (forced failure mid-tx leaves NOTHING — no orphan friendship), and creator-deleted-then-claim 404.
6. Package gates (-race) on challenges + social + habits; full tree stays PM-coordinated. openapi + typegen at the end.
7. Report on this thread with the tx-design explanation FIRST (which methods became tx-aware and why) — this bead gets a triple review (grok + gpt-5.6-sol + PM), so the report's design section matters.
close_reason: Claim transaction shipped after full triple review: 5 round-1 + 2 round-2 findings (tx poisoning, misdirected notification, revoke race, lock ordering, frontend case) all fixed and sol-verified to zero; six packages -race green independently; committed

title: Todos manage overlay (edit, delete, reorder, completed history)
description:
Child 3 of the Todo list epic (split from the Today-section bead so the two surfaces can be built/reviewed separately). DEPENDS ON the Today-section bead (api client, query keys, mutations, and section entry point exist).

SPEC:
- New overlay id "todos" registered in useOverlayRouter/RootNavigator (inherits browser-back behavior automatically once the back-button bead lands — integration is at router level). Entry: a quiet "manage" affordance on the Today to-dos section header.
- Open todos list: tap a row to edit via a small sheet (title + due date; date input: plain input type=date on web is acceptable, verify the native-side approach via Context7 — do not hand-roll a calendar). dueDate clearable (-> null).

On 409 (list changed on another device): auto-refetch, reapply the user's intended order to the fresh set where ids still exist, retry ONCE; if it fails again, toast + refreshed list. Never lose the user's edit silently.

title: Habit reminder notifications (the dead habit_reminders toggle)
description:
Filed 2026-07-16 during the idea-backlog review (discovered by architecture research, related to GH issue #134): notification_settings.habit_reminders exists, DEFAULTS TO TRUE, and is presumably toggleable in the UI — but NOTHING reads it. No scheduler, cron, or ticker exists anywhere in the backend; no reminder push has ever been sent. A settings toggle that does nothing is lying to users.

WHY BUILD (not just hide the toggle): the product philosophy in CLAUDE.md literally names the reminder voice — "Encourage, never punish — 'Ready to log today?' not 'You haven't logged today!'". A gentle daily reminder is core to a habit tracker's retention loop and was clearly always intended.

PRODUCT SPEC (v1):
- One optional reminder push per day at a user-chosen local time (default 19:00), web push only (matches current delivery capability).
- SENT ONLY IF USEFUL: skip entirely when the user has no habits due today, or has already completed all due habits (never nag someone who is done — completing your habits must never earn you a notification).
- Copy: encouraging, varied slightly, never guilt ("Ready to log today?" / "Your flame's waiting 🔥"). Backend sends data; but push payload carries the display strings today (check how existing pushes build payloads and match).
- Honors the existing habit_reminders toggle; adds a reminder time preference.
- TIMEZONE REALITY: the backend stores NO user timezone (tz is per-request headers by design). Scheduling server-side requires persisting one — the settings save will carry the device's IANA timezone. This is a deliberate, minimal exception to the per-surface-timezone rule: stored tz is used ONLY for reminder scheduling, never for completion/stats math. Document this in code.

NON-GOALS (v1): per-habit reminders, multiple reminders per day, smart timing, native push.

Children: backend scheduler first, then settings UI.

comments.author: gabko14
comments.text: Gabriel confirmed 2026-07-16: build reminders as specced, priority stays P2 (not just hiding the toggle).

title: Reminder scheduler + settings backend
description:
Backend child of the Habit-reminders epic.

SPEC:
- Migration (next number): notification_settings ADD reminder_time time NOT NULL DEFAULT '19:00', reminder_timezone varchar(64) NULL (IANA name; NULL = we have never learned this user's tz -> send nothing, never guess).
- Settings endpoints: extend the existing notification-settings GET/PUT (locate them in internal/notifications — research did not enumerate them) to carry reminderTime ("HH:MM") and reminderTimezone. Validate the tz against Go's tzdata (time.LoadLocation), the time as HH:MM.
- SCHEDULER: an in-process ticker in the notifications module (single Railway instance — no distributed-lock ceremony, but write it behind a small interface so a future multi-instance world can swap in an advisory-lock guard; add a code comment). Every minute: SELECT users where habit_reminders = true AND reminder_timezone IS NOT NULL AND their local wall-clock time == reminder_time (compute per distinct tz in Go — the distinct-timezone count is tiny at this scale, do not build a per-user loop of LoadLocation calls).
- For each candidate, the USEFULNESS check: user has >=1 habit due today (frequency/customDays vs their local weekday) that is NOT yet completed today. Reuse habits-module logic through the existing cross-module interface pattern (habits service method, e.g. a new HasUncompletedDueHabits(ctx, userID, localDate) — small, tx-free read). Skip silently otherwise.
- Delivery through the EXISTING notification pipeline (create notification row + web push), with idempotency_key = "reminder:{userID}:{localDate}" so restarts/ticker overlaps can never double-send (the unique index on idempotency_key is the guarantee, not the ticker).
- DST correctness: comparing wall-clock in the user's location handles DST inherently; the idempotency key handles the one pathological repeat (fall-back replays an hour); spring-forward can skip a nonexistent local time — mitigate by matching >= reminder_time within the last 15 minutes and relying on the idempotency key, not exact ==. Spell out the chosen algorithm in a doc comment.
- Ticker lifecycle: started from main.go alongside service wiring; stops cleanly on shutdown (context cancellation).

ACCEPTANCE:
- Integration tests (ConnectParallel): the scheduler's core function is testable with an injected "now" + fixed tz fixtures — happy (due+incomplete user gets exactly one notification row + push scheduled), edge (all-complete user skipped, no-due-habits user skipped, NULL tz skipped, toggle-off skipped, double-tick same minute -> one send via idempotency, DST fall-back replay -> one send), error (invalid tz/time rejected at the settings endpoint).
- Settings roundtrip test (PUT then GET reflects reminderTime + tz).
- No reminder logic leaks into completion/stats timezone handling (per-surface tz contracts untouched).
- Backend gates green.
notes:
EXECUTION STEPS
1. `br show winzy.ai-wyw6.1` AND `br show winzy.ai-wyw6` (epic = product spec; READ-ONLY, no br writes).
2. Reserve backend/internal/notifications/** and backend/migrations/0012_reminder_settings.*; openapi/schema only while editing.
3. Migration number is 0012 (0010/0011 belong to concurrent workers; gaps in your tree are fine).
4. SEQUENCING CONSTRAINT — internal/habits is another worker's territory RIGHT NOW: build everything against a small consumer-side interface (e.g. dueChecker with HasUncompletedDueHabits(ctx, userID, localDate)) defined in notifications and faked in tests. Do NOT touch internal/habits or main.go wiring for it until the PM posts GO-HABITS on this thread — then add the real habits-service method + wiring as your final step.
5. Implement per spec: reminder_time/reminder_timezone columns (NULL tz = never guess, send nothing), settings GET/PUT extension (locate the existing notification-settings endpoints first and follow their conventions), minute ticker behind an interface (single-instance comment), per-distinct-timezone wall-clock matching with the >=15-min catch-up window, usefulness check via the interface, delivery through the EXISTING notification pipeline with idempotency_key reminder:{userID}:{localDate}, clean shutdown via context.
6. Tests per the bead's acceptance list (injected now + fixed tz fixtures; DST edges; double-tick idempotency; settings roundtrip). Package-scoped during dev; full gates at end.
7. openapi + typegen at the end (reserve briefly).
8. Report on this thread, release reservations.
close_reason: Scheduler + settings shipped; dueness logic verified in lockstep with frontend due-today filter; notifications+habits -race green independently; committed

title: Reminder settings UI (time picker, timezone capture)
description:
Frontend child of the Habit-reminders epic. DEPENDS ON the scheduler/settings backend bead.

SPEC:
- The notifications area of SettingsScreen: under the existing habit-reminders toggle, when ON, show a time picker for the reminder time (default 19:00). Plain input type=time is acceptable on web; verify the native-side approach via Context7 before picking a component.
- Every settings save sends reminderTime + the device's IANA timezone (Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone). Also send the timezone opportunistically on app start IF the user has reminders on and the stored tz differs (travel case) — piggyback on an existing settings fetch/save path, do not invent a new endpoint.
- Copy is encouraging and expectation-setting: "A gentle nudge if you haven't logged yet — never when you're already done."
- If push permission is not granted, the time picker area explains reminders need notifications enabled and offers the existing subscribe flow (reuse usePushNotifications).

ACCEPTANCE:
- Set a time -> persisted (GET reflects it); toggle off hides the picker; timezone lands server-side; permission-less state guides to subscribe instead of failing silently.
- Component tests for the conditional rendering (toggle on/off, permission states) and the save payload; frontend gates green.

title: Push notification clicks don't navigate anywhere in the app
description:
Filed 2026-07-16 during the idea-backlog review (discovered by architecture research; verification bead winzy.ai-i1q6 will confirm live behavior). sw.js handles notificationclick (frontend/public/sw.js:116-135): it focuses an existing app window and posts a {type: "NOTIFICATION_CLICK", ...} message, or opens "/" — but NO code in the app listens for that message. Tapping a push therefore lands the user on whatever screen was last open, with no connection to what the notification said. Notifications that go nowhere feel broken and erode trust in the whole social layer.

SPEC:
- App-side listener (App.tsx or RootNavigator mount): navigator.serviceWorker message events with type NOTIFICATION_CLICK route by notification type using the existing tab/overlay APIs: friend_request -> Friends tab; friend activity / habit completed -> Feed tab; challenge_* -> challenges overlay (or Friends tab v1 if the overlay needs params the payload lacks — inspect what data the backend puts in the push payload, notifications/push.go + service.go, and extend the payload with the IDs needed for deep routing if trivial).
- COLD START: the message can fire before React mounts, or the sw opens a fresh window at "/" with no message at all (openWindow path cannot postMessage reliably). Make the sw encode the target in the opened URL (e.g. /?notif=friend_request) and have app boot read+consume it (replaceState clean-up, same pattern as the /@username handling). The warm-window postMessage path and the cold-start URL path must land on the same routing function.
- Buffer/replay: register the message listener before sw ready resolves or queue early messages — do not drop clicks that race app boot.
- Coordinate with the browser-back bead (winzy.ai-y4s1): routing must go through the same router-level APIs so history stays consistent (no direct state pokes).

ACCEPTANCE:
- Warm window: tap push -> app focuses AND lands on the right surface. Cold start: tap push with app closed -> opens and lands on the right surface. Both verified manually on staging (document in comment).
- Unknown/legacy notification types fall back to the Feed tab, never crash.
- Unit tests for the type->destination mapping + URL-param consumption; frontend gates green.
notes:
EXECUTION STEPS
1. `br show winzy.ai-penv` (READ-ONLY, no br writes) — spec + your OWN live findings from i1q6 (recorded in the bead comment: payloads already carry data.url /friends|/challenges).
2. Reserve frontend/public/sw.js, frontend/src/pwa/register-sw.ts (if touched), the new listener/routing module you add, and frontend/App.tsx ONLY while editing (touch-and-release hotspot). RootNavigator: same touch-and-release rule if you need a hunk; the router-level APIs from the back-button work (useOverlayRouter/selectTab paths, commit 93e98ad) are what you route through — no direct state pokes.
3. Implement per spec: (a) warm path — serviceWorker message listener registered at app boot, buffering events that arrive pre-mount, routing NOTIFICATION_CLICK by data.url (/friends -> Friends tab, /challenges -> challenges overlay or Friends tab if params are missing — inspect what the payload actually carries and pick honestly); (b) cold path — sw openWindow encodes the target in the URL (?notif= or the data.url path itself), app boot consumes + replaceState-cleans it (same pattern as /@username handling); both paths land in ONE routing function.
4. Unknown/absent targets -> Feed tab fallback, never crash.
5. Unit tests: routing map, URL-param consumption + cleanup, pre-mount buffering. Full frontend gates EXCEPT expect sibling WIP noise (d56m.3, rs2n in flight) — scoped results + report noise, don't fix.
6. Manual staging verification of warm+cold paths comes AFTER commit (you'll get a follow-up GO) — don't block the report on it.
7. CONTEXT RULE: you're at ~57% — if you approach 75% without a compaction, stop and write a handoff report. Output hygiene throughout.
8. Report + release.
close_reason: Shipped; PM line-read (resolver normalization, buffer shape, router-level integration, file ownership verified clean) + scoped suites green; manual staging warm/cold check follows post-push; committed

comments.author: gabko14
comments.text: Live confirmation (i1q6 verification 2026-07-17): sw.js notificationclick fires focus+postMessage(NOTIFICATION_CLICK, {url}) — and the push payloads ALREADY carry data.url (/friends, /challenges), so the app-side listener can route on the url field directly; cold-start openWindow('/') path confirmed as the gap needing the URL-param mechanism.

title: Batch habit stats endpoint — remove Today's N+1 request pattern
description:
Filed 2026-07-16 during the idea-backlog review. TodayScreen fires one GET /habits/{id}/stats per due habit in parallel on every load (useTodayHabits.ts:81-83). With N habits that is N+1 requests per visit — the real source of the sluggishness behind GH issue #129 alongside the missing cache. TanStack (winzy.ai-d56m) fixes refetch waste; this fixes the request fan-out.

SPEC:
- GET /habits/stats (authed; X-Timezone header REQUIRED, 400 without it — the exact contract of the per-habit stats endpoint): returns [{habitId, ...same per-habit stats shape...}] for ALL active habits in one response. Reuse the existing per-habit computation (habits/service.go HabitStats) — batch the completions fetch (one query for all habits, group in Go) rather than looping N DB round-trips; the flame/consistency math stays COMPUTED FRESH per request (no caching — deliberate, see CLAUDE.md).
- Keep the per-habit endpoint (HabitDetailScreen legitimately wants one habit's stats); the batch endpoint reuses the same code path so the two can never drift.
- openapi.yaml + generate:api-types.
- Frontend adoption: useTodayHabits (or its TanStack successor ['habits','stats',tz]) switches to the batch call — one query invalidated on any completion mutation. If d56m.1 has landed, adopt there; otherwise adapt the hook.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Integration tests (ConnectParallel): happy (multi-habit batch matches per-habit results exactly — property-style assertion comparing both endpoints), edge (zero habits -> empty array, archived excluded), error (missing X-Timezone 400, unauthenticated 401).
- Frontend: Today load performs exactly 2 data requests (habits list + batch stats) — assert in a component test with a mocked client; visible behavior unchanged.
- Both gate suites green.
notes:
EXECUTION STEPS (BACKEND ONLY — the frontend adoption clause in the spec is DEFERRED: the hooks are mid-migration by another worker; adoption happens with the week-strip bead. Your scope is the endpoint.)
1. `br show winzy.ai-sp8f` (READ-ONLY, no br writes). Description = spec; ignore the "Frontend adoption" paragraph for this dispatch.
2. Reserve backend/internal/habits/**; openapi.yaml + generated schema.ts only while editing (avatar worker shares them).
3. GET /habits/stats — all active habits, X-Timezone header REQUIRED (exact same 400-without-it contract as the per-habit endpoint). Reuse the existing HabitStats computation; batch the data fetches (one completions query for all habits, group in Go) — flame math stays computed fresh, NO caching (CLAUDE.md). The per-habit endpoint stays; both must share the same code path so they can never drift.
4. Route shape care: GET /habits/stats vs GET /habits/{id} — verify mux precedence (the {a}/{b} and {id} patterns in routes.go) so "stats" is never captured as an id.
5. openapi + typegen at the end. Integration tests per the bead's acceptance list, incl. the property-style batch-vs-per-habit equality assertion, in-package with -race.
6. Package-scoped gates; full tree stays PM-coordinated. Report + release.
close_reason: Batch endpoint shipped (backend); shared code path + property test verified, -race green independently; frontend adoption deferred to the week-strip/TanStack era per dispatch; committed

title: IDEA (not approved for implementation): AI-verified proof + photo feed
description:
STATUS: IDEA DOCUMENTATION ONLY — Gabriel has explicitly NOT decided to build this (2026-07-17: "I dont wanna pump too many features too fast, but I will think about this"). Agents: do NOT implement, spec, or create child beads from this. It becomes real work only when Gabriel says so in a live session. It is deferred-status so it never appears in br ready — keep it that way.

CONTEXT: born from a strategy discussion (2026-07-16/17) about why Winzy lacks breakout potential. Gabriel's frame: virality factor first, retention value second. Diagnosis of the current app: the flame is a solo artifact, quitting is consequence-free, the feed is boring event-log spam ("gabo completed a habit"), and habit trackers churn BY NATURE — even successful users graduate after weeks (Gabriel included). A tracker slightly better than the thousands of others is ngmi. Something standout is missing; the current bead board is bonus features Gabriel wants, not stuff people sign up for.

THE TWO IDEAS (Gabriel's, complementary):

1. RICHER FEED (cheap, no AI needed): feed entries built from meaningful moments instead of raw completions — "Gabo has a 7-day meditation streak" instead of "Gabo completed a habit". Better event selection + templating on data we already have.

2. AI-VERIFIED PROOF -> PROOF BECOMES CONTENT (the big one): completions require photo proof, verified by a vision LLM instead of a human referee (StickK's referee model without the social awkwardness — a genuine LLM-only unlock, not buildable before vision models). Proof is private by default; user can make a proof public, and a public proof IS a post -> the feed becomes real photos of real life (the Strava insight: the activity must natively produce a shareable artifact; proof photos are that artifact for generic habits). Verification also makes streaks CREDIBLE: a self-reported streak is cheap talk, a verified streak is a receipt — and credibility is what makes a streak flex-worthy/shareable ("streaks with receipts").

KEY DESIGN FINDINGS FROM THE DISCUSSION:
- THE DRAGON: mandatory friction (BeReal lesson — viral mechanic, retention poison: exploded 2022, engagement collapsed ~70%+ through 2023, sold to Voodoo 2024, survives as a niche). Forcing proof on every check-in makes the "trackers are a pain" churn problem WORSE.
- Proposed resolution (Claude's, not decided): proof as the PRICE OF THE SPOTLIGHT, per-habit opt-in — private habits stay 2-second frictionless; only proof-backed habits get verified flame / feed presence / public streak. Nobody forced; audience-seekers pay the friction happily (Strava dynamics).
- OPEN QUESTION Gabriel must answer first: per-habit opt-in tier ("Strava mode") vs proof-as-whole-product-identity ("BeReal mode"). Everything downstream follows from this choice.
- AI verification honesty: vision models can judge "this is a gym/an open book", NOT "you actually did it today". Bar to hit is "cheating is possible but feels like cheating": in-app camera only (no gallery), timestamp checks, perceptual-hash re-use detection. Proportionate to friends-scale trust, never courtroom-proof. Never combine with money stakes.
- Some small apps already do AI habit proof; nobody has won the space. Concept alone is not a moat — speed and taste would be.
- Related rejected directions from the same discussion (context): duels/head-to-head (StickK territory, punishment-flavored), scheduled social rewards (inverts spontaneity of friendship — Gabriel's critique), design sessions/mockups (Gabriel decides on realized products, not mocks).

RELATIONSHIP TO CURRENT BOARD: none. The existing beads proceed as written (Gabriel, 2026-07-17). If this idea is ever greenlit, it likely REPLACES parts of the feed and reshapes the social layer — re-plan at that point, do not graft onto current specs.

title: Kill the My-Habits screen: + creates directly, management folds into Today + HabitDetail
description:
Implementation of the 9igm decision (Gabriel, 2026-07-17): the My-Habits screen's functions are needed but its placement is the problem — tapping "+ Create Habit" detours through a list that duplicates Today. Resolution: kill the screen, relocate its three functions.

SPEC:
1. CREATE: the "+ Create Habit" FAB on TodayScreen opens CreateHabitScreen directly (new overlay id e.g. "createHabit" in create mode). No list detour.
2. EDIT + ARCHIVE move to HabitDetailScreen: an explicit "Edit habit" action (opens CreateHabitScreen pre-filled, the existing edit mode) plus Archive. NOTE: on web, HabitDetail's archive currently fires with NO confirmation (research 2026-07-16: HabitDetailScreen.tsx:606-618 — Alert.alert is native-only, web calls onArchive directly). Add a real confirm on web using the design-system Modal (coordinate with the space-key bead winzy.ai-k1o9 which touches that Modal).
3. REACHABILITY: TodayScreen gains a collapsed "Not today" group at the bottom of the list — active habits NOT due today (frequency-filtered out), muted rows, no completion toggles, no week strip, tap-through to HabitDetail, count in the header ("Not today (2)"), hidden entirely when every habit is due today. Zero noise for daily-habit users; every active habit stays one tap away.
4. DELETE HabitListScreen.tsx, its tests, its overlay registration ("habits" overlay id) and the onCreateHabit/onEdit plumbing that pointed at it. Grep for stragglers (RootNavigator if-chain, e2e selectors).

COORDINATION:
- TodayScreen is contended: week strip (winzy.ai-rs2n) and todos section (winzy.ai-30sf.2) also rebuild it — sequence with those, do not run concurrently.
- The reorder-mode bead (winzy.ai-6dj1) depends on THIS bead: reorder will live on Today (see its spec).
- If the TanStack habits migration (winzy.ai-d56m.1) has landed, the "Not today" group derives from the same ['habits'] query as the main list — one data source, client-side split by due-today.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Creating a habit is one tap shorter; edit and archive both reachable from HabitDetail on web AND native; archiving on web now confirms first.
- A Tuesday-only habit is reachable on a Wednesday via "Not today"; group hidden when empty.
- HabitListScreen fully gone (no dead code, no dead overlay id); e2e create-habit flow updated and green.
- Component tests: not-today grouping logic, create-FAB routing, web archive confirm. Frontend gates green.

title: PWA glanceability: app-icon badge + manifest shortcuts
description:
Follow-up from the widgets research (winzy.ai-c2pr, 2026-07-17) — the maximum home-screen presence a PWA can have; real widgets are impossible without native apps (see c2pr's report comment). Small, zero new infra. GABRIEL VETO WINDOW: filed per the research recommendation; kill it if it counts as feature-pumping.

SPEC:
- Badge = number of habits due today and not yet completed (the gentle kind of glanceability: it clears itself as you log; it is a count, never a guilt message).
- Frontend: feature-detected navigator.setAppBadge(count) / clearAppBadge() — called (a) on app foreground/background transitions and after any completion mutation, (b) from the service worker on push receipt (sw.js already handles push; compute-or-carry the count in the push payload — coordinate with notifications payload shape).
- iOS: works on installed PWAs 16.4+ only with notification permission granted — degrade silently without it. Note: EU-device behavior is historically flaky (DMA changes); feature-detect and never throw.
- Android: Chrome auto-badges on unread push already — setAppBadge is a no-op there; the win is iOS.
- manifest.json shortcuts array: "Log today" (opens Today) and "My flame" (opens Profile/flame) — Android long-press quick actions; iOS ignores them harmlessly. Wire the shortcut URLs through the same boot-time URL handling the notification-click bead (winzy.ai-penv) builds — coordinate; do not invent a second URL-param mechanism.
- Timezone correctness: "due today" is device-local — compute in the client, same due-today logic TodayScreen uses (share it, don't fork it).

ACCEPTANCE:
- Complete all habits -> badge clears; new day -> badge reflects due count (verify the recompute triggers: app open, visibility change, push receipt).
- No errors on browsers without the API (desktop Firefox etc.).
- Shortcuts appear on Android long-press and route correctly.
- Unit tests for the count computation + trigger points; frontend gates green.

title: Friend-activity push says 'A friend' instead of the friend's name
description:
Found during live push verification (winzy.ai-i1q6, 2026-07-17): the HabitCompleted friend-activity push renders title/body as "A friend completed X!" even when the completing user has a displayName — the events.HabitCompleted payload (or the notifications handler consuming it) never carries the actor's display name, so the copy can't personalize.

For a social product the name IS the content: "Gabo completed Meditation 🔥" lands completely differently from "A friend completed...".

SPEC:
- Carry the actor's display name into the friend-activity notification: either add DisplayName to events.HabitCompleted at emit (habits/service.go emit site — best if other consumers could use it) or resolve via the auth BatchProfiles interface in the notifications handler (one lookup per event, cross-module via service interface). Pick whichever matches the existing event-payload philosophy (check how FriendRequestAccepted resolves names, if it does).
- Push title/body use the name when present, fall back to "A friend" when absent (deleted accounts). Respect the existing payload shape (data.url etc.).
- PRIVACY CHECK before shipping: the recipient is a confirmed friend, so seeing the name is fine — but confirm the fan-out only ever targets accepted friends with FriendActivity on (verified live 2026-07-17: the toggle gates delivery correctly).
- In-app notification row (notifications table data jsonb) should carry the same name so the notifications list matches the push.

ACCEPTANCE: friend completes habit -> push and in-app row show their display name; user with no displayName -> falls back gracefully; existing tests updated + one new fan-out content test; backend gates green.
